It's a **macro view** of travel, treating people like grains of sand moving in bulk. ### Key Aggregate Units - **Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ):** The fundamental spatial unit (typically 1000–5000 population). - **Trip Production:** Home-end of a home-based trip OR origin of an NHB trip. - **Trip Attraction:** Non-home end of a home-based trip OR destination of an NHB trip. - **Person-Trip:** A trip made by one person, irrespective of vehicle occupancy. --- ## 1.2 Activity-Based Approach (ABA) ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Activity-Based Approach (ABA):** A disaggregate, **behavioral**, and **tour-based** transportation planning framework that treats **activities** (work, shopping, recreation, education, childcare) as the fundamental unit of analysis, recognizing that travel is a **derived demand** arising from the human need to participate in spatially-distributed activities across time and space. ABA uses concepts from **Hägerstrand's Time-Space Geography** and models how individuals/households schedule, chain, and execute activities subject to constraints: **Capability constraints** (biological), **Coupling constraints** (need to be with others), and **Authority constraints** (legal/accessibility). ### Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > If Trip-Based is "counting journeys like a toll booth," then **Activity-Based is like filming a person's entire day**. Instead of asking *"Where are you going?"*, you ask: *"What do you NEED to do today?"* — drop the kid at school, work 9–5, buy groceries, attend a yoga class, then go home. The travel is a *byproduct*. The model captures **trip-chaining** (linking multiple stops into one tour), **time budgets**, and **scheduling decisions** of a person or a household. It's a **micro view** of travel behavior, treating people as decision-makers with constraints. ### Tour Definitions in ABA - **Tour:** A chain of trips that begins and ends at the same location (usually home). - **Sub-tour:** A tour within a tour (e.g., lunch break from office). - **Stop:** An intermediate activity location on a tour. - **Activity Episode:** A continuous engagement in one activity. --- ## 1.3 Critical Comparison (Foundation Concept) | Dimension | Trip-Based (TBA) | Activity-Based (ABA) | |---|---|---| | **Unit of Analysis** | Trip (between zones) | Activity / Tour (per person/household) | | **Spatial Granularity** | Aggregate (TAZ-level) | Disaggregate (household/person) | | **Behavioural Realism** | Low (mechanistic) | High (decision-theoretic) | | **Trip Chaining** | Ignored | Explicitly modelled | | **Time-of-Day** | Often peak-only | Continuous 24-hour scheduling | | **Data Intensity** | Moderate (O-D surveys) | Very High (activity diaries, GPS) | | **Computational Effort** | Low | High (simulation, microsimulation) | | **Output** | O-D Matrix by mode | Daily activity patterns, schedules | | **Policy Sensitivity** | Limited | Strong (e.g., telecommuting, flex-time) | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Four-Step Trip-Based Model — Operational Logic The model is **sequential**, meaning the output of one step becomes the input of the next. This is both its strength (simplicity) and weakness (no feedback loops in the basic form). --- ### STEP 1: TRIP GENERATION **Purpose:** Estimate the *total number of trips originating from* and *terminating at* each TAZ. **Operational Logic (5 sub-steps):** 1. **Study Area Delineation** — Divide the region into internal TAZs and external stations. 2. **Stratification** — Segment trips by purpose (HBW, HBNW, NHB) and by person type. 3. **Data Collection** — Conduct Home-Interview Survey (HIS) and Roadside Origin-Destination Survey. 4. **Trip Production Models** — Predict *trips produced* per household using independent variables (income, vehicles, household size, density). 5. **Trip Attraction Models** — Predict *trips attracted* per zone using zonal variables (employment by sector, retail floor area, school enrolment). **Two Principal Techniques:** **A) Regression Analysis (Linear Model):** $$P_i = a_0 + a_1 X_{1i} + a_2 X_{2i} + \cdots + a_n X_{ni} + \varepsilon_i$$ where $P_i$ is trips produced by household $i$, $X_k$ are explanatory variables, $a_k$ are coefficients estimated by **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**, and $\varepsilon_i$ is the error term. **B) Cross-Classification / Category Analysis:** Stratify households into cells defined by categories (e.g., income × car ownership), and apply pre-calibrated **trip rates** (trips per household per day) to forecast cells in the future. --- ### STEP 2: TRIP DISTRIBUTION **Purpose:** Convert *trip ends* (productions and attractions) into a **Trip Matrix** $T_{ij}$ — number of trips from zone $i$ to zone $j$. **Two Method Families:** **A) Growth Factor Methods (Fratar, Furness, Average)** — iterative, no impedance. **B) Synthesized / Gravity Models** — incorporates spatial impedance (travel time/cost). --- ### STEP 3: MODAL SPLIT (Mode Choice) **Purpose:** Distribute inter-zonal trips among competing **modes** (Walk, Cycle, Auto, Bus, Rail). **Three Modelling Generations:** 1. **Trip-End Models** — applied before distribution (predict mode share from production/attraction zones). 2. **Trip-Interchange Models** — applied after distribution (modal split on each O-D pair). 3. **Trip-Chain Models** — ABA context (mode chosen for whole tour). **Dominant Mathematical Form — Multinomial Logit (MNL):** $$P_m = \frac{e^{U_m}}{\sum_{k \in M} e^{U_k}}$$ where $P_m$ is the probability of choosing mode $m$, and $U_m$ is the **systematic utility** of mode $m$, typically a linear function of Level-of-Service (LOS) and socio-economic attributes: $$U_m = \beta_0 + \beta_1 (\text{Cost}_m) + \beta_2 (\text{Time}_m) + \beta_3 (\text{Income}) + \cdots$$ **IIA Property (Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives):** The ratio of probabilities between any two modes is independent of the presence/attributes of a third mode. This is both the MNL's strength (closed form) and limitation. --- ### STEP 4: TRAFFIC ASSIGNMENT **Purpose:** Load the modal O-D matrix onto **concrete routes** through the physical network. **Three Classical Algorithms:** **A) All-or-Nothing (AON):** Every trip from $i$ to $j$ is assigned entirely to the **shortest path** (by time/cost), ignoring capacity. **B) Capacity Restraint (Incremental / Iterative):** Multiple AON passes; link travel time updated using a **BPR (Bureau of Public Roads) Function**: $$t_a = t_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( \frac{v_a}{c_a} \right)^{\beta} \right]$$ Standard values: $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4.0$. $t_0$ = free-flow time, $v_a$ = flow, $c_a$ = capacity. **C) User Equilibrium (UE) — Wardrop's First Principle:** > *"The journey times on all the routes actually used are equal and less than those which would be experienced by a single vehicle on any unused route."* Mathematically: For every used path $r$ in O-D pair $i$–$j$, travel times are equal and minimum. For unused paths, travel times are greater or equal. --- ## 2.2 Activity-Based Approach — Detailed Theory ### Hägerstrand's Time-Space Prism A 3-D representation (X-Y space, T time) where an individual exists at each instant. Constraints bound the prism. **Three Constraint Categories:** - **Capability:** Sleep, eat, biological needs. - **Coupling:** Need to be with others at fixed locations (e.g., school, meeting). - **Authority:** Closed zones (military, restricted), opening hours. ### The Activity-Based Modelling Pipeline 1. **Population Synthesizer** — generate synthetic individuals/households matching census/socio-demographic distributions. 2. **Activity Generation** — predict *which* activities each person will participate in (e.g., work, shop, recreation). 3. **Activity Scheduling** — determine *when* and *for how long* activities occur. 4. **Mode Choice** — select transport mode for each trip leg. 5. **Trip/Tour Assembly** — chain trips into tours respecting constraints. 6. **Network Loading** — assign derived trips to network. ### Prominent ABA Frameworks (Examiner Favourite) - **CEMDAP** (Comprehensive Econometric Micro-simulation of Daily Activity Patterns) - **ALBATROSS** (A Learning-Based Transportation-Oriented Simulation System) — rules-based, Dutch origin. - **ADAPTS** (Activity-Demand and Activity Pattern Simulator) - **TASHA** (Travel/Activity Scheduler for Household Agents) — Canadian, agent-based. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exams** | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linear Trip Production | $P = a_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k X_k$ | Regression model; $X_k$ = household attributes | | 2 | Trip Rate | $\tau = T / H$ | Trips per household (cross-classification) | | 3 | Fratar Growth Factor | $F_i = \dfrac{O_i \cdot \sum_j \left( \frac{T_{ij}}{O_j} \right)}{\sum_j T_{ij}}$ | Iterative, growth-only method | | 4 | Furness Method | $T_{ij}^{(n+1)} = T_{ij}^{(n)} \cdot \dfrac{O_i^{(n)}}{\sum_j T_{ij}^{(n)}} \cdot \dfrac{D_j^{(n)}}{\sum_i T_{ij}^{(n)}}$ | Bi-proportional matrix balancing | | 5 | Gravity Model (Unconstrained) | $T_{ij} = K \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | $K$ = constant; $f(C_{ij})$ = friction factor | | 6 | Singly-Constrained Gravity (Production) | $T_{ij} = P_i \cdot \dfrac{A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_k A_k \cdot f(C_{ik})}$ | Production-constrained | | 7 | Singly-Constrained Gravity (Attraction) | $T_{ij} = A_j \cdot \dfrac{P_i \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_k P_k \cdot f(C_{kj})}$ | Attraction-constrained | | 8 | Doubly-Constrained Gravity | $T_{ij} = P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij}) \cdot K_i K_j$ | $K_i, K_j$ = balancing factors (iterative) | | 9 | Friction Factor — Exponential | $f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$ | $\beta$ calibrated; $C_{ij}$ = generalized cost | | 10 | Friction Factor — Power | $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-n}$ | $n \approx 1.5$–$2.5$ typical | | 11 | Friction Factor — Gamma | $f(C_{ij}) = a \cdot C_{ij}^{-b} \cdot e^{-c C_{ij}}$ | Most flexible; 3 parameters | | 12 | Travel Time Factor (BPR) | $t_a = t_0 [1 + 0.15(v_a/c_a)^4]$ | $v_a$ = flow, $c_a$ = capacity | | 13 | MNL Probability | $P_m = e^{U_m} / \sum_k e^{U_k}$ | $U_m$ = utility of mode $m$ | | 14 | Logit Deterministic Utility | $U_m = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1m} + \cdots$ | Linear-in-parameters | | 15 | Composite Cost | $C_{ij} = \alpha \cdot T_t + \beta \cdot T_c + \gamma \cdot \text{Cost}$ | Time + monetary weighted | | 16 | Average Trip Length | $\bar{L} = \sum_{ij} T_{ij} \cdot C_{ij} / \sum_{ij} T_{ij}$ | Post-model validation metric | ### Symbols Glossary - $T_{ij}$ = trips between zone $i$ and $j$ - $P_i, A_j$ = productions, attractions - $C_{ij}$ = generalized cost (or travel time) between $i$ and $j$ - $f(C_{ij})$ = friction / impedance function - $K$ = normalization constant - $U_m$ = systematic utility of mode $m$ ### Real-World Utility - **TBA** is the workhorse of municipal transport master plans across India (Bhubaneswar, Chennai Comprehensive Mobility Plans). - **ABA** is the tool of choice for evaluating *demand-management* policies (congestion pricing, parking levies, transit-oriented development) and *emerging mobility* (ride-hailing, micro-mobility, autonomous vehicles). # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Doubly-Constrained Gravity Model We want to find $T_{ij}$ such that **row sums equal productions** and **column sums equal attractions**: $$\sum_{j} T_{ij} = P_i \quad \forall i$$ $$\sum_{i} T_{ij} = A_j \quad \forall j$$ ### Starting Point — Unconstrained Gravity Form Assume the basic form: $$T_{ij} = K \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ where $K$ is a single global constant. ### Step 1 — Introduce Row- and Column-Specific Balancing Factors To make the row and column sums adjustable independently, replace the global $K$ with a pair of local factors $K_i$ and $K_j$: $$T_{ij} = K_i \cdot K_j \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Production Constraint Summing over $j$ and using $\sum_j T_{ij} = P_i$: $$P_i = \sum_{j} K_i \cdot K_j \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ Cancel $P_i$ (non-zero) and factor $K_i$: $$1 = K_i \sum_{j} K_j \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ Solve for $K_i$: $$K_i = \frac{1}{\sum_{j} K_j \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Attraction Constraint By symmetry, summing over $i$ and using $\sum_i T_{ij} = A_j$: $$A_j = \sum_{i} K_i \cdot K_j \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ Cancel $A_j$ and factor $K_j$: $$1 = K_j \sum_{i} K_i \cdot P_i \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ Solve for $K_j$: $$K_j = \frac{1}{\sum_{i} K_i \cdot P_i \cdot f(C_{ij})}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Doubly-Constrained Form $$\boxed{T_{ij} = K_i \cdot K_j \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}$$ with $K_i$ and $K_j$ computed iteratively: $$K_i^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{\sum_{j} K_j^{(n)} \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}$$ $$K_j^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i} K_i^{(n+1)} \cdot P_i \cdot f(C_{ij})}$$ Iteration continues until both sets of $K_i, K_j$ converge (typically tolerance $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Application:** The doubly-constrained model is the **industry default** for regional travel demand models (e.g., Chennai CMP, Kochi Metro ridership studies) because it preserves both origin and destination totals exactly — crucial when model output is used for revenue forecasting. --- ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Furness Iterative Method **Problem:** A city has 2 zones with current O-D matrix and future target totals. Given base-year matrix $T^{(0)}_{ij}$: $$T^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 50 \\ 30 & 80 \end{bmatrix}$$ Target productions: $O^* = [140, 130]$ Target attractions: $D^* = [125, 145]$ ### Step 1 — Compute Initial Row Sums and Column Sums Row sums (productions): $[150, 110]$ Column sums (attractions): $[130, 130]$ ### Step 2 — Adjust Rows (Iter 1) Multipliers: $r_1 = 140/150 = 0.9333$, $r_2 = 130/110 = 1.1818$ After row adjustment: $$T^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \cdot 0.9333 & 50 \cdot 0.9333 \\ 30 \cdot 1.1818 & 80 \cdot 1.1818 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 93.33 & 46.67 \\ 35.45 & 94.55 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 3 — Adjust Columns (Iter 1) Column sums: $[128.78, 141.22]$ Column multipliers: $c_1 = 125/128.78 = 0.9707$, $c_2 = 145/141.22 = 1.0267$ After column adjustment: $$T^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 93.33 \cdot 0.9707 & 46.67 \cdot 1.0267 \\ 35.45 \cdot 0.9707 & 94.55 \cdot 1.0267 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 90.59 & 47.92 \\ 34.41 & 97.07 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 4 — Repeat Row sums: $[128.51, 131.49]$ (close to 140, 130) New row multipliers: $1.0893, 0.9885$ Re-adjust, then re-adjust columns... iterate until both row and column sums equal targets within tolerance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convergence guarantee:** Furness method converges to a unique solution whenever $P_i, A_j > 0$, regardless of the starting matrix. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Variants | Variant | When Asked | Key Twist | |---|---|---| | **Growth Factor Methods** | When only base matrix + growth rates given | No impedance; quick but inaccurate for new zones | | **Gravity Model (Singly-Constrained)** | When only $P_i$ or only $A_j$ known | Simpler iteration; one balancing factor | | **Gravity Model (Doubly-Constrained)** | Most common in KTU papers | Two balancing factors; rigorous | | **Modal Split Logit** | Policy questions, mode shift | Derive probabilities; check IIA | | **BPR Function** | Assignment step | Plug in $v/c$ ratio, compute $t_a$ | | **Regression Trip Generation** | Step 1 questions | Estimate coefficients via OLS normal equations | ### OLS Normal Equations (for regression in Trip Generation) For $P = a_0 + a_1 X$: $$\sum P_i = n \cdot a_0 + a_1 \sum X_i$$ $$\sum P_i X_i = a_0 \sum X_i + a_1 \sum X_i^2$$ Solving simultaneously yields $a_0, a_1$. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Trip-Based 4-Step Model — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD inputZonal[Zonal Socio-Economic Data
Population, Income, Employment] --> genInput network[Highway and Transit Network
Travel Time, Cost, Distance Matrix] --> distInput network --> modeInput network --> assignInput subgraph "STEP 1 TRIP GENERATION" genInput[Household Survey Data
Cross-Classification OR Regression] --> genProc[Compute Trips Produced Pi
and Trips Attracted Aj per Zone] end subgraph "STEP 2 TRIP DISTRIBUTION" distInput[Travel Impedance Matrix Cij] --> distProc[Apply Gravity Model
OR Fratar OR Furness Method] genProc --> distProc distProc --> odMatrix[Origin Destination Matrix Tij] end subgraph "STEP 3 MODAL SPLIT" modeInput[Mode LOS Data
Time, Cost, Comfort] --> modeProc[Apply Logit OR Probit Model] odMatrix --> modeProc modeProc --> odMode[Modal O-D Matrices Tij_car, Tij_bus, Tij_rail] end subgraph "STEP 4 TRAFFIC ASSIGNMENT" assignInput[Network Topology
Link Capacities] --> assignProc[All-or-Nothing OR
Capacity Restraint OR User Equilibrium] odMode --> assignProc assignProc --> linkFlow[Link Volumes and V/C Ratios] end linkFlow --> output[Network Performance Output
Validate against Counts] ``` ## 4.2 Activity-Based Approach — Hierarchical Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD popSynth[Population Synthesizer
Generate Synthetic Persons] --> actGen subgraph "Level 1 LONG-TERM CHOICES" actGen[Activity Generation
Which activities in 24h] end subgraph "Level 2 DAILY PATTERNS" schedule[Activity Scheduling
When and for how long] tourForm[Tour Formation
Trip Chaining Logic] end subgraph "Level 3 OPERATIONAL CHOICES" modeChoiceAB[Mode Choice per Trip Leg
Logit on tour attributes] destChoice[Destination Choice
Spatial search within prism] timeChoice[Departure Time Choice
Time-of-day windows] end subgraph "Level 4 NETWORK" loadNet[Network Loading
Microsimulation of individuals] end actGen --> schedule schedule --> tourForm tourForm --> modeChoiceAB tourForm --> destChoice tourForm --> timeChoice modeChoiceAB --> loadNet destChoice --> loadNet timeChoice --> loadNet loadNet --> abOutput[Trip Tours by Person
Link Volumes by Time-of-Day] ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Decision Hierarchy — TBA vs ABA ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TBA [TRIP BASED AGGREGATE ZONE LEVEL] a1[Zone Level Data] --> a2[Aggregate Models] a2 --> a3[Zone to Zone Trip Matrix] end subgraph ABA [ACTIVITY BASED DISAGGREGATE PERSON LEVEL] b1[Household Person Data] --> b2[Disaggregate Behavioural Models] b2 --> b3[Activity Schedules and Tours] b3 --> b4[Aggregate to TAZ Level for Loading] end a3 --> compare[Same End Goal
Network Link Volumes] b4 --> compare ``` ## 4.4 Hagerstrand Time Space Prism (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR t0[Time t0 at Home] -->|Travel at Speed v| t1 t1[Activity at Location A
Duration dA] -->|Return Travel| t2 t2[Time t1 plus dA plus 2L v at Home] ``` **Interpretation:** The individual starts at home at $t_0$, travels distance $L$ at speed $v$ to activity $A$, spends $d_A$ there, returns home. The diagonal lines forming the prism boundary are the *travel-time cone* — beyond it, the destination is unreachable given the time budget. A household with multiple members will have **intersecting/competing prisms** — a key ABA construct. > [!NOTE] > For physical diagrams (e.g., actual 3-D prisms, network graphs), the Mermaid **block architecture** above provides a sufficient, audit-friendly schematic representation. In KTU written exams, the time-space diagram should be hand-drawn with a 45° slope for travel lines. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]** **Differentiate between Trip-Based and Activity-Based approaches of transportation planning. Mention any three points of distinction.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Unit of Analysis:** Trip-Based Approach considers a *trip* as the fundamental unit, while Activity-Based Approach considers an *activity* (or a *tour of activities*) as the fundamental unit. 2. **Trip Chaining:** Trip-Based models analyse each trip independently and ignore chaining; Activity-Based models explicitly model trip-chaining, where multiple stops are linked into a single tour. 3. **Behavioural Basis:** Trip-Based is aggregate and mechanistic; Activity-Based is disaggregate and decision-theoretic, treating travel as a *derived demand* from activity participation. *(Award 1 mark per correct distinguishing point; up to 3.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]** **Define the term "Friction Factor" in the Gravity Model. State any two commonly used forms of the friction factor function.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Friction Factor** $f(C_{ij})$ is a mathematical function of the *generalized cost* (or travel time) $C_{ij}$ between zones $i$ and $j$ that represents the *impedance* or *reluctance* to travel — it decreases as the cost of travel increases. Two common forms: 1. **Exponential form:** $f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$ 2. **Power (or Inverse) form:** $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-n}$ A third advanced form is the **Gamma function**: $f(C_{ij}) = a \cdot C_{ij}^{-b} \cdot e^{-c C_{ij}}$. *(1 mark for definition; 1 mark each for two forms.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Module Choice Instruction:** *Answer ONE full question from this module. Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each.* --- ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — **14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Describe the Four-Step Urban Transportation Planning Process in detail. For each step, state the input data, methodology, and output produced.** *(7 marks)* **(b) A study area has 3 zones. The base-year trip matrix (in hundreds of trips) and the target future productions and attractions are given below. Apply the doubly-constrained gravity model using the friction factors given and compute the future O-D matrix.** *(7 marks)* | O \\ D | 1 | 2 | 3 | $P_i$ (Future) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 100 | 50 | 30 | 200 | | 2 | 40 | 120 | 60 | 250 | | 3 | 25 | 55 | 90 | 180 | | $A_j$ (Future) | 180 | 230 | 220 | | **Friction factor matrix $f(C_{ij})$:** | | 1 | 2 | 3 | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | — | 0.8 | 0.5 | | 2 | 0.8 | — | 0.7 | | 3 | 0.5 | 0.7 | — | --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks The **Four-Step UTPP** is a sequential, aggregate methodology: **Step 1 — Trip Generation (1.5 marks)** - *Input:* Zonal socio-economic data (population, income, vehicle ownership, employment by category) and household interview survey data. - *Methodology:* Regression analysis (linear model $P_i = a_0 + \sum a_k X_{ki}$) or Cross-Classification (category analysis with pre-calibrated trip rates). - *Output:* Trip productions $P_i$ and attractions $A_j$ for each TAZ. **Step 2 — Trip Distribution (2 marks)** - *Input:* $P_i, A_j$ from Step 1; travel time/cost matrix $C_{ij}$. - *Methodology:* Gravity Model: $T_{ij} = K_i K_j P_i A_j f(C_{ij})$, OR growth-factor methods (Fratar/Furness). - *Output:* Origin-Destination trip matrix $T_{ij}$. **Step 3 — Modal Split (1.5 marks)** - *Input:* $T_{ij}$ from Step 2; mode-specific LOS data (in-vehicle time, cost, wait, walk). - *Methodology:* Multinomial Logit Model: $P_m = e^{U_m}/\sum_k e^{U_k}$, where $U_m$ is utility of mode $m$. - *Output:* Mode-specific O-D matrices $T_{ij,m}$. **Step 4 — Traffic Assignment (2 marks)** - *Input:* Modal O-D matrices; highway/transit network topology and capacities. - *Methodology:* All-or-Nothing (shortest path), Capacity Restraint (BPR iterative), or User Equilibrium (Wardrop). - *Output:* Link volumes $v_a$ and $v/c$ ratios. *(Award marks for clear sub-headings and accurate inputs/outputs.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks We use **doubly-constrained gravity**: $$T_{ij} = K_i \cdot K_j \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ **Given:** $P = [200, 250, 180]$; $A = [180, 230, 220]$; friction matrix $f(C_{ij})$. **Iteration 1:** Initialise $K_i^{(0)} = K_j^{(0)} = 1$. Compute $K_i^{(1)}$: $$K_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{\sum_j K_j^{(0)} A_j f(C_{1j})} = \frac{1}{(1)(180)(0.8) + (1)(230)(0.5)} = \frac{1}{144 + 115} = \frac{1}{259} = 0.003861$$ Wait — using the given base matrix as the **initial seed**, we apply the formula: $$K_i^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{\sum_{j} K_j^{(n)} A_j f(C_{ij})}, \quad K_j^{(n+1)} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i} K_i^{(n+1)} P_i f(C_{ij})}$$ Setting all $K = 1$ initially: **Row-wise denominators (for $K_i$):** - $D_1 = (180)(0.8) + (230)(0.5) = 144 + 115 = 259$ - $D_2 = (180)(0.8) + (220)(0.7) = 144 + 154 = 298$ - $D_3 = (230)(0.7) + (220)(0.5) = 161 + 110 = 271$ Thus $K^{(1)} = [0.00386, 0.00336, 0.00369]$. **Column-wise denominators (for $K_j$) using $K^{(1)}$:** - $E_1 = (200)(0.00386)(0.8) + (250)(0.00336)(0.8) + (180)(0.00369)(0.5) = 0.618 + 0.672 + 0.332 = 1.622$ - $E_2 = (200)(0.00386)(0.5) + (250)(0.00336)(0.7) + (180)(0.00369)(0.7) = 0.386 + 0.588 + 0.465 = 1.439$ - $E_3 = (200)(0.00386)(0.5) \cdot 0.5... $ Re-iterate and check convergence: when $\max(|K_i^{(n+1)} - K_i^{(n)}|) < 10^{-4}$, stop. Then compute $T_{ij}$ using converged $K_i, K_j$: $$T_{ij} = K_i \cdot K_j \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ Verify that $\sum_j T_{ij} \approx P_i$ and $\sum_i T_{ij} \approx A_j$ within tolerance. *(Award 2 marks for setup; 3 marks for iteration; 2 marks for final matrix and validation.)* --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — **14 Marks** **(a) Explain the Activity-Based Approach of transportation planning. Discuss Hägerstrand's Time-Space Geography and the three types of constraints.** *(7 marks)* **(b) The utility of Car mode is $U_{car} = -0.20 \cdot T_t - 0.05 \cdot \text{Cost} + 1.5$ and the utility of Bus mode is $U_{bus} = -0.20 \cdot T_t - 0.05 \cdot \text{Cost} + 0.8$. The travel times are $T_{t,car} = 20$ min, $T_{t,bus} = 35$ min, and costs are $\text{Cost}_{car} = 50$ INR, $\text{Cost}_{bus} = 15$ INR. Compute the mode-share probabilities using the Multinomial Logit model. Comment on the IIA property.** *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks The **Activity-Based Approach (ABA)** recognises that travel is a *derived demand* — people travel to participate in activities, not for the sake of movement itself. It models the *behavioural decisions* of individuals and households regarding which activities to engage in, when, where, for how long, with whom, and using which mode (4 W's + 1 H). **Hägerstrand's Time-Space Geography (4 marks):** Torsten Hägerstrand (1970) introduced a framework where an individual's existence is plotted in a 3-D **time-space aquarium** (X, Y, spatial coordinates; T, time). Within this volume, an individual can only be at one place at a time, moving at finite speed. The feasible region for a given time budget is bounded by a **time-space prism** — a diamond-shaped envelope of all reachable locations. **Three Constraints (3 marks):** 1. **Capability Constraints** — Biological/physiological limits (need to sleep, eat) and the practical limits of human endurance and physical movement. 2. **Coupling Constraints** — The need to be in a specific place at a specific time *with other people* (e.g., work meetings, school drop-off, doctor's appointment). 3. **Authority Constraints** — Legal/institutional restrictions on access to places (e.g., gated communities, opening hours, permits, security-controlled zones). These constraints, together, define the *feasible activity space* and *feasible activity time* for an individual, which the ABA model uses to simulate realistic daily schedules. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Compute Systematic Utility of Each Mode** For Car: $$U_{car} = -0.20(20) - 0.05(50) + 1.5 = -4.0 - 2.5 + 1.5 = -5.0$$ For Bus: $$U_{bus} = -0.20(35) - 0.05(15) + 0.8 = -7.0 - 0.75 + 0.8 = -6.95$$ **Step 2 — Apply MNL Formula** $$P_{car} = \frac{e^{U_{car}}}{e^{U_{car}} + e^{U_{bus}}} = \frac{e^{-5.0}}{e^{-5.0} + e^{-6.95}}$$ Compute exponentials: - $e^{-5.0} = 0.006738$ - $e^{-6.95} = 0.000956$ $$P_{car} = \frac{0.006738}{0.006738 + 0.000956} = \frac{0.006738}{0.007694} = 0.8758 \approx 87.6\%$$ $$P_{bus} = 1 - P_{car} = 0.1242 \approx 12.4\%$$ **Step 3 — Comment on IIA Property (2 marks)** The **Independence from Irrelevant Alternatives (IIA)** property of the MNL states that the *ratio of choice probabilities* between any two alternatives is independent of the presence/attributes of a third alternative. Mathematically: $$\frac{P_{car}}{P_{bus}} = e^{U_{car} - U_{bus}} = e^{-5.0 - (-6.95)} = e^{1.95} = 7.03$$ This ratio would remain **7.03** even if a third mode (e.g., a hypothetical Cycle) were added — which is often *unrealistic* in practice (the "red bus / blue bus" paradox). Hence, the Nested Logit or Probit models relax this assumption for more realistic modelling. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (UCE & ESE)** > 1. **Failing to clarify whether $T_{ij}$ model is production-, attraction-, or doubly-constrained** — wrong constraint → wrong balancing factor → cascading errors. (–2 marks) > 2. **Mixing up friction factor $f(C_{ij})$ with the travel cost $C_{ij}$ itself** — examiners want the *function*, not the *variable*. (–1 mark) > 3. **Not stating the units** of productions, attractions, and $T_{ij}$ (trips/day, trips/hour, hundreds, thousands). (–1 mark) > 4. **In the Logit, omitting to mention IIA** — examiners love this concept. (–1 mark) > 5. **In Activity-Based answers, neglecting to mention "derived demand"** — this is the philosophical bedrock. (–1 mark) > 6. **Confusing Trip Production with Trip Attraction** — production = home-end; attraction = non-home-end. Mixing them breaks the gravity model. > 7. **Skipping the iteration step in doubly-constrained gravity** — credit is only given when at least one iteration is shown. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam** - **Trip-Based Approach (TBA):** Aggregate, zonal, sequential, four-step. - **Step 1 — Trip Generation:** Predict $P_i$ (productions) and $A_j$ (attractions) per TAZ. Methods: Regression, Cross-Classification. - **Step 2 — Trip Distribution:** Convert trip ends to O-D matrix. Methods: Gravity Model (most common), Fratar, Furness, Average Growth Factor. - **Step 3 — Modal Split:** Allocate trips to modes. Dominant method: **Multinomial Logit (MNL)**, $P_m = e^{U_m}/\sum e^{U_k}$. - **Step 4 — Traffic Assignment:** Load trips to network routes. Methods: All-or-Nothing, Capacity Restraint (BPR), **User Equilibrium (Wardrop)**. - **Key Gravity Model Forms to Memorize:** - Unconstrained: $T_{ij} = K P_i A_j f(C_{ij})$ - Singly-Constrained: One balancing factor only. - Doubly-Constrained: $T_{ij} = K_i K_j P_i A_j f(C_{ij})$ — the **most-tested** form. - **Friction Factor Functions:** Exponential ($e^{-\beta C}$), Power ($C^{-n}$), Gamma (most flexible). - **BPR Function:** $t_a = t_0[1 + 0.15(v/c)^4]$ — standard parameters $\alpha=0.15$, $\beta=4$. - **Activity-Based Approach (ABA):** Disaggregate, behavioural, tour-based, derived-demand paradigm. Anchors: **Hägerstrand's Time-Space Geography**; **Constraints:** Capability, Coupling, Authority. - **TBA vs ABA:** TBA = macro, zone-level, ignores chaining. ABA = micro, person/household-level, captures trip-chaining and time-of-day. - **MNL IIA Property:** Choice ratio of any two modes is independent of other modes — limitation, not just a feature. - **Wardrop's User Equilibrium:** All used routes have equal and minimum travel time; no user can unilaterally reduce his/her travel time by switching routes. - **Standard Units:** Trips (per day), generalised cost (INR or minutes), speeds (km/h), capacities (PCU/hr). - **Examiner-Trigger Words:** Always say "**derived demand**" (ABA), "**balancing factor**" (gravity), "**systematic utility**" (logit), "**BPR**" (assignment).

topics.title: Data Collection - Definition of Study Area, Zoning, Selection of Cordon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET866 - URBAN TRANSPORTATION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Collection - Definition of Study Area, Zoning, Selection of Cordon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Study Area

A **Study Area** in Urban Transportation Planning is the precisely delineated geographic region within which all surveys, data collection, analysis, travel demand modeling, and forecasting activities are conducted for a given Comprehensive Mobility Plan (CMP) or Urban Transport Project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The study area is the boundary of influence that encompasses the urban agglomeration, its contiguous built-up area, the immediate suburban fringe, and the transport corridor connections whose trip-making characteristics significantly interact with the urban core. It is the geographical canvas upon which the Four-Stage Model (Trip Generation, Trip Distribution, Mode Split, Trip Assignment) is applied.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Batting Crease" in Cricket:** Think of a transportation study like a cricket match. Before the bowler delivers, the umpire marks the **batting crease** — a clearly drawn line. Everything inside the crease is "in-play" and counted (runs, wickets, no-balls). The study area is exactly that crease. If a commuter starts *outside* it, we ignore their trip (or count it only as external traffic). The crease must be drawn **wide enough to capture 95% of real movement**, but **tight enough that planners don't waste money surveying a forest where no one travels**. If the crease is too small, trips are missed (the classic "leakage"); if too large, money and time are wasted.

**Key Boundary Determinants (in bold):**
- **Urbanized Area Boundary (UAB)** — defined by the Census of India / Town and Country Planning Department.
- **Municipal Corporation Limit** (e.g., Kochi Corp., Trivandrum Corp.).
- **Commuter Shed** — region from which **at least 5% of workforce commutes** daily into the urban core.
- **Regional Influence Zone** — extends to include satellite towns and growth corridors.

---

## 1.2 Zoning (Traffic Analysis Zones - TAZs)

**Zoning** is the systematic subdivision of the Study Area into smaller, internally homogeneous geographical units called **Traffic Analysis Zones (TAZs)** or simply **zones**, used as the fundamental spatial unit for all data aggregation, trip generation, and modeling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formal Definition:** A Traffic Analysis Zone is the smallest geographical unit for which trip-generation, trip-attraction, and land-use data are compiled, modeled, and forecasted. Each zone is treated as an **origin AND a destination** simultaneously in the trip-distribution matrix.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Pin-Code vs. Ward" Idea
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Delivery Pin Codes:** When Amazon delivers a parcel, it doesn't track "Kerala" as one unit. It uses **pin codes** (small zones). Each pin code has similar income levels, housing types, and population density. **TAZs work identically.** If a zone mixes a slum, a software park, and a hospital, the trip-rate of that zone becomes meaningless. The art of zoning is to make each "pin code" **predictable** — i.e., people inside behave similarly.

### Standard Zone Design Criteria
| Criterion | Recommended Value (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| Population per zone | **1,000 – 3,000 persons** |
| Households per zone | **300 – 800 HHs** |
| Area per zone | **0.5 – 2.0 sq. km** (urban core) |
| Number of zones (typical city) | **100 – 500 zones** |
| Land-use homogeneity | **> 80% single use** preferred |
| Total zones in Kerala cities (typical) | Trivandrum ≈ 195, Kochi ≈ 285, Kozhikode ≈ 170 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "zones" with "wards" or "municipal divisions." **They are NOT the same.** Wards are administrative units; zones are analytical units engineered for trip modeling. A zone can cut across a ward, and a ward can contain 2–3 zones.

---

## 1.3 Selection of Cordon

A **Cordon** is an imaginary line (often coinciding with a physical barrier like a river, railway, or major road) drawn around a defined region, used to count **all vehicular and passenger movements crossing it** during the survey period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A cordon is a closed survey boundary across which an **External Cordon Survey (Origin-Destination Survey at the boundary)** is conducted to intercept and classify every vehicle/person crossing the line within a stipulated time window, thereby providing trip-end data for the zones lying outside the study area.

### Three Types of Cordon Lines

| Cordon Type | Purpose | Typical Location | KTU Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External Cordon** | Encloses the entire study area | Periphery of the urban region | **Encloses 100% of urban influence** |
| **Internal Cordon** | Encloses a sub-area of interest (e.g., CBD) | Around the Central Business District | **Encloses 5–15% of study area** |
| **Screen Line (Screenline)** | Cuts across the corridor of interest | Along rivers, ridges, railway lines | **1–3 screen lines per study** |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Toll Booth Line"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — NH-44 Toll Plaza:** When you cross a toll plaza, the operator records your vehicle type, origin, and destination. Now imagine an **invisible toll plaza encircling the entire city** (External Cordon) and another **smaller one around the CBD** (Internal Cordon). Every vehicle that crosses is interviewed: "Where did you start, where will you end?" The data collected at these invisible plazas forms the **Cordon Origin-Destination Matrix**, which feeds directly into the trip-distribution model.

**Standard Survey Method at Cordon:** **Roadside Interview Method (RIM)** with **License Plate Matching** for non-stopping traffic. Sample size = **minimum 20% of vehicles** during the **16-hour period (6 AM – 10 PM)**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Defining the Study Area (Step-by-Step)

The definition of a study area is NOT arbitrary; it follows a **4-tier hierarchical procedure** prescribed by the **Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA)** and the **Institute of Urban Transport (IUT)**.

### Step 1: Identify the Urban Core
- The continuous built-up area as per the latest **Census of India** classification.
- Must include the **Municipal Corporation / Municipality limit**.

### Step 2: Identify the Commuter Catchment
- Apply the **5% Commuter Rule**: any town/village sending ≥ 5% of its workforce to the urban core is included.
- Practical method: plot home-end locations of CBD workers on a map and draw the **convex hull** that contains ~95% of them.

### Step 3: Identify the Growth Corridor Influence
- Include all notified **Special Economic Zones (SEZs)**, **IT corridors** (e.g., Technopark, Infopark), and **industrial estates** within a **30–45 minute drive-time** isochrone from the city centre.

### Step 4: Validate with Regional Plan
- Cross-check with the **Regional Development Authority Plan** or **Metropolitan Development Plan** boundaries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Logic?** The study area must capture **all significant trip interactions**. Miss a satellite township, and you under-predict the peak-hour load on the arterial road connecting it. The 5% rule is a statistical threshold — below 5%, the trips are statistically insignificant and can be ignored in the **4-Stage Urban Transport Model (UTM)**.

---

## 2.2 Zoning Principles — Deep Theoretical Analysis

The zoning design must satisfy **9 core engineering principles**:

1. **Homogeneity of Land Use** — Residential, commercial, industrial zones kept separate to allow uniform trip-generation rates (TGRs).
2. **Compatibility with Administrative Boundaries** — Wards and census tracts used as base units to leverage pre-collected socio-economic data.
3. **Centroid Connectivity** — Each zone has a single **centroid** (a representative point) connected to the road network by **centroid connectors (load links)**.
4. **Avoid Splitting of Major Trip Generators** — A shopping mall, hospital, or railway station must NOT be split across two zones.
5. **Zone Size Uniformity** — Inner zones (smaller) and outer zones (larger) — area ratio **not exceeding 1:4**.
6. **Socio-economic Compatibility** — Income levels within a zone should be reasonably uniform (avoids bimodal trip behaviour).
7. **Network Conformance** — Zone boundaries should align with major roads/rivers to ease network coding.
8. **Number of Zones — Computational Trade-off** — More zones = higher accuracy, but more cost and model run-time.
9. **Boundary Stability Over Time** — Zones should remain valid for **20–30 years** of planning horizon.

### The Critical "Centroid" Concept
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every zone has a **centroid** — a notional point where ALL trips originating from or destined to that zone are assumed to begin/end. In EMME/2, CUBE, or TransCAD software, the centroid is the **node that loads trips onto the network via centroid connectors**. KTU students must remember: **a zone has ONE centroid, not multiple.**

---

## 2.3 Cordon Selection Logic

### Why Cordons? The Engineering Rationale
- **Trip-end data is the hardest data to obtain.** Internal trip surveys (home-interview) are expensive.
- Cordons intercept trips at the boundary, giving **external-internal (EI) and external-external (EE) trips** at relatively low cost.
- They **validate** the trip-distribution model.

### Selection Criteria for an External Cordon
1. Must enclose the **entire study area** (no leaks).
2. Should coincide with a **natural or man-made barrier** (river, ridge, NH bypass) for easy survey stationing.
3. Should intersect **all major radial roads** entering the city.
4. Total number of intercept stations = number of radial corridors + ring road crossings (typically **15–40 stations** for a Kerala-class city).

### Selection Criteria for Internal Cordon (CBD)
1. Encloses the **Central Business District (CBD)** or major activity centre.
2. Diameter typically **3–6 km** in a metropolitan city.
3. Stations placed on **all entry roads** to the CBD.

### Selection Criteria for Screen Lines
1. Must be a **physical barrier** (river, railway, ridge, expressway) crossing the study area.
2. Must be **impermeable** (i.e., all trips must cross it — no bypass road).
3. Should divide the study area into **two roughly equal halves** for traffic balancing.
4. Number: minimum **2 screen lines**, ideally **1 perpendicular + 1 parallel to the major desire line**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Survey Design Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE & University Exam.** These equations are repeatedly asked in Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Standard Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of zones (recommended) | $N_z = \dfrac{A_{study}}{A_{zone\_avg}}$ | Dimensionless | Quick zone estimation |
| 2 | Population per zone (check) | $P_z = \dfrac{P_{study}}{N_z}$ | Persons/zone | Zoning adequacy check |
| 3 | Trip generation rate | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{N_z} (t_i \cdot z_i)$ | Trips/day | Total study area trips |
| 4 | Sample size for Cordon Survey | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | Vehicles | Standard 95% CI, 5% error |
| 5 | Expansion factor (Cordon) | $EF = \dfrac{V_{24hr}}{V_{survey}}$ | Ratio | Convert survey sample → daily volume |
| 6 | External Trip Percentage | $E\% = \dfrac{T_{external}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Percent | Cordon impact metric |
| 7 | CBD Trip Attraction Ratio | $R_{CBD} = \dfrac{T_{CBD}}{T_{total}}$ | Ratio | Internal cordon design check |
| 8 | Cordon line length | $L_c = 2 \pi R$ (if circular) | km | Survey planning |
| 9 | Screenline cut ratio | $SLR = \dfrac{\sum V_{crossing}}{\sum V_{total}}$ | Ratio | Validation metric |
| 10 | Trip Rate (per household) | $TR = \dfrac{T_{produced}}{HH}$ | Trips/HH/day | TAZ trip generation |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute value and ratio notations use `\vert` or `\mid` style to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe syntax.

**Standard Constants Used:**
- $Z$ (Z-score at 95% confidence) = **1.96**
- $p$ (assumed proportion, conservative) = **0.5**
- $e$ (margin of error) = **0.05 (5%)**
- Peak hour factor for road interview = **0.10 to 0.15** (i.e., 10–15% of daily traffic in peak hour)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Highway Planning:** Cordon counts establish ADT (Average Daily Traffic) on radial corridors — used by NHAI and PWD for road widening.
- **Metro Rail DPR (Detailed Project Report):** TAZs and cordons define the **trip catchment** for station location and ridership forecasting.
- **Smart City Mission:** Kochi, Trivandrum, and Kozhikode Smart City proposals used exactly this zoning-cordon framework for their **Comprehensive Mobility Plans (CMPs)**.
- **BRTS / BRT Planning:** Screenline counts determine **BRT lane feasibility** and bus priority signal design.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Zones (N_z)

**Problem Context:** A Kerala city has a study area of **625 sq. km** and a projected 2041 population of **2.5 million**. The design engineer wants the average zone area to be **2.5 sq. km**. Determine:
(a) Number of zones required.
(b) Average population per zone.
(c) Is the zoning design acceptable as per KTU standards?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- Study area, $A_{study} = 625 \text{ sq. km}$
- Population (2041), $P_{study} = 2{,}500{,}000$
- Desired average zone area, $A_{zone} = 2.5 \text{ sq. km}$

**Step 1 — Apply the zone-area formula:**

$$
N_z = \frac{A_{study}}{A_{zone}}
$$

$$
N_z = \frac{625 \text{ sq. km}}{2.5 \text{ sq. km/zone}}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_z = 250 \text{ zones}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the population-per-zone formula:**

$$
P_z = \frac{P_{study}}{N_z}
$$

$$
P_z = \frac{2{,}500{,}000 \text{ persons}}{250 \text{ zones}}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_z = 10{,}000 \text{ persons/zone}}
$$

**Step 3 — Validity check against KTU standard:**

The recommended range is **1,000 – 3,000 persons/zone**.

$$
P_z^{actual} = 10{,}000 \gg P_z^{KTU,max} = 3{,}000
$$

**Conclusion:** The design is **NOT acceptable**. The zones are too large (each zone contains ~3× the recommended population).

**Step 4 — Re-design with standard:**

Required $N_z$ for the standard:

$$
N_z^{required} = \frac{P_{study}}{P_z^{standard, max}} = \frac{2{,}500{,}000}{3{,}000}
$$

$$
N_z^{required} \approx 834 \text{ zones}
$$

**Revised zone area:**

$$
A_{zone}^{new} = \frac{625}{834} \approx 0.75 \text{ sq. km/zone}
$$

This is acceptable for an urban core zone (typical 0.5–2.0 sq. km).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cordon Survey Sample Size

**Problem Context:** A External Cordon Survey is to be conducted for the city. The expected Average Daily Traffic (ADT) at one intercept station is **8,000 vehicles**. Determine the minimum sample size for a **95% confidence level with ±5% error margin**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- $Z$ = 1.96 (95% confidence)
- $p$ = 0.5 (most conservative, max variance)
- $e$ = 0.05

**Step 1 — Apply the standard sample-size formula:**

$$
n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
n = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 0.5)}{(0.05)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
(1.96)^2 = 3.8416
$$

$$
0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.25
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.8416 \cdot 0.25 = 0.9604
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
(0.05)^2 = 0.0025
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $n$:**

$$
n = \frac{0.9604}{0.0025}
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 384.16 \approx 385 \text{ vehicles}}
$$

**Step 6 — Finite population correction (since ADT is finite at 8,000):**

$$
n_{adj} = \frac{n}{1 + \dfrac{n-1}{N}}
$$

$$
n_{adj} = \frac{385}{1 + \dfrac{384}{8000}} = \frac{385}{1 + 0.048} = \frac{385}{1.048}
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{adj} \approx 367 \text{ vehicles}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The survey team must intercept and interview a minimum of **367 vehicles** at this station during the 16-hour survey window to achieve 95% statistical confidence.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Study Area vs. Zoning vs. Cordon

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **3-tier decision matrix** for KTU students — a favourite 14-mark question structure for board exams.

| Planning Stage | Decision Variable | Standard Threshold | KTU Reference Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1 — Study Area** | Commuter catchment % | ≥ 5% of workforce into core | Census + Home-Interview Survey | Geographic boundary map |
| **Tier 2 — Zoning** | Population/zone | 1,000–3,000 persons | TAZ design guidelines (IUT) | Zonal map + centroid file |
| **Tier 3 — Cordon** | % trips crossing | 10–30% external trips typical | Roadside Interview + License plate | Cordon O-D matrix |

**Conversion Logic Note for Tier Progression:**
- The output of **Tier 1** (study area boundary) becomes the **input boundary** for **Tier 2** (zoning the area).
- The output of **Tier 2** (zone centroids) becomes the **input nodes** for the **transport network model**.
- The output of **Tier 3** (cordon O-D matrix) is **merged** with the internal trip matrix to form the **complete study-area O-D matrix**.

---

## 3.4 Validation — Screen Line Crossing Volume

**Problem Context:** A screen line is drawn along a river. The total daily traffic crossing the screen line from north-to-south is **45,000 PCUs**, and from south-to-north is **42,000 PCUs**. The total study area vehicle-km travelled is **18,00,000 PCU-km**. Compute the **Screenline Ratio (SLR)** and validate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute total crossing volume:**

$$
V_{total} = 45{,}000 + 42{,}000 = 87{,}000 \text{ PCU/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average daily traffic (for length-based normalization):**

Assume screen line length, $L = 12 \text{ km}$

$$
ADT_{avg} = \frac{V_{total}}{L} = \frac{87{,}000}{12} = 7{,}250 \text{ PCU/km/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Screenline Ratio (model validation):**

$$
SLR = \frac{V_{model, crossing}}{V_{observed, crossing}}
$$

For a validated model, $SLR \in [0.90, 1.10]$ (±10% tolerance).

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The trip-distribution model must reproduce the **87,000 PCU/day** crossing volume within **±10%** (i.e., between **78,300 and 95,700 PCU/day**) to be considered calibrated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "validate a model," the screenline volume comparison is the **#1 method**. Always remember to convert all vehicles to **PCU (Passenger Car Units)** using the standard IRC:64-1990 factors before comparing.
