# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ). List any **four** essential criteria used in designing a TAZ.*

### **Model Answer:**

A **Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ)** is the smallest geographical unit for which socio-economic, land-use, and trip data are compiled and analyzed in the urban transport planning process. It serves as the fundamental origin-destination unit in the four-stage travel demand model.

**Four essential TAZ design criteria:**
1. **Homogeneity of land use** — a zone should ideally have a single dominant land use (residential, commercial, or industrial).
2. **Population range of 1,000–3,000 persons per zone** — ensures statistical reliability of trip-generation rates.
3. **Compatibility with administrative boundaries** — wards and census tracts should be used as base units.
4. **Centroid assignment** — each zone must have a single representative point (centroid) connected to the road network via centroid connectors.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for criteria — students must number the criteria clearly.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between an **External Cordon**, an **Internal Cordon**, and a **Screen Line** with one example each.*

### **Model Answer:**

| Cordon Type | Purpose | Location | Example (Kerala Context) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External Cordon** | Encloses the **entire study area** to intercept all external trips | Periphery of the city | Outer ring road encircling Trivandrum city |
| **Internal Cordon** | Encloses a **specific sub-area** (typically CBD) to capture CBD-bound trips | Around the CBD | Boundary around Trivandrum's Statue Junction – Palayam – Thampanoor CBD |
| **Screen Line** | A line (usually along a barrier) cutting the city to **validate the model** via volume comparison | Along a river / railway / ridge | Screen line along the Vembanad Lake backwaters in Kochi |

*(Examiner's Note: Tabular comparison is the best way to score full marks in 3-mark questions.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions in ESE carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (Q7 OR Q8, Q9 OR Q10, etc.). Both choices must be of **equal cognitive level** and must be from the same module. We present both Question A and Question B below as independent, full-mark alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for defining the Study Area** for a Comprehensive Mobility Plan (CMP) of a metropolitan city in Kerala. List all the criteria and justify each with a one-line engineering reason.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A proposed study area has an area of **400 sq. km** and a population of **1.2 million (as per 2041 projections)**. The design engineer has proposed **100 zones**. Critically evaluate the zoning design using KTU 2024 standards and recommend a revised number of zones with justification.

### **Model Answer (a):**

**Procedure for Defining the Study Area:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Urban Core:** Use Census of India data to identify the continuous built-up area. *Justification:* Forms the un-disputed base of the study area where 100% of trips originate/terminate.

2. **Step 2 — Apply the 5% Commuter Catchment Rule:** Identify all towns/villages from which ≥ 5% of the workforce commutes to the urban core daily. *Justification:* Statistically significant trip streams; ignoring them creates a "leakage" error in trip forecasting.

3. **Step 3 — Map the Growth Corridor Influence Zone:** Include all notified SEZs, IT parks (Technopark, Infopark, SmartCity), industrial estates within a 30–45 minute drive-time isochrone. *Justification:* These are major trip generators/attractors of the future; missing them will lead to severe under-design of connecting roads.

4. **Step 4 — Cross-validate with Regional/Metropolitan Development Plan:** Reconcile with the Town & Country Planning Department and the Metropolitan Development Authority boundaries. *Justification:* Ensures statutory alignment and prevents planning conflict.

5. **Step 5 — Conduct Pilot Surveys:** Carry out pilot home-interview and roadside surveys to check the validity of the boundary. *Justification:* Empirical confirmation of the theoretical boundary.

6. **Step 6 — Finalise the Boundary:** Prepare a final map at **1:50,000 scale** with the study area boundary marked in bold, with the municipal limit, growth corridors, and commuter catchment shown distinctly.

*(Examiner's Note: 7 marks = 1 mark per step + 1 mark for engineering justification summary.)*

### **Model Answer (b):**

**Given:**
- $A_{study} = 400 \text{ sq. km}$
- $P_{study} = 1{,}200{,}000$
- $N_z^{proposed} = 100$

**Step 1 — Compute average zone area:**

$$
A_{zone} = \frac{400}{100} = 4.0 \text{ sq. km/zone}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute population per zone:**

$$
P_z = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{100} = 12{,}000 \text{ persons/zone}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with KTU 2024 standards:**

| Parameter | Proposed | KTU Standard | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zone area | 4.0 sq. km | 0.5 – 2.0 sq. km | ❌ Too large |
| Population/zone | 12,000 | 1,000 – 3,000 | ❌ 4× too high |

**Step 4 — Recommendation — Revised $N_z$:**

$$
N_z^{revised} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{3{,}000} = 400 \text{ zones (maximum threshold)}
$$

$$
N_z^{revised} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{2{,}000} = 600 \text{ zones (recommended middle value)}
$$

**Final Recommendation:** The proposed **100 zones** is grossly inadequate. A revised design of **400–600 zones** is recommended, with an average zone area of **0.67–1.00 sq. km** — which falls within the KTU 2024 standard range.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Cordon Lines** in urban transportation planning. With neat schematics, distinguish between the **External Cordon, Internal Cordon, and Screen Line**. State **two criteria** for selecting each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An External Cordon Survey is planned for a metropolitan city. The expected daily traffic at one intercept station is **10,000 vehicles**. The surveyor wishes to achieve a **95% confidence level with a 5% margin of error**. Calculate the **minimum sample size** required. Also compute the **expansion factor** if the surveyor successfully interviews **400 vehicles** during the **16-hour survey window**.

### **Model Answer (a):**

A **Cordon** is a closed imaginary line drawn around a defined geographical area across which an **Origin-Destination (O-D) survey** is conducted to intercept and classify all vehicular/person movements. Three types of cordons exist:

**1. External Cordon:**
- Encloses the **entire study area**.
- Captures all **external-internal (EI)** and **external-external (EE)** trips.
- **Selection criteria:** (i) Must coincide with a natural/man-made barrier for easy stationing. (ii) Must intersect every major radial corridor entering the study area.

**2. Internal Cordon:**
- Encloses a **sub-area** (typically the CBD) within the study area.
- Captures trips entering/leaving the CBD.
- **Selection criteria:** (i) Diameter should be 3–6 km in a metropolitan city. (ii) Should intersect all major entry roads to the CBD.

**3. Screen Line:**
- A **line** (not closed) that cuts the study area, usually along a barrier.
- Used to **validate the trip-distribution model** by comparing observed vs. modelled crossing volumes.
- **Selection criteria:** (i) Must be along an impermeable barrier (river, railway). (ii) Should divide the study area into roughly equal halves.

*(Examiner's Note: Students should draw all three types in a single city schematic — see SECTION 4.2 for reference diagram. Skipping the sketch costs 1–2 marks.)*

### **Model Answer (b):**

**Given:**
- $N = 10{,}000$ vehicles (ADT)
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence)
- $p = 0.5$
- $e = 0.05$
- Survey interviews collected = 400 vehicles

**Step 1 — Standard sample size (infinite population assumption):**

$$
n_{\infty} = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{(0.05)^2}
$$

$$
n_{\infty} = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.25}{0.0025} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0025}
$$

$$
n_{\infty} = 384.16 \approx 385 \text{ vehicles}
$$

**Step 2 — Finite population correction:**

$$
n_{adj} = \frac{n_{\infty}}{1 + \dfrac{n_{\infty} - 1}{N}} = \frac{385}{1 + \dfrac{384}{10{,}000}}
$$

$$
n_{adj} = \frac{385}{1.0384} = \frac{385}{1.0384} \approx 370.7 \approx 371 \text{ vehicles}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with actual survey (400 vehicles):**

Since $400 > 371$ ✓, the survey is **statistically adequate**.

**Step 4 — Compute Expansion Factor:**

The expansion factor converts the 16-hour surveyed vehicles into 24-hour daily traffic:

$$
EF = \frac{V_{24hr}}{V_{survey}} = \frac{10{,}000}{400}
$$

$$
\boxed{EF = 25}
$$

**Conclusion:** Every surveyed vehicle represents **25 actual daily vehicles** in the population. The O-D data of the 400 surveyed vehicles must be multiplied by 25 to represent the full day's trip pattern.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON THIS TOPIC:**
> 1. **Confusing "zones" with "wards"** — these are NOT the same. Wards = administrative; Zones = analytical. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the finite population correction** in sample-size problems. Always check if $N$ is given; if yes, FPC is mandatory. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up the three cordon types** — examiners often test by asking for "two criteria of each" — students write them generically instead of type-specific. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not drawing a schematic** for cordon questions. A clear labelled diagram is worth 1–2 marks; without it, full marks are rarely awarded.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering justification** in study-area definition — listing 4 steps is only worth 4 marks; the 5th mark requires a "why this step matters" line.
> 6. **Wrong unit usage** in expansion-factor problems — students write "25 vehicles" instead of "25 (dimensionless ratio)" or "25 vehicles/vehicle".

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Study Area** = the geographical canvas of the project. Defined by **Urban Core + 5% Commuter Rule + Growth Corridors + Regional Plan**.
- ✅ **Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ)** = smallest analytical unit. **1,000–3,000 persons/zone**, single land use, single centroid, aligned with census tracts.
- ✅ **Centroid** = ONE per zone, connected to the network via **centroid connectors (load links)**.
- ✅ **External Cordon** = encloses the entire study area; intercepts **EI + EE** trips.
- ✅ **Internal Cordon** = encloses CBD; intercepts **CBD-bound** trips.
- ✅ **Screen Line** = cuts across the study area along a barrier; used for **model validation**.
- ✅ **Standard sample size formula:** $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ with $Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.5$, $e = 0.05$.
- ✅ **FPC formula:** $n_{adj} = \dfrac{n_{\infty}}{1 + (n_{\infty}-1)/N}$ — apply when $N$ is given.
- ✅ **Expansion Factor** $EF = V_{24hr} / V_{survey}$ — convert sample to population.
- ✅ **Cordon selection criteria:** (i) Barrier coincidence, (ii) intercept all radial roads, (iii) impermeable for screen lines.
- ✅ **Zoning design violates** if: pop/zone < 1000 or > 3000, land use not homogeneous, centroid missing, major generator split across zones.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 standard** for Kerala-class cities: **100–500 zones**; 15–40 cordon stations; 2–3 screen lines.
- ✅ **Most-asked concept:** Difference between External Cordon, Internal Cordon, and Screen Line — always answer with a **table + sketch**.
- ✅ **Numerical favourite:** Sample size with FPC and Expansion Factor — practice at least 3 numerical problems before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Techniques and Sample Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET866 - URBAN TRANSPORTATION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sampling Techniques and Sample Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sampling** in the context of Urban Transportation Planning refers to the statistical procedure of selecting a representative subset (called a **sample**) of individuals, households, trips, or zones from a larger **population** (or universe), such that the characteristics observed in the sample can be reliably generalized to estimate the corresponding parameters of the entire population within a known margin of error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**: Sampling is the process of selecting a finite number of units from a large population to estimate the population parameters (mean, variance, proportion) for transportation planning surveys such as **Origin-Destination (O-D) surveys**, **household interview surveys**, and **traffic counts**.

The **Sample Size** is the numerically determined number of sampling units that must be surveyed to achieve a pre-specified level of **statistical confidence** and an acceptable **margin of error** (precision).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to know the average height of all 50,000 students in a university. Measuring every single student would take months. Instead, you randomly pick **200 students**, measure them carefully, and use their average height as a reasonable estimate for everyone.

**The Real-World Analogy**: 

Think of sampling like **tasting a small spoonful of soup** to decide if the whole pot is properly salted. You don't drink the entire pot — you take a small but **representative** spoonful. The key word is **representative** — the spoonful must contain the same mix of ingredients (vegetables, broth, spices) as the entire pot, not just the watery top layer.

In transportation planning, instead of interviewing all 5 million residents of a city about their daily travel behavior (which would be impossibly expensive and time-consuming), we interview **a few thousand carefully selected households** and use their responses to model travel demand for the entire city.

## 1.3 Why Sampling is Critical in Urban Transportation Planning

- **Cost Reduction**: Full-population surveys are prohibitively expensive.
- **Time Efficiency**: O-D surveys of entire cities can take years; sampling reduces this to weeks/months.
- **Data Quality**: Trained enumerators can produce higher-quality data on a smaller sample than poorly trained staff on a massive census.
- **Statistical Reliability**: Properly designed samples yield results with **quantifiable confidence** (e.g., "We are 95% confident that the true mean trip rate lies between X and Y trips/household/day").

## 1.4 Key Terminology Callout

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Statistical Terms You MUST Know**
> - **Population (N)**: The total number of units about which information is desired (e.g., total households in a city).
> - **Sample (n)**: The subset of population actually surveyed.
> - **Parameter**: A numerical characteristic of the POPULATION (true value, usually unknown) — denoted by Greek letters like μ, σ, π.
> - **Statistic**: A numerical characteristic computed from the SAMPLE — denoted by Latin letters like x̄, s, p.
> - **Confidence Level (1 − α)**: The probability that the interval estimate contains the true population parameter (commonly 90%, 95%, or 99%).
> - **Margin of Error (E) / Precision (e)**: The maximum acceptable difference between the sample statistic and the true population parameter.
> - **Standard Deviation (σ)**: A measure of variability/spread in the population.
> - **Standard Error (SE)**: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sampling Techniques

Sampling techniques in transportation planning are broadly classified into **two main categories**:

### A. Non-Probability (Judgemental / Convenience) Sampling
The selection of units is based on the researcher's judgement rather than random chance.
- **Haphazard / Convenience Sampling**: Surveying whoever is conveniently available (e.g., interviewing people at a bus stop).
- **Purposive / Judgement Sampling**: Surveyor selects units believed to be "typical".
- **Drawback**: Results cannot be generalized to the population with known statistical confidence.

### B. Probability (Random) Sampling
Every unit in the population has a **known, non-zero probability** of being selected. This is the **only method** that allows valid statistical inference.

The four principal probability sampling techniques used in urban transportation surveys are:

#### 1. Simple Random Sampling (SRS)
- Every possible sample of size *n* has an **equal probability** of being selected.
- **Procedure**: Assign a unique number to each unit in the population, then use a random number table or random number generator to pick *n* units.
- **Use Case**: Small, homogeneous populations (e.g., selecting 100 intersections from 500 intersections in a city for turning movement counts).

#### 2. Systematic Sampling
- Select every *k*-th unit from an ordered list after a random start.
- **Sampling Interval**: $k = N/n$
- **Procedure**: Choose a random start between 1 and *k*, then pick every *k*-th unit thereafter.
- **Use Case**: Household surveys where addresses are arranged sequentially along streets.
- **Risk**: **Periodicity bias** — if the population has a hidden periodic pattern aligned with *k*, the sample becomes unrepresentative.

#### 3. Stratified Sampling
- The population is divided into mutually exclusive **strata** (sub-groups) based on known characteristics (e.g., income group, zone type, household size), and a **random sample is drawn from each stratum**.
- **Types**:
  - **Proportionate Stratification**: Sample size from each stratum is proportional to its share of population.
  - **Disproportionate Stratification**: Sample size is over-allocated to smaller but more variable strata.
- **Use Case**: Household travel surveys where income strata differ significantly in trip-making behavior.

#### 4. Cluster Sampling
- The population is divided into naturally occurring **clusters** (e.g., city blocks, residential colonies, traffic analysis zones), and **entire clusters** are randomly selected for survey.
- **Use Case**: When a complete list of individuals is unavailable but clusters are easily identifiable; often used in developing countries for O-D surveys.
- **Limitation**: Higher sampling error than SRS of the same size because units within a cluster tend to be similar (intra-cluster correlation).

#### 5. Multi-Stage Sampling (Hierarchical Sampling)
- Sampling is conducted in **multiple stages**, each with progressively smaller units (e.g., Stage 1: select zones → Stage 2: select households → Stage 3: select individuals).
- **Use Case**: Large-scale O-D surveys (e.g., the four-stage process used in Chennai, Mumbai, and Bangalore Comprehensive Mobility Plans).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note**: In Indian urban transportation studies, the **home-interview survey** typically uses **stratified random sampling** stratified by **Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ)**, with the **TAZ itself often selected via cluster or multi-stage sampling**.

## 2.2 Principles of Determining Sample Size

The sample size depends on **three governing parameters**:

| Governing Parameter | Symbol | Effect on n |
|---|---|---|
| Confidence Level (1 − α) | 90%, 95%, 99% | Higher confidence → Larger n |
| Margin of Error / Precision | E | Smaller E → Larger n |
| Population Variability | σ or p | Higher variability → Larger n |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Table 1: Sample Size Formulas for Different Estimators

| **Estimator Type** | **Sample Size Formula** | **When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Estimating a **Mean** $\mu$ | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}{E^{\,2}}$ | When estimating average trip length, average income, etc. |
| Estimating a **Mean** (finite population correction) | $n = \dfrac{N \cdot Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}{(N-1) \cdot E^{\,2} + Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}$ | When *n* > 5% of *N* |
| Estimating a **Proportion** $\pi$ | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{\,2}}$ | When estimating mode share, % of transit users, etc. |
| Estimating a **Proportion** (FPC) | $n = \dfrac{N \cdot Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{(N-1) \cdot E^{\,2} + Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}$ | Finite population proportion estimation |
| **Difference between Two Means** | $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot (\sigma_{1}^{\,2} + \sigma_{2}^{\,2})}{(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2})^{2}}$ | Comparing mean trip rates between two zones/modes |
| **Difference between Two Proportions** | $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2} \cdot [p_{1}(1-p_{1}) + p_{2}(1-p_{2})]}{(p_{1} - p_{2})^{2}}$ | Comparing modal split between two scenarios |
| **Stratified Sample Size** (proportionate) | $n = \dfrac{\left(\sum N_{h} \sigma_{h}\right)^{2}}{N^{2} E^{\,2} / Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} + \sum N_{h} \sigma_{h}^{\,2}}$ | Optimal allocation across strata |
| **Stratified Allocation** (Neyman) | $n_{h} = n \cdot \dfrac{N_{h} \sigma_{h}}{\sum N_{h} \sigma_{h}}$ | Allocating *n* across strata |

### Table 2: Standard Z-Values for Confidence Levels

| **Confidence Level (1 − α)** | **Z_{\alpha/2} Value** |
|---|---|
| 80% | **1.28** |
| 90% | **1.645** |
| 95% | **1.96** |
| 98% | **2.33** |
| 99% | **2.576** |
| 99.9% | **3.29** |

### Table 3: Decision Matrix — Which Sampling Technique to Use?

| **Condition in the Survey** | **Recommended Technique** |
|---|---|
| Population list is available and homogeneous | Simple Random Sampling |
| Population is ordered/list available, want speed | Systematic Sampling |
| Population has distinct sub-groups with different behaviour | Stratified Sampling |
| Complete individual list unavailable; clusters exist | Cluster Sampling |
| Large-scale survey with hierarchical structure | Multi-Stage Sampling |
| Prior information about variability is unavailable | Use conservative estimate $\sigma \approx \frac{R}{6}$ or $p = 0.5$ |

## 2.4 The Finite Population Correction (FPC) Factor

When the sample size *n* exceeds **5% of the population N** ($n/N > 0.05$), the standard formula **overstates** the required sample size. The **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** factor adjusts for this:

$$\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N - n}{N - 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition**: Without FPC, treating a population as infinite when it is actually finite leads to redundant sampling — we keep pulling units that look very similar because the population is small relative to *n*. FPC reduces *n* in such cases.

## 2.5 Practical Considerations in Transportation Surveys

- **Response Rate Adjustment**: Surveyed sample size must be inflated by the expected non-response rate:

$$n_{\text{contacted}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{required}}}{r}$$

where $r$ is the expected response rate (typically 0.7 to 0.9 for home-interview surveys).

- **Variance Estimation for Planning Purposes**: When no prior data is available for $\sigma$, planners often use the **range rule of thumb**:

$$\sigma \approx \dfrac{\text{Range}}{4} \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma \approx \dfrac{\text{Range}}{6}$$

- **Rule for Proportion**: When no prior estimate of $p$ is available, use $p = 0.5$ because $p(1-p)$ is **maximized** at $p = 0.5$, yielding a **conservative (largest) sample size**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The sample size formulas above are used in:
- **Comprehensive Mobility Plans (CMPs)** for Indian cities (MoHUA guidelines recommend ~1.5–2% household sampling for cities > 1 million population).
- **Modal Split Surveys** (determining transit share).
- **Parking Demand Surveys** (estimating occupancy of parking lots).
- **Public Transit Ridership Surveys** (estimating average trip length, passenger km).
- **Before-After Studies** for evaluating transport project impacts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Sample Size for Estimating a Population Mean

### Step 1: Start with the Confidence Interval for the Mean

For a simple random sample of size *n* from a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the sample mean $\bar{x}$ has standard error:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

The $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for $\mu$ is:

$$\bar{x} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \mu \le \bar{x} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### Step 2: Define the Margin of Error

The half-width of the confidence interval is the **margin of error (E)**:

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### Step 3: Solve for n

Multiply both sides by $\sqrt{n}$:

$$E \sqrt{n} = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma$$

Square both sides:

$$E^{\,2} \cdot n = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}$$

Solve for *n*:

$$n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}{E^{\,2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Significance**: This derivation is a **direct exam favourite**. You may be asked to derive the sample size formula starting from the confidence interval — memorize every step.

## 3.2 Derivation of Sample Size for Estimating a Population Proportion

### Step 1: Variance of a Sample Proportion

For a binomial proportion $p$ estimated from sample proportion $\hat{p}$:

$$\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

### Step 2: Confidence Interval for Proportion

$$\hat{p} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}} \le \pi \le \hat{p} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

### Step 3: Margin of Error

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

### Step 4: Solve for n

$$E^{\,2} = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

$$n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{\,2}}$$

## 3.3 Inclusion of the Finite Population Correction (FPC)

### Step 1: Original Standard Error Without FPC

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### Step 2: Standard Error With FPC

When sampling without replacement from a finite population:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{N - n}{N - 1}}$$

### Step 3: Margin of Error With FPC

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{N - n}{N - 1}}$$

### Step 4: Algebraic Rearrangement

Square both sides:

$$E^{\,2} = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma^{\,2}}{n} \cdot \dfrac{N - n}{N - 1}$$

Multiply both sides by $n(N-1)$:

$$E^{\,2} \cdot n \cdot (N - 1) = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2} \cdot (N - n)$$

Expand:

$$E^{\,2} \cdot n \cdot N - E^{\,2} \cdot n = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2} \cdot N - Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2} \cdot n$$

Collect *n* terms on one side:

$$E^{\,2} \cdot n \cdot N + Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2} \cdot n = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2} \cdot N + E^{\,2} \cdot n$$

Wait — let's redo this systematically:

$$n \cdot [E^{\,2}(N - 1) + Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \sigma^{\,2}] = N \cdot Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{n = \dfrac{N \cdot Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}{(N - 1) E^{\,2} + Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem**: A transportation planner wants to estimate the **average household trip rate** (trips per household per day) for a city with **N = 50,000 households**. From a previous pilot study, the standard deviation is known to be $\sigma = 2.5$ trips/household/day. The planner wants the estimate to be within **±0.25 trips** of the true value at the **95% confidence level**.

**Solution Using Table-Formula**:

| Step | Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Identify Z value (95% confidence) | $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$ | 1.96 |
| Identify $\sigma$ | Given | 2.5 |
| Identify E | Given | 0.25 |
| Compute $Z^{\,2} \sigma^{\,2}$ | $(1.96)^{2} \times (2.5)^{2} = 3.8416 \times 6.25$ | 24.01 |
| Compute $E^{\,2}$ | $(0.25)^{2}$ | 0.0625 |
| Compute *n* (infinite population) | $24.01 / 0.0625$ | **384.16 → 385** |
| Check $n/N$ ratio | $385 / 50000 = 0.0077$ | < 0.05 |
| Decision on FPC | Not needed | — |
| Final Sample Size | **n = 385 households** | |

## 3.5 Worked Example for Proportion

**Problem**: A planner wants to estimate the **proportion of commuters using public transit** in a city of **N = 200,000 workers**. From a previous survey, $p = 0.30$. The desired margin of error is **E = 0.04** at **95% confidence**.

**Solution**:

$$n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{\,2}} = \dfrac{(1.96)^{2} \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.04)^{2}}$$

Step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} &= (1.96)^{2} = 3.8416 \\
p(1-p) &= 0.30 \times 0.70 = 0.21 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 3.8416 \times 0.21 = 0.8067 \\
\text{Denominator} &= (0.04)^{2} = 0.0016 \\
n &= \dfrac{0.8067}{0.0016} = 504.19 \rightarrow 505
\end{aligned}$$

Since $n/N = 505/200000 = 0.0025 < 0.05$, FPC is not applied. **Final Sample Size: n ≈ 505 workers**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sampling Techniques

| **Criterion** | **SRS** | **Systematic** | **Stratified** | **Cluster** | **Multi-Stage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Requires complete population list? | **Yes** | Yes | Yes (per stratum) | **No** (only clusters) | No |
| Statistical efficiency | Baseline | Slightly better than SRS | **Highest** (if strata differ) | Lowest (if clusters similar) | Moderate |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate-High | **Lowest** | Moderate |
| Risk of bias | Low | Periodicity bias | Low (if strata well-defined) | High (intra-cluster correlation) | Moderate |
| Ease of execution | Simple | **Very simple** | Moderate | Simple | Complex |
| Use in Indian CMPs | Rare | Common for HH lists | **Most common** | Common for slums/rural | Standard for city-wide |
| Variance reduction achieved | — | Slight | **Major** | May increase | Variable |

## 3.7 Sample Size Adjustment for Non-Response

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{contacted}} &= \dfrac{n_{\text{required}}}{\text{Expected Response Rate}} \\
\text{If } r = 0.80, \quad n_{\text{contacted}} &= \dfrac{385}{0.80} = 482 \text{ households}
\end{aligned}$$

Thus, the planner should approach 482 households to get 385 completed responses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Sampling Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SAMPLING TECHNIQUES] --> B[Probability Sampling]
    A --> C[Non-Probability Sampling]
    
    B --> D[Simple Random Sampling]
    B --> E[Systematic Sampling]
    B --> F[Stratified Sampling]
    B --> G[Cluster Sampling]
    B --> H[Multi-Stage Sampling]
    
    F --> F1[Proportionate]
    F --> F2[Disproportionate]
    
    G --> G1[Single-Stage Cluster]
    G --> G2[Two-Stage Cluster]
    
    H --> H1[Two-Stage]
    H --> H2[Three-Stage]
    H --> H3[Four-Stage]
    
    C --> I[Convenience Sampling]
    C --> J[Judgement Sampling]
    C --> K[Quota Sampling]
    C --> L[Snowball Sampling]
    
    D --> D1[Uses random number tables]
    D --> D2[Every unit equal chance]
    
    E --> E1[Interval k = N divide n]
    E --> E2[Risk of periodicity bias]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#f59e0b,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#10b981,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#10b981,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#10b981,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#10b981,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#10b981,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Workflow of Sample Size Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Population N] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Parameter to Estimate]
    S2 --> S3{Mean or Proportion?}
    S3 -->|Mean| S4[Step 3A: Obtain sigma from pilot study]
    S3 -->|Proportion| S5[Step 3B: Obtain p from prior data]
    
    S4 --> S6[Step 4: Set Confidence Level 1-alpha]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Step 5: Set Margin of Error E]
    S7 --> S8[Step 6: Compute n using Formula]
    
    S8 --> S9{Check n over N ratio}
    S9 -->|n/N greater than 0.05| S10[Step 7: Apply FPC and Recompute n]
    S9 -->|n/N less than 0.05| S11[Step 7: Skip FPC]
    
    S10 --> S12[Step 8: Adjust for Non-Response]
    S11 --> S12
    
    S12 --> S13[Final Sample Size n_final]
    
    style S1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style S13 fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Stratified Sampling Allocation (Neyman Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Population N] --> S1[Stratum 1: N1 sigma1]
    P --> S2[Stratum 2: N2 sigma2]
    P --> S3[Stratum 3: N3 sigma3]
    P --> SN[Stratum h: Nh sigmah]
    
    S1 --> A1[n1 = n times N1 sigma1 over Sum Nh sigmah]
    S2 --> A2[n2 = n times N2 sigma2 over Sum Nh sigmah]
    S3 --> A3[n3 = n times N3 sigma3 over Sum Nh sigmah]
    SN --> AN[nh = n times Nh sigmah over Sum Nh sigmah]
    
    A1 --> SUM[Total: n = sum nh]
    A2 --> SUM
    A3 --> SUM
    AN --> SUM
    
    SUM --> OUT[Optimal Allocation Across Strata]
    
    style P fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff
    style SUM fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Multi-Stage Sampling in a CMP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STG1[STAGE 1: Select TAZs from City] --> STG2[STAGE 2: Select Enumeration Blocks within TAZs]
    STG2 --> STG3[STAGE 3: Select Households within Blocks]
    STG3 --> STG4[STAGE 4: Select Trip Makers within Households]
    STG4 --> SURVEY[Conduct O-D Interview]
    SURVEY --> DATA[Compile Trip Data]
    DATA --> EXPAND[Expand to Population Estimates]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree for Choosing Sampling Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is a complete list of units available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does the population have distinct sub-groups?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Are natural clusters identifiable?}
    
    Q2 -->|Yes| STRAT[Use STRATIFIED SAMPLING]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Is the list in a meaningful order?}
    
    Q4 -->|Yes, no periodicity| SYS[Use SYSTEMATIC SAMPLING]
    Q4 -->|No or random order| SRS[Use SIMPLE RANDOM SAMPLING]
    
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q5{How many stages needed?}
    Q5 -->|One stage| CLUS[Use CLUSTER SAMPLING]
    Q5 -->|Multiple| MULTI[Use MULTI-STAGE SAMPLING]
    
    Q3 -->|No| JUDG[Use JUDGEMENT SAMPLING with caution]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**Q: Differentiate between Simple Random Sampling and Stratified Sampling. State one situation in urban transportation planning where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | Simple Random Sampling | Stratified Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| **Population division** | No sub-groups; treated as one whole | Divided into mutually exclusive strata |
| **Selection** | Every unit has equal chance via random numbers | Random sample drawn independently from each stratum |
| **When useful** | Homogeneous population, e.g., selecting intersections for spot-speed studies | Heterogeneous population, e.g., household trip-rate surveys stratified by income group |
| **Statistical efficiency** | Lower when strata differ | **Higher**, because within-stratum variance is reduced |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for the distinguishing feature, 1 mark for the correct transportation example.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Q: Why is the value p = 0.5 recommended for sample size calculation when no prior estimate of proportion is available?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

1. The variance of a sample proportion $p(1-p)$ is **maximized when p = 0.5**.
2. Using $p = 0.5$ therefore yields the **largest (most conservative) sample size**, ensuring the desired precision is met regardless of the true population proportion.
3. This is a **safe default** used by planners when historical data are unavailable, e.g., during first-time surveys of new urban areas or new transport modes.

*(1 mark each for the three points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for determining the sample size for estimating the **mean of a population** in a transportation survey. Derive the formula starting from the confidence interval expression. State clearly the role of each variable involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A transport planner wishes to estimate the **average daily vehicle-km travelled per household** in a city of **N = 80,000 households**. A pilot survey of 50 households yielded a standard deviation $\sigma = 8$ km. The desired margin of error is **±1.0 km** at **95% confidence**. Determine the required sample size. State whether the finite population correction is necessary. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The sample mean $\bar{x}$ has standard error:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2:** The $(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval is:

$$\bar{x} - Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \mu \le \bar{x} + Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3:** Define margin of error E:

$$E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for *n* by squaring:

$$E^{\,2} = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma^{\,2}}{n}$$

$$\boxed{n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}{E^{\,2}}}$$

**Role of Variables:**

| Variable | Role |
|---|---|
| $Z_{\alpha/2}$ | Critical value from standard normal table for the chosen confidence level |
| $\sigma$ | Population standard deviation (variability measure) |
| $E$ | Desired precision / margin of error |
| $n$ | Required sample size |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $N = 80000$, $\sigma = 8$ km, $E = 1.0$ km, Confidence = 95%

**Step 1: Identify Z value**

$$Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96 \quad \text{(for 95\% confidence)}$$

**Step 2: Compute components**

$$Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} = (1.96)^{2} = 3.8416$$

$$\sigma^{\,2} = (8)^{2} = 64$$

$$E^{\,2} = (1.0)^{2} = 1.0$$

**Step 3: Calculate n (infinite population assumption)**

$$n = \dfrac{3.8416 \times 64}{1.0} = 245.86$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 246 \text{ households}}$$

**Step 4: Check n/N**

$$\dfrac{n}{N} = \dfrac{246}{80000} = 0.00308 = 0.308\%$$

Since $0.308\% < 5\%$, **FPC is NOT necessary**.

**Step 5: Final Answer**

The required sample size is **n = 246 households**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **stratified random sampling** technique as applied to an urban transportation household survey. How is the sample size allocated across strata using the **Neyman allocation** method? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A city has **3 income strata** with the following characteristics. Using **Neyman allocation**, distribute a total sample of **n = 600 households** across the strata.

| Stratum | $N_h$ (households) | $\sigma_h$ (trips/HH/day) |
|---|---|---|
| Low Income | 40,000 | 1.5 |
| Middle Income | 30,000 | 2.5 |
| High Income | 10,000 | 4.0 |

**(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Stratified Sampling Procedure

**1. Definition:** Stratified sampling divides the heterogeneous population into mutually exclusive, internally homogeneous **strata** based on a relevant characteristic (e.g., income, zone type, household size).

**2. Procedure in Transportation Surveys:**
- **Step 1:** Obtain the population frame and identify relevant stratification variables (e.g., monthly household income).
- **Step 2:** Divide the city into strata based on these variables.
- **Step 3:** Determine total sample size *n* using the overall variance formula.
- **Step 4:** Allocate *n* to each stratum using either:
  - **Proportionate allocation**: $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h}{N}$
  - **Neyman allocation**: $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h \sigma_h}{\sum N_h \sigma_h}$
- **Step 5:** Draw a random sample of size $n_h$ from each stratum independently.
- **Step 6:** Conduct surveys and combine stratum estimates using weighted averages.

**3. Neyman Allocation Formula:**

$$n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h \sigma_h}{\sum_{i=1}^{L} N_i \sigma_i}$$

This method maximizes statistical precision for a fixed total *n* by giving larger samples to strata with higher variability (larger $\sigma_h$) and larger population share.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1: Compute $N_h \sigma_h$ for each stratum**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Low Income:} \quad N_1 \sigma_1 &= 40000 \times 1.5 = 60000 \\
\text{Middle Income:} \quad N_2 \sigma_2 &= 30000 \times 2.5 = 75000 \\
\text{High Income:} \quad N_3 \sigma_3 &= 10000 \times 4.0 = 40000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sum the products**

$$\sum N_h \sigma_h = 60000 + 75000 + 40000 = 175000$$

**Step 3: Compute $n_h$ for each stratum**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= 600 \times \dfrac{60000}{175000} = 600 \times 0.3429 = 205.7 \rightarrow \mathbf{206} \\
n_2 &= 600 \times \dfrac{75000}{175000} = 600 \times 0.4286 = 257.1 \rightarrow \mathbf{257} \\
n_3 &= 600 \times \dfrac{40000}{175000} = 600 \times 0.2286 = 137.1 \rightarrow \mathbf{137}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Verify the total**

$$n_1 + n_2 + n_3 = 206 + 257 + 137 = 600 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Allocation:**

| Stratum | $n_h$ (households) | % of Total |
|---|---|---|
| Low Income | 206 | 34.3% |
| Middle Income | 257 | 42.8% |
| High Income | 137 | 22.8% |
| **Total** | **600** | **100%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to Square Variables**: Students frequently write $E$ instead of $E^{\,2}$ in the formula. Always remember the formula comes from **squaring** the margin-of-error equation.
> 2. **Wrong Z-Value**: Using Z = 1.96 (95%) when the question specifies 90% (Z = 1.645). Always re-check the confidence level.
> 3. **FPC Omission**: Students apply the basic formula without checking if $n/N > 0.05$. When population is small, applying FPC is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Not Rounding Up**: Always **round up** the final sample size — sampling 246.1 households is impossible; you must round to 247 or 246.2 → 247 depending on convention, but examiners expect *ceiling*.
> 5. **Stratified Allocation Mistakes**: For Neyman allocation, students sometimes use $N_h / N$ instead of $N_h \sigma_h / \sum N_h \sigma_h$. The latter is correct (Neyman), the former is *proportionate allocation*.
> 6. **No Response Rate Adjustment**: In real surveys, you must adjust for non-response ($n_{\text{contacted}} = n/r$). Examiners may deduct marks if this is omitted in applied problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — MUST KNOW**

- [ ] **Sampling** = selecting a representative subset of a population to estimate parameters.
- [ ] **Probability Sampling** is the **only** method allowing valid statistical inference.
- [ ] Four main techniques: **SRS, Systematic, Stratified, Cluster** (plus Multi-Stage as a hybrid).
- [ ] **Systematic Sampling Interval**: $k = N/n$; risk = **periodicity bias**.
- [ ] **Stratified Sampling** divides population by a relevant variable; reduces variance.
- [ ] **Neyman Allocation**: $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h \sigma_h}{\sum N_h \sigma_h}$.
- [ ] **Cluster Sampling** uses naturally occurring groups; lowest cost, highest variance.
- [ ] **Multi-Stage Sampling** = hierarchical selection of units (used in CMPs).
- [ ] **Sample Size for Mean**: $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma^{\,2}}{E^{\,2}}$.
- [ ] **Sample Size for Proportion**: $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{\,2}}$.
- [ ] **FPC** applies when $n/N > 0.05$; formula: $n = \dfrac{N Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \sigma^{\,2}}{(N-1)E^{\,2} + Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \sigma^{\,2}}$.
- [ ] **Z-values**: 90% → 1.645, 95% → 1.96, 99% → 2.576.
- [ ] **Conservative default** for $p$ (when unknown) = **0.5** (maximizes $p(1-p)$).
- [ ] **Non-response adjustment**: $n_{\text{contacted}} = n_{\text{required}} / r$.
- [ ] **Range rule of thumb** for $\sigma$ when no pilot data: $\sigma \approx \text{Range}/4$ or Range/6.
- [ ] Always **round up** final sample size.
- [ ] Indian CMPs typically sample **1.5–2%** of households for cities > 1 million.
- [ ] **Stratified > SRS > Cluster** in terms of statistical efficiency (when designed well).
- [ ] **Margin of Error E** is the **half-width** of the confidence interval.
- [ ] **Confidence Level** = probability the interval contains the true parameter.
- [ ] For two-sample comparisons, formulas include $(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^{2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Aid for Exam Hall:**
> *"Sampling is to surveys what tasting is to cooking — small, representative, and statistically defensible."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Data and Types of Surveys for Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET866 - URBAN TRANSPORTATION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sources of Data and Types of Surveys for Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Urban Transportation Planning Data** refers to the systematically collected, organized, and analyzed quantitative and qualitative information about travel behavior, transportation infrastructure, land use patterns, demographic characteristics, and economic activities of a study area. This data forms the empirical foundation for all phases of the **4-Stage Urban Transportation Planning (UTP) Model** — Trip Generation, Trip Distribution, Modal Split, and Traffic Assignment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Data collection in urban transportation planning is the systematic process of gathering information through **Primary Surveys** (conducted specifically for the study) and **Secondary Surveys** (derived from existing sources like census, government records, and previous studies). The data ultimately feeds the demand forecasting models.

## 1.2 Primary vs. Secondary Data — The Two Pillars

| Aspect | Primary Data | Secondary Data |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | Data collected originally for the specific study | Data already collected for other purposes |
| **Collection Method** | Surveys, interviews, field measurements | Census, reports, maps, archives |
| **Cost** | High (expensive) | Low (economical) |
| **Time** | Time-consuming | Quick to obtain |
| **Accuracy** | High, study-specific | May not match exact requirements |
| **Examples** | O-D survey, parking survey, speed-delay survey | Census data, master plans, GIS layers |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Doctor's Diagnostic Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Diagnosing a Patient**
> 
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a sick patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor:
> 1. **Asks questions** (interview) — equivalent to **Home Interview Survey** in transportation
> 2. **Runs blood tests & X-rays** (clinical tests) — equivalent to **Traffic Volume Counts & Speed Surveys**
> 3. **Reviews old medical records** (medical history) — equivalent to **Secondary Data** like census records
> 
> Similarly, a transportation planner cannot design a metro line or a flyover "blindly." They must **collect data** about where people travel, why, when, by what mode, and along which routes — just as a doctor must understand the patient's symptoms before treatment.

## 1.4 Classification of Surveys for Transportation Planning

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** classifies transportation surveys into three broad categories:

### A. **Travel Surveys (Demand Side)**
- Home Interview Survey (Origin-Destination Survey)
- Roadside Origin-Destination Survey
- Postcard / Mail-back Survey
- Work Place / Establishment Survey
- Truck / Taxi Survey
- Public Transport Passenger Survey
- Cordon / Screen Line Survey

### B. **Traffic & Infrastructure Surveys (Supply Side)**
- Traffic Volume Count Survey
- Speed and Delay Survey
- Parking Survey
- Inventory of Roadway Facilities
- Intersection Turning Movement Count

### C. **Socio-Economic & Land Use Surveys (Context)**
- Land Use Survey
- Demographic Survey (Population, Employment, Income)
- Economic Survey (GDP, vehicle ownership)

## 1.5 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Definitions for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Trip**: A one-way movement from an origin to a destination for a specific purpose.
> - **Origin (O)**: The location where a trip begins.
> - **Destination (D)**: The location where a trip ends.
> - **Study Area**: The geographic boundary (often the municipal corporation or metropolitan region) within which data is collected.
> - **Cordon Line**: An imaginary boundary around the study area where external-internal trips are intercepted.
> - **Screen Line**: A natural or artificial barrier (river, railway) cutting across the study area used to validate trip distribution models.
> - **Sampling Rate**: The percentage of households or trips actually surveyed (typically **3% to 5%** for urban areas).
> - **Expansion Factor**: The multiplier used to convert sample data to represent the entire population (e.g., if 4% sample → factor = 25).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Survey Methodologies

### 2.1.1 Home Interview Survey (Origin-Destination Survey)

The **gold standard** of urban travel surveys. Conducted at the residence of the household.

**Procedure:**
1. Select a **random sample** of households (3–5% in large cities, up to 10% in small towns).
2. Trained enumerators visit homes and conduct face-to-face interviews.
3. Each household member (above 5 years) reports **all trips made on the previous day**.
4. Data collected: Trip purpose, mode, origin, destination, departure time, arrival time, cost.

**Merits:**
- Most accurate and comprehensive
- Captures socio-economic variables (income, vehicle ownership, family size)

**Demerits:**
- Very expensive and time-consuming
- Respondent fatigue biases long reporting periods
- Difficult in high-rise apartments and gated communities

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Home Interview Surveys form the basis of the **Conventional Urban Transportation Planning (CUTP) Process** adopted by the **Town and Country Planning Organisation (TCPO)**, Government of India.

### 2.1.2 Roadside Origin-Destination Survey

Conducted at the **cordon line** by intercepting vehicles.

**Procedure:**
1. Station enumerators at major entry/exit points.
2. Stop a sample of vehicles (cars, trucks, buses).
3. Record vehicle type, occupancy, origin, destination, and trip purpose.

**Merits:**
- Useful for **external-internal trips**
- Cheaper than home interview
- Captures through-traffic

**Demerits:**
- Causes traffic delays
- Cannot capture internal-int ernal trips

### 2.1.3 Postcard / Mail-Back Survey

Survey forms are distributed at workplaces, shopping centers, or mailed to households.

**Merits:**
- Very economical
- Wide geographic coverage

**Demerits:**
- **Low response rate (typically 15–30%)** — major limitation
- Self-selection bias

### 2.1.4 Cordon and Screen Line Surveys

- **Cordon Line**: Imaginary boundary enclosing the study area; all trips crossing it are surveyed.
- **Screen Line**: A physical line (river, rail corridor) dividing the study area; comparison of counts on both sides validates trip distribution.

### 2.1.5 Other Key Surveys

| Survey Type | Purpose |
|-------------|---------|
| **Traffic Volume Count** | Measure vehicles/hour on a road |
| **Speed & Delay Survey** | Measure journey speed, identify bottlenecks |
| **Parking Survey** | Parking demand, duration, accumulation |
| **Inventory Survey** | Road width, pavement type, intersections |
| **Land Use Survey** | Zonal characteristics (residential, commercial) |
| **Demographic Survey** | Population, employment, income distribution |

## 2.2 Sampling Theory for Transportation Surveys

### 2.2.1 Determination of Sample Size

The sample size is critical to ensure statistical reliability.

**Formula for Infinite Population:**
$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

Where:
- $n_0$ = sample size (initial)
- $Z$ = Z-score (e.g., **1.96** for 95% confidence)
- $p$ = estimated proportion (use **0.5** for maximum sample size)
- $e$ = acceptable error (typically **0.05** or 5%)

**Finite Population Correction:**
$$
n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{n_0 - 1}{N}}
$$

Where $N$ = total population of study area.

**For Transportation Engineering (KTU Standard):**
$$
n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{e^2}
$$

Where $\sigma$ = standard deviation of trip rates.

### 2.2.2 Expansion Factor

$$
\text{Expansion Factor (EF)} = \frac{\text{Total Population (or Households)}}{\text{Sample Size}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** If a city has 100,000 households and 4,000 are surveyed, **EF = 25**. Every trip recorded in the sample represents 25 trips in reality.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Unit | Application |
|-------------------|----------------------|------|-------------|
| **Sample Size (Infinite)** | $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | households | Initial estimate |
| **Sample Size (Finite)** | $n = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0-1}{N}}$ | households | Adjusted estimate |
| **Expansion Factor** | $EF = \dfrac{N}{n}$ | dimensionless | Scaling up sample |
| **Z-score (95% confidence)** | $Z = 1.96$ | unitless | Standard normal table |
| **Mean Trip Rate** | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{n}$ | trips/household/day | Trip Generation |
| **Sampling Rate** | $SR = \dfrac{n}{N} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Survey planning |
| **Traffic Volume (PCU)** | $V = \sum (V_i \times PCU_i)$ | PCU/hour | Capacity analysis |
| **Journey Speed** | $S = \dfrac{L}{T}$ | km/hr | Speed-delay survey |
| **Parking Accumulation** | $A = \sum_{t=1}^{n} P_t$ | vehicles | Parking demand |
| **Average Parking Duration** | $D = \dfrac{\sum (t_{out} - t_{in})}{n}$ | hours | Parking survey |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application:**
> - **CUTP Studies (India):** All major cities — Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Bangalore — have undergone home interview surveys as part of Comprehensive Mobility Plans (CMPs).
> - **GIS Integration:** Modern surveys use GPS-enabled tablets, smartphone apps (e.g., **MOVE** by Uber), and GIS mapping for real-time data.
> - **Big Data Revolution:** Cities now supplement surveys with **passenger data from metro smart cards, mobile phone CDRs (Call Detail Records), and floating car data (FCD)** from taxi aggregators like Ola and Uber.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1: Sample Size Calculation (KTU Board Favorite)

### Problem Statement
> A transportation planner is conducting a home interview survey for a city with **50,000 households**. The desired confidence level is **95%**, the estimated proportion of trip-makers is **0.40**, and the acceptable error is **5%**. Calculate the required sample size with finite population correction.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the parameters**
- Population, $N = 50{,}000$ households
- Confidence level = 95% → $Z = 1.96$
- Estimated proportion, $p = 0.40$
- Acceptable error, $e = 0.05$

**Step 2: Calculate the initial sample size (infinite population)**
$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

Substituting values:
$$
n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.40 \cdot (1 - 0.40)}{(0.05)^2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute each component**
$$
(1.96)^2 = 3.8416
$$
$$
0.40 \cdot 0.60 = 0.24
$$
$$
(0.05)^2 = 0.0025
$$

**Step 4: Final calculation of $n_0$**
$$
n_0 = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.24}{0.0025} = \frac{0.921984}{0.0025} = 368.79 \approx 369
$$

**Step 5: Apply Finite Population Correction**
$$
n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{n_0 - 1}{N}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{369}{1 + \frac{369 - 1}{50{,}000}} = \frac{369}{1 + \frac{368}{50{,}000}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{369}{1 + 0.00736} = \frac{369}{1.00736} = 366.30 \approx 367
$$

**Step 6: Final Answer**
> The planner must survey **367 households** (rounded up for safety).

**Step 7: Calculate the Expansion Factor**
$$
EF = \frac{N}{n} = \frac{50{,}000}{367} \approx 136.24
$$

> Every household surveyed represents approximately **136 households** in the city.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2: Sampling Rate Verification

### Problem Statement
> For a metropolitan area with **1,000,000 households**, a sample of **4,000 households** is to be surveyed. Compute the **sampling rate** and comment on its adequacy as per Indian Standard practices (recommended: 3% to 5% for large cities).

### Solution

**Sampling Rate:**
$$
SR = \frac{n}{N} \times 100 = \frac{4{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 0.4\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Board Insight:** A sampling rate of **0.4%** is **inadequate** for a metropolitan area of 1 million households. As per standard Indian practice, large cities require **3% to 5%** sampling. The current rate would lead to **high statistical error** and unreliable forecasts.

**Recommended Sample Size:**
$$
n_{recommended} = 0.04 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ households}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Survey Selection Decision Framework

| Survey Type | Best Used For | Sample Size Guidance | Cost (Relative) | Data Quality |
|-------------|---------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Home Interview | Internal trips, all modes | 3–5% (large cities) | Very High | Excellent |
| Roadside O-D | External-internal trips | 10–20% of vehicles | Moderate | Good |
| Postcard / Mail | Wide coverage, low budget | 10–15% response expected | Low | Moderate |
| Work Place | Work trips, peak hours | 30–50% of establishments | Moderate | Good |
| Truck / Taxi | Freight, commercial | 10–25% of fleet | High | Good |
| Public Transport | Bus/metro passengers | 5–10% of commuters | Moderate | Good |
| Cordon Line | External trips | At all entry points | High | Excellent |
| Screen Line | Model validation | Continuous count stations | Moderate | Excellent |

## 3.4 Detailed Data Flow in Transportation Planning

$$
\text{Surveys} \rightarrow \text{Raw Data} \rightarrow \text{Coding} \rightarrow \text{Validation} \rightarrow \text{Expansion} \rightarrow \text{Zonal Aggregation} \rightarrow \text{Model Input}
$$

> **Step-by-Step Logic:**
> 1. **Surveys** generate raw trip records.
> 2. **Coding** assigns zone numbers, mode codes, purpose codes.
> 3. **Validation** checks for errors (e.g., impossible trips, missing data).
> 4. **Expansion** applies the expansion factor to scale up.
> 5. **Zonal Aggregation** sums trips by Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ).
> 6. **Model Input** feeds the trip generation, distribution, and modal split models.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
