topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET866 - URBAN TRANSPORTATION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Land Use Transportation Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Land Use Transportation Interaction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Land Use Transportation Interaction (LUTI)** is the bidirectional, dynamic, and often self-reinforcing relationship between the spatial distribution of urban activities (residential, commercial, industrial, recreational, institutional) and the movement of people, goods, and information across the transport network. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, it is treated as a **co-evolutionary system** in which land-use decisions (location, density, mix, and intensity) generate travel demand, while transport supply (capacity, accessibility, connectivity, and level-of-service) reshapes locational attractiveness, property values, and ultimately, the future land-use pattern.

The two-way causation is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{Land Use} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Transport}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "Land Use Transport Interaction is a feedback mechanism in which land-use patterns determine trip generation, distribution, modal split, and route assignment, while the transport system's level of service, in turn, influences the locational attractiveness, accessibility, and densification of land use, producing a continuous equilibrium-seeking cycle."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The City as a Living Organism"

Imagine a city as a **giant human body**:
- **Land use** = the **organs** (heart = CBD/commercial zone, lungs = parks/open spaces, arteries = residential zones, kidneys = industrial belts that filter waste).
- **Transportation** = the **circulatory system** (blood = vehicles/trips, veins/arteries = roads, heartbeat = peak-hour demand).
- Just as blood flow is dictated by where organs are placed, **trip patterns** are dictated by where activities are located. Conversely, when a new road is built (a "bypass" in the body), the surrounding organs grow denser because "oxygen" (accessibility) increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> If you build a metro station in a barren field, within 5–10 years you will see high-rise apartments, malls, and offices around it. The transport facility *attracted* the land use. Conversely, if you build a hospital, school, and IT park in a suburban area without good roads, the area will underperform because accessibility is poor — the land use *demanded* transport.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in LUTI Analysis

| Metric | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Population Density** | Persons per hectare (pph) or persons per km² | Drives trip generation |
| **Floor Space Index (FSI / FAR)** | Ratio (dimensionless) | Intensity of built form |
| **Accessibility Index (Hansen)** | Dimensionless score | Ease of reaching opportunities |
| **Trip Rate** | Trips per hectare / per dwelling unit / per employee | Land-use productivity |
| **Trip Length Frequency Distribution** | km | Spatial dispersion of activities |
| **Land Use Transport Equilibrium (LUTE)** | Iterative convergence criterion | Long-run balance point |
| **Modal Share** | Percentage (%) | Implication for road congestion |
| **Vehicle Kilometers Travelled (VKT)** | veh-km/day | Network performance indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects you to know the **causality direction** (which causes which), the **feedback cycle**, and the role of **accessibility** as the central mediator. The **Hansen Index** of accessibility is the most-tested formula under this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LUTI Feedback Cycle — Step-by-Step Logic

The interaction is best understood as a **closed-loop system** with four distinct stages that occur over different time horizons.

### Step 1 — Land Use Sets the Stage (Long-Term, 5–30 years)
- Zoning regulations decide *where* residential, commercial, and industrial activities can occur.
- Density norms (FSI caps, building heights) decide *how intense* each plot becomes.
- Mixing of uses determines *internal capture* of trips (walkable, mixed-use vs. segregated, car-dependent).

> **Why?** Because every activity has a "trip-production potential." A residential building of 100 units generates a predictable number of person-trips per day.

### Step 2 — Land Use Generates Travel Demand (Short-Term, daily)
- Using **trip generation models** (cross-classification, regression, ITE rates), trips are estimated.
- Trip ends feed the **trip distribution, modal split, and assignment** stages (the classical four-step model).
- Result: peak-hour congestion, load on road/rail, OD matrices.

> **How?** Through empirical relationships of the form:
> Trips = f(Density, Mix, Connectivity, Socio-economics)

### Step 3 — Transport Alters Accessibility (Medium-Term, 1–10 years)
- The completed transport network modifies **generalized cost** (time + money + comfort) between zones.
- Hansen (1959) formalized **accessibility** as a spatial opportunity measure.
- Areas near new metro stations, ring roads, or expressways experience **accessibility boost**.

### Step 4 — Accessibility Re-shapes Land Use (Long-Term feedback)
- Higher accessibility → higher land rent → densification → change in land use.
- The cycle restarts, often producing **self-reinforcing agglomeration** (e.g., CBD growth) or **decentralization** (e.g., edge cities).

> [!NOTE]
> **Central Theorem of LUTI:**
> *"Transport improvements reduce the cost of spatial interaction; the savings are capitalized into land values, which in turn attract denser and often different land uses, regenerating new travel demand."*
> This is the *Mohring–Mitchell* capitalization principle, often quoted by KTU examiners.

## 2.2 Directional Causality — Three Schools of Thought

| School | Direction of Causality | Implication for Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport-Led (Top-Down)** | Transport causes Land Use | Build infrastructure first, growth follows (Singapore's MRT) |
| **Land-Use-Led (Bottom-Up)** | Land Use causes Transport | Master-plan land uses, transport adapts (post-war UK new towns) |
| **Integrated / Co-Evolution** | Both directions, simultaneous | Use LUT models (UrbanSim, MATSim, MEPLAN, TRANUS) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** To preserve markdown table integrity, all absolute-value and conditioning bars are written using `\vert` and `\mid` rather than the unescaped pipe symbol.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | **Hansen Accessibility Index** — zone $i$'s access to opportunities $E_j$ weighted by an impedance function $f(C_{ij})$ of cost $C_{ij}$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $A_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{E_j}{C_{ij}^{\beta}}$ | **Hansen Power Form** — most commonly used impedance; $\beta$ is the sensitivity parameter | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $T_{ij} = K \cdot P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | **Hansen Gravity-like Opportunity Model** — trips from $i$ to $j$ proportional to population at $i$, opportunities at $j$, and inverse cost | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $\text{Trip Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Total Trips}}{\text{Acres (or DU, or Employees)}}$ | Land-use productivity of trip production / attraction | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $D(x) = D_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda x}$ | **Clark's Negative Exponential Density Gradient** — population density falls exponentially with distance $x$ from CBD; $D_0$ is CBD density, $\lambda$ is density gradient | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $\ln D(x) = \ln D_0 - \lambda x$ | **Linearized form of Clark** for regression fitting | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $LUE = \dfrac{\text{Total Trips Generated}}{\text{Total Area (hectares)}}$ | **Land Use Efficiency** indicator | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $\eta_{cap} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{land}}{\Delta C_{access}}$ | **Capitalization Elasticity** — % change in land price per % change in accessibility | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $\text{VKT} = \sum_{i,j} T_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}$ | Vehicle Kilometers Travelled across the network | ⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{trips}}{A_{zone}}$ | **Trip Density** (trips per unit area) — direct measure of land-use transport intensity | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | $M_{ij} = \dfrac{T_{ij}}{\sum_{k} T_{ik}}$ | **Modal share** between modes $i$ and $j$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 12 | $A_{gravity} = \sum_{j} \dfrac{P_j}{(C_{ij})^{\beta}}$ | **Gravity-based cumulative accessibility** (used in Lowry model) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Town & Country Planning:** Quantifying how a new ring road or metro corridor will reshape the city.
- **Traffic Impact Assessment (TIA):** Estimating trips from a proposed mall/IT park.
- **Land Value Capture (LVC):** Recovering project cost from property-value uplift caused by transit.
- **Smart-Growth & TOD (Transit-Oriented Development):** Engineering the feedback loop *intentionally* to reduce VKT.
- **Climate & Energy Policy:** Estimating emissions reduction from compact, mixed-use development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Questions of 7–14 marks frequently demand *derivation* of the Hansen index, *interpretation* of the Clark density gradient, and *application* to a small numerical example (3–4 zones with given cost matrix).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hansen Accessibility Index

**Starting Premise (Hansen, 1959):**
Accessibility of zone $i$ is the *ease* with which the residents of $i$ can reach spatially distributed opportunities (jobs, shops, schools, etc.).

### Step A — Define the Opportunity Set
Let there be $n$ zones. Zone $j$ contains $E_j$ opportunities (e.g., number of jobs). The maximum accessibility, ignoring all friction, is simply the **sum of all opportunities**:

$$
A_{i,\max} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j
$$

### Step B — Introduce the Impedance Function
In reality, reaching a distant zone is harder. Multiply each opportunity by a *decay function* $f(C_{ij})$, where $C_{ij}$ is the **generalized cost** (travel time, distance, or composite) between $i$ and $j$. The function is bounded:

$$
0 \;\le\; f(C_{ij}) \;\le\; 1, \quad f(0)=1, \quad f(\infty)=0
$$

> **Textual Explanation of Step B:**
> We are saying "every opportunity counts, but the further away it is, the less 'accessible' it feels." When cost is zero, the function equals 1 (full access). When cost is infinite, the function equals 0 (no access).

### Step C — Formulate the Hansen Index
The accessibility of zone $i$ is therefore the *opportunity-weighted, friction-discounted* sum:

$$
A_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j \cdot f(C_{ij})
$$

### Step D — Choose a Parametric Form (Power Function)
The most widely used decay function in KTU exams is the power form, which gives the famous **Hansen Power Index**:

$$
f(C_{ij}) \;=\; \frac{1}{C_{ij}^{\beta}}, \quad \beta \ge 0
$$

Substituting:

$$
A_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{E_j}{C_{ij}^{\beta}}
$$

### Step E — Interpret the Sensitivity Parameter $\beta$
- $\beta = 0$ → accessibility equals total opportunities (no friction).
- $\beta = 1$ → opportunity weighted by inverse distance (typical urban).
- $\beta = 2$ → strong distance decay (typical inter-city, walking).

> **Why this matters:** Planners tune $\beta$ to represent the *transport mode*. A city with poor transit and high car dependence has a *steeper* $\beta$ because people can only tolerate short travel times for daily activities.

### Step F — Normalized Form (Optional, often asked)
To compare zones on a uniform 0–1 scale, normalize by the maximum possible:

$$
A_i^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \frac{A_i}{A_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{E_j}{C_{ij}^{\beta}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The Hansen index is essentially a *gravity-style potential model*. It is **monotonically increasing** in the number of opportunities and **monotonically decreasing** in transport cost — capturing the bidirectional LUTI feedback in a single equation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical, 4-Zone System)

> **KTU-style question pattern:** "Compute the Hansen accessibility of zone 1 using the data below. Use $\beta = 2$."

**Given:**

| Zone $j$ | $E_j$ (jobs) | $C_{1j}$ (mins) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5000 | 5 |
| 2 | 3000 | 15 |
| 3 | 2000 | 30 |
| 4 | 1000 | 50 |

**Solution — Step by Step:**

$$
A_1 \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{4} \frac{E_j}{C_{1j}^{2}}
$$

Compute each term (one blank line separation per display block as required by protocol):

$$
A_1 \;=\; \frac{5000}{5^{2}} + \frac{3000}{15^{2}} + \frac{2000}{30^{2}} + \frac{1000}{50^{2}}
$$

$$
A_1 \;=\; \frac{5000}{25} + \frac{3000}{225} + \frac{2000}{900} + \frac{1000}{2500}
$$

$$
A_1 \;=\; 200.00 + 13.33 + 2.22 + 0.40
$$

$$
A_1 \;=\; 215.95 \;\; \text{opportunity-units}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Zone 1 has strong within-zone employment (5000 jobs) and modest reach to zone 2. Its Hansen index is dominated by *internal* opportunities because the inverse-square cost function aggressively penalizes long trips. **Planning implication:** To raise $A_1$ substantially, the city must either (a) create more jobs in nearby zones, or (b) cut travel time to zones 3 and 4 (a metro or BRT corridor).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Clark Negative Exponential Density Gradient

### Step A — Empirical Observation
For many cities (Chicago, London, Kolkata, Kochi), the population density $D$ at distance $x$ km from the CBD decays smoothly.

### Step B — Assume a Negative Exponential Form
The simplest differentiable, monotonically decreasing, non-negative function satisfying $D(0) = D_0$ is:

$$
D(x) \;=\; D_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda x}, \quad \lambda > 0
$$

### Step C — Linearize for Empirical Calibration
Take the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln D(x) \;=\; \ln D_0 - \lambda x
$$

This is a **straight line** when plotting $\ln D(x)$ against $x$. The intercept gives $\ln D_0$ and the slope gives $-\lambda$. Using OLS regression on census data, both parameters are estimated.

### Step D — Interpret $\lambda$
- Small $\lambda$ → city is "flat" (e.g., Los Angeles, low-density sprawl).
- Large $\lambda$ → city is "steep" (e.g., Hong Kong, high-density concentration).

> **Why this matters for transport:** $\lambda$ directly governs **trip length**. A steep city has short average trip lengths (high transit viability); a flat city has long trips (high car dependency).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of LUTI Models

| Model | Year | Land-Use Variable | Transport Variable | Equilibrium? | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hansen** | 1959 | Opportunities $E_j$ | Generalized cost $C_{ij}$ | One-shot (not iterative) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Lowry (Garin-Lowry)** | 1964 | Basic + retail population | Gravity model | Static equilibrium | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Putman** | 1970s | Households + jobs | Disaggregate mode | Iterative | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **MEPLAN** | 1978 | Floorspace, rents | Multi-modal | Spatial input-output | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **TRANUS** | 1982 | Activities, floorspace | Multi-modal | Joint equilibrium | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **UrbanSim** | 2000s | Households, jobs, parcels | Activity-based | Dynamic | ⭐⭐ |
| **MATSim** | 2000s | Agents | Multi-modal | Co-evolutionary | ⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected:** Students should be able to draw a **flowchart of the Lowry model** in the exam, show the iteration between *residential allocation* and *retail employment*, and explain how it converges to a **basic–non-basic** equilibrium.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Lowry Model Allocation (1 Iteration)

> **Setup:** Two zones, total basic employment $B_1 = 1000$, $B_2 = 0$. Cost matrix $C_{ij} = \vert i-j \vert \cdot 10$ min. $\beta = 1$. Each household has 1 worker.

**Step 1 — Initial accessibility:**

$$
A_i^{(0)} \;=\; \sum_{j} \frac{B_j}{C_{ij}}
$$

For $i=1$: $A_1 = 1000/0 + 0/10 = 1000$. For $i=2$: $A_2 = 1000/10 + 0/0 = 100$.

> *(Note: $C_{ii}=0$ requires a small in-zone friction $\epsilon$ in practice; we use it as a formal limit.)*

**Step 2 — Allocate total population $P = 4000$ proportional to accessibility:**

$$
P_1^{(1)} = P \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_1+A_2} = 4000 \cdot \frac{1000}{1100} = 3636
$$

$$
P_2^{(1)} = 4000 \cdot \frac{100}{1100} = 364
$$

**Step 3 — Generate non-basic (retail) employment** at ratio $r = 0.3$:

$$
NB_1 = 0.3 \cdot P_1^{(1)} = 1091, \quad NB_2 = 0.3 \cdot P_2^{(1)} = 109
$$

**Step 4 — Re-allocate** using $B_j^{new} = B_j + NB_j$ and iterate until convergence.

> **Insight:** Even with $B_2 = 0$, zone 2 still attracts ~9% of population because it is the *only* alternative. The model captures the *agglomeration feedback* elegantly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The LUTI Feedback Cycle (Mermaid Flow)

> The following Mermaid block uses alphanumeric node IDs (lowercase, prefixed with letters) and clean uppercase labels — no markdown formatting or reserved keywords inside node text — to compile reliably.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1L["LAND USE POLICY: ZONING, FSI, MIX"] --> n2T["GENERATED TRIP ENDS: PROD ATTR"]
    n2T --> n3D["DEMAND MATRICES: OD, MODE, ROUTE"]
    n3D --> n4N["NETWORK PERFORMANCE: VKT, LOS, CONGESTION"]
    n4N --> n5A["ACCESSIBILITY HANSEN INDEX"]
    n5A --> n6P["LAND RENT AND PROPERTY VALUE"]
    n6P --> n7R["REZONING DENSIFICATION AND INVESTMENT"]
    n7R --> n1L
    n4N --> n8E["EXTERNALITIES: EMISSIONS NOISE CRASHES"]
    n8E --> n9G["GOVERNANCE RESPONSE: REGULATION TOLL"]
    n9G --> n1L
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Start at `n1L` (Land Use Policy) and follow the outer loop. The cycle restarts continuously.
- The inner loop through `n8E` (Externalities) and `n9G` (Governance Response) is the *societal correction mechanism* — congestion, pollution, and safety problems trigger policy that feeds back to land use.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Integrated LUT Model

> This block diagram represents the typical **MEPLAN / TRANUS** style integration. It is preferred over physical drawings because it conveys data flow precisely.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nLU["LAND USE SUB-MODEL: Floorspace, Households, Jobs, Activities"] --> nG1["INTERFACE 1: Activity Locations and Intensities"]
    nG1 --> nTR["TRANSPORT SUB-MODEL: Multimodal Network, LOS, Costs"]
    nTR --> nG2["INTERFACE 2: Skim Matrices, Travel Times, Utilities"]
    nG2 --> nLU
    nTR --> nPR["PERFORMANCE METRICS: VKT, VHT, Emissions, Accessibility"]
    nPR --> nPL["PLANNER DECISION: Pricing, Investment, Regulation"]
    nPL --> nLU
    nPL --> nTR
```

**Interpretation:**
- The two sub-models (land use and transport) exchange information *iteratively*.
- The planner observes performance metrics and adjusts either sub-model's inputs.
- This is the canonical **joint equilibrium** architecture examined at KTU.

---

## 4.3 Hierarchy of LUTI Decisions (Time-Scale Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nS["STRATEGIC LAYER: 20 to 30 YEARS: Master Plan, Ring Road, Metro Spine"]
    nT["TACTICAL LAYER: 5 to 10 YEARS: Corridor BRT, TOD Zones, FSI Hike"]
    nO["OPERATIONAL LAYER: 1 to 3 YEARS: Signal Plans, Bus Frequency, Parking"]
    nS --> nT --> nO
```

> **Engineering Takeaway:** The slower the time horizon, the more powerful the lever. A master-plan corridor can out-perform ten years of signal optimization. This is why KTU emphasizes **strategic** LUTI reasoning.

---

## 4.4 Cause-Effect Sub-Model (Microscopic View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA["Density Increase"] --> nB["More Daily Trips per Hectare"]
    nB --> nC["Higher Peak Demand"]
    nC --> nD["Congestion Cost Rises"]
    nD --> nE["Some Trips Shift Mode to Transit Walk"]
    nE --> nF["VKT per Capita Falls"]
    nF --> nG["If Transit is Available, Otherwise Sprawl Continues"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Cue:** When asked *"How does densification affect VKT?"*, this is the canonical causal chain. Mark allocation typically awards 1 mark for each correct arrow plus a concluding line of interpretation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**Define Land Use Transportation Interaction. State any two key metrics used to quantify this interaction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Land Use Transportation Interaction is a bidirectional, dynamic feedback process in which the spatial distribution of urban activities (residential, commercial, industrial) generates travel demand, while the transport system's level of service and accessibility re-shapes the locational attractiveness and density of those activities over time.

**Two metrics (1 mark):**
1. **Hansen Accessibility Index** — opportunity-weighted measure of spatial reach.
2. **Trip Rate** (trips per hectare or per dwelling unit) — direct empirical coupling of land use to travel demand.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Floor Space Index, Modal Share, Vehicle Kilometres Travelled, Clark Density Gradient parameter $\lambda$.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between transport-led growth and land-use-led growth with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transport-Led Growth | Land-Use-Led Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **Causality direction** | Transport infrastructure drives land-use change | Master-planned land use dictates transport demand |
| **Mechanism** | Accessibility boost → land rent ↑ → densification | Zoning / FSI cap → trip generation → transport supply adapts |
| **Example** | **Singapore MRT** — metro corridors preceded and *attracted* residential towers | **Chandigarh / Islamabad** — Le Corbusier's grid plan and sectoral land-use predetermined the road hierarchy |
| **Risk** | Induced sprawl if not paired with zoning | Congestion if transport lags land-use allocation |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two alternatives are offered; students answer *either*. Each alternative carries **7 + 7 marks** structured as sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S8 B.Tech Civil]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Hansen Accessibility Index**. Derive its mathematical form starting from the concept of opportunity-weighted spatial friction. State any two assumptions of the model.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A city has **three zones** with the following data. Compute the Hansen Accessibility Index of **Zone 1** using the power impedance function with $\beta = 1.5$. Comment on which zone contributes most to Zone 1's accessibility and why.

| Zone $j$ | Opportunities $E_j$ (jobs) | Travel time $C_{1j}$ (min) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6000 | 4 |
| 2 | 3500 | 12 |
| 3 | 1500 | 25 |

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) Theory of the Hansen Accessibility Index

**Step 1 — Concept (1 mark):**
Accessibility of a zone is the *ease* with which the residents of that zone can reach opportunities (jobs, schools, services) located elsewhere in the city.

**Step 2 — Mathematical formulation (3 marks):**

$$
A_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j \cdot f(C_{ij})
$$

where $A_i$ is the accessibility of zone $i$, $E_j$ is the number of opportunities in zone $j$, $C_{ij}$ is the generalized cost of travel from $i$ to $j$, and $f(C_{ij})$ is the impedance function satisfying $f(0)=1$ and $f(\infty)=0$.

**Step 3 — Power impedance (2 marks):**
Adopting the power form $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-\beta}$:

$$
A_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{E_j}{C_{ij}^{\beta}}
$$

The parameter $\beta \ge 0$ controls the strength of distance decay: larger $\beta$ implies people are less willing to travel.

**Step 4 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Opportunities are **lumpy and countable** (jobs, households).
2. Travel cost $C_{ij}$ is **symmetric and isotropic** (same in both directions, direction-independent).

*(Acceptable: 3. People maximize accessibility / minimize generalized cost; 4. Static single-period analysis.)*

#### (b) Numerical Solution

Compute each term with $\beta = 1.5$:

$$
A_1 \;=\; \frac{6000}{4^{1.5}} + \frac{3500}{12^{1.5}} + \frac{1500}{25^{1.5}}
$$

Compute the powers:

$$
4^{1.5} = 8.00, \quad 12^{1.5} \approx 41.57, \quad 25^{1.5} = 125.00
$$

Substitute and evaluate:

$$
A_1 \;=\; \frac{6000}{8.00} + \frac{3500}{41.57} + \frac{1500}{125.00}
$$

$$
A_1 \;=\; 750.00 + 84.20 + 12.00
$$

$$
A_1 \;=\; 846.20 \;\; \text{opportunity-units}
$$

**Contribution Analysis (1 mark):**
- Zone 1 → **750.00 (88.6%)** dominates.
- Zone 2 → **84.20 (9.95%)**.
- Zone 3 → **12.00 (1.42%)**.

**Comment (1 mark):** The bulk of Zone 1's accessibility comes from *internal* opportunities because the inverse-power impedance heavily penalizes long trips. Therefore, increasing jobs in Zone 1 itself is the most cost-effective lever; improving transit to Zone 2 would yield the *next* biggest gain.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, S8 B.Tech Civil]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flowchart, describe the **Land-Use Transport (LUT) feedback cycle**. Identify and explain any **four links** of the cycle with engineering examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A metropolitan region has a **Clark density gradient** parameter $\lambda = 0.08$ per km and a CBD density $D_0 = 25000$ persons per km². Calculate:
1. The population density at 8 km from the CBD.
2. The distance at which the population density falls to 5000 persons per km².
3. The **average density** within a 10-km radius of the CBD.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) LUT Feedback Cycle — Flowchart & Explanation

**Flowchart (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nA["ZONING AND LAND USE ALLOCATION"] --> nB["DENSITY AND MIX OF ACTIVITIES"]
    nB --> nC["TRIP GENERATION AND DISTRIBUTION"]
    nC --> nD["TRANSPORT NETWORK PERFORMANCE"]
    nD --> nE["ACCESSIBILITY CHANGES"]
    nE --> nF["LAND VALUES AND LOCATIONAL CHOICE"]
    nF --> nA
```

**Four Key Links with Examples (4 × 1 mark):**

| # | Link | Explanation | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zoning → Density | Floor Space Index caps and use-mix rules control how many people/jobs occupy a hectare. | Kochi Smart City zone with FSI 4 vs. suburban FSI 1.5 |
| 2 | Density → Trip Generation | Higher density → more trip-ends per hectare, raising peak demand. | IT parks in Technopark, Trivandrum generate ~0.8 trips/employee in peak hour |
| 3 | Trip Generation → Network Performance | Aggregated trips load the road/rail, producing congestion and LOS degradation. | Peak-hour crush-load on Ernakulam–Thrissur corridor |
| 4 | Network Performance → Accessibility | Faster, higher-LOS transport (e.g., metro) increases Hansen index of nearby zones. | Kochi Metro boosting accessibility of MG Road and Aluva |
| 5 | Accessibility → Land Values | Capitalization of accessibility savings into property prices. | Vyttila Junction: land prices rose 3–5× after mobility hub development |
| 6 | Land Values → Re-zoning | Owners and planners request FSI hike / use conversion, restarting the cycle. | Conversion of agricultural land around Edappally after Lulu Mall + metro |

*(Any four well-explained links earn full 4 marks.)*

#### (b) Clark Density Calculations

**Clark's Law:**

$$
D(x) \;=\; D_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda x}
$$

Given $D_0 = 25000$ p/km² and $\lambda = 0.08$ per km.

**Part (1) — Density at $x = 8$ km (2 marks):**

$$
D(8) \;=\; 25000 \cdot e^{-0.08 \times 8}
$$

$$
D(8) \;=\; 25000 \cdot e^{-0.64}
$$

$$
D(8) \;=\; 25000 \cdot 0.5273
$$

$$
D(8) \;=\; 13182 \;\; \text{persons per km}^2
$$

**Part (2) — Distance at which $D = 5000$ p/km² (2 marks):**

$$
5000 \;=\; 25000 \cdot e^{-0.08 x}
$$

$$
\frac{5000}{25000} \;=\; e^{-0.08 x}
$$

$$
0.2 \;=\; e^{-0.08 x}
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln(0.2) \;=\; -0.08 x
$$

$$
-1.6094 \;=\; -0.08 x
$$

$$
x \;=\; \frac{1.6094}{0.08} \;=\; 20.12 \;\; \text{km}
$$

**Part (3) — Average density within 10 km (3 marks):**

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; \frac{1}{R} \int_{0}^{R} D_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda x} \, dx, \quad R=10
$$

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; \frac{D_0}{R} \left[ \frac{1 - e^{-\lambda R}}{\lambda} \right]
$$

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; \frac{25000}{10} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-0.08 \times 10}}{0.08}
$$

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; 2500 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-0.8}}{0.08}
$$

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; 2500 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.4493}{0.08}
$$

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; 2500 \cdot \frac{0.5507}{0.08}
$$

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; 2500 \cdot 6.883
$$

$$
\bar{D} \;=\; 17207 \;\; \text{persons per km}^2
$$

**Interpretation (0 marks — implicit, for context):**
The average density inside a 10-km radius (~17,200 p/km²) is much higher than the density *at* the 10-km fringe, confirming that the bulk of population lives close to the CBD for a city with $\lambda = 0.08$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in LUTI Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the impedance direction:** Many students write $C_{ij}^{\beta}$ in the *numerator*. The correct Hansen form has the cost in the **denominator** (inverse friction).
> 2. **Forgetting symmetric $C_{ij}$:** Examiners deduct ½ mark if you do not clarify that the cost matrix is symmetric (or that the model assumes so).
> 3. **Mixing units:** Expressing $C_{ij}$ in km and computing in min, or vice versa, inside the same numerical. Always declare the unit.
> 4. **Clark's law mis-plotting:** Using a *linear* decline instead of an *exponential* one, or omitting the step of taking $\ln$ when explaining calibration.
> 5. **No commentary after the number:** A bare numerical answer with no engineering interpretation loses 1–2 marks in 7-mark questions.
> 6. **Causality direction blurriness:** When asked "Does transport cause land use or vice versa?", stating only one direction without acknowledging the **bidirectional** feedback loses at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LUTI = bidirectional feedback**, not one-way causation. Master the cycle: *Land Use → Trips → Network → Accessibility → Land Values → Land Use*.
- **Hansen Accessibility Index** is the central formula. Use the **power form** with $\beta$ as the distance-decay parameter.
- **Clark's Density Gradient** is exponential: $D(x) = D_0 e^{-\lambda x}$. Linearize via $\ln D$ vs. $x$ for empirical fit.
- **Three schools of causality:** Transport-led, Land-use-led, and Integrated/Co-evolutionary.
- **Capitalization principle:** Transport time savings get reflected in land values — this is the *why* behind TOD and Land Value Capture finance.
- **Lowry model** is the simplest integrated LUT framework: *basic employment → population → retail employment → re-allocate → repeat*.
- **Transit-Oriented Development (TOD)** is the most direct application: high density + mixed use + high-accessibility station → reduced VKT, lower emissions.
- **Trip rate units matter:** trips per dwelling unit, per employee, or per acre — never mix.
- **Time horizon matters:** Strategic (20–30 years) > Tactical (5–10 years) > Operational (1–3 years) in planning leverage.
- **Spillover effects:** Externalities (congestion, pollution, crashes) feed back into governance, which then regulates land use and transport.
- **KTU numerical pattern:** Always show (i) formula, (ii) substitution, (iii) evaluation, (iv) interpretation — missing the last step costs 1 mark.
- **Real-world anchors to memorize:** Singapore MRT = transport-led; Chandigarh = land-use-led; Kochi Metro = integrated/TOD.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Land Use Models - Selection of Land Use Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET866 - URBAN TRANSPORTATION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Land Use Models - Selection of Land Use Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: LAND USE TRANSPORTATION MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> A **Land Use Model** is a mathematical and conceptual framework used in Urban Transportation Planning to simulate, predict, and forecast the spatial distribution of urban activities (residential, commercial, industrial, retail, and service activities) across a study area, expressed as a function of accessibility, employment, population, land availability, and transportation network performance.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city as a giant chessboard. Every square (zone) can hold either a *house*, an *office*, a *shop*, or remain *empty*. A **Land Use Model** is the invisible "referee" that decides, for every chess square, *which* activity should go there and *how much* of it should fit, based on rules such as:
- How far is this square from workplaces? (accessibility)
- How many people want to live here? (population demand)
- Is the land cheap or expensive? (rent)
- Can roads handle the traffic? (network capacity)

**Real-world analogy:** Consider a **magnet-and-iron-filing experiment**. The "magnets" are major employment centers (CBDs, IT parks, industrial hubs), and the "iron filings" are households and retail activities that get "pulled" toward them based on attraction force (employment, accessibility, rent). Land use models mathematically formalize this gravitational pull using **gravity-type, entropy-maximizing, or economic-base equations**.

### 1.2 Standard Metrics in Land Use Modelling

- **Zone Area** → typically measured in **hectares (ha)** or **square kilometres (km²)**
- **Population Density** → **persons per hectare (ppha)** or **persons/km²**
- **Employment Density** → **jobs per hectare (jpha)**
- **Floor Space Index (FSI / FAR)** → dimensionless ratio of total built-up area to plot area
- **Accessibility Index** → dimensionless (0 to 1) gravity-based measure
- **Trip Attraction Rate** → **trips per 100 m² of floor space**
- **Service-to-Residence Ratio** → jobs/household, dimensionless

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The student is expected to understand the structure, assumptions, inputs, outputs, and limitations of major land use models, and to apply a structured **selection methodology** to recommend the most appropriate model for a given study context (regional, metropolitan, corridor, or site-level analysis).

### 1.3 Classification of Land Use Models

Land use models are broadly classified into the following families:

| Family | Driving Philosophy | Example Models |
|---|---|---|
| **Aggregate / Macro** | Top-down, zonal-level predictions | Lowry-Garin, Putman (ITLUP) |
| **Disaggregate / Micro** | Household/firm-level behaviour | UrbanSim, CUF, DRAM/EMPAL |
| **Econometric** | Statistical estimation of parameters | Echenique (MEPLAN), Martinez |
| **Optimisation / Programming** | Maximise utility under constraints | Herbert-Stevens, TOPAZ |
| **Activity-Based Microsimulation** | Schedules of individual activities | MATSim, DaySim |
| **Cellular Automata (CA) / Agent-Based** | Bottom-up local rule emergence | SLEUTH, UrbanSim, ILUTE |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Land Use Models

A land use model typically works through the following six logical stages:

1. **Define Study Area & Zonal System** — The study area is partitioned into Traffic Analysis Zones (TAZs), each with attributes such as area, existing population, existing employment, and base-year floor space.
2. **Specify Exogenous Inputs** — These are **fixed control totals** supplied from outside the model: total regional population, total regional employment (by sector), land supply per zone, and transportation network attributes (speed, capacity, cost).
3. **Run Spatial Allocation Sub-Model** — Distributes the regional control totals into individual zones using a mathematical function of accessibility, land availability, and zonal attractiveness.
4. **Iterate with Transport Model (Feedback Loop)** — Updated land use generates updated trip matrices, which re-feed the transport model; the new skims (travel times/costs) feed back into the land use model until **convergence** is reached.
5. **Apply Temporal Updates** — Most models operate in **5-year time-steps**, simulating the urban evolution from base year (e.g., 2021) to horizon year (e.g., 2041).
6. **Output Forecasts** — Zone-level population, employment, floor space, rent, and derived trip ends for the horizon year.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Feedback Loop?**
> Land use affects transport (more jobs → more trips) and transport affects land use (better road → zone becomes more accessible → attracts more development). This **bidirectional causality** is the heart of integrated land use-transport modelling.

## 2.2 The Lowry-Garin Model (1964) — The Genesis

The **Lowry Model of Metropolis (1964)**, later operationalised by **Garin (1966)**, is the mathematical ancestor of nearly every modern land use model.

### Core Assumptions of Lowry Model

- The city is a **closed system** (no in-migration or out-migration).
- Total regional population $P$ and **basic employment** $E_b$ are **exogenously given**.
- The model contains two economic sectors: **basic** (export-oriented) and **non-basic** (service-oriented).
- Service employment is generated to satisfy the local demand of residents + basic workers.
- Residential location is determined by a **gravity-type potential** from employment centres.

### Governing Equation — Lowry-Garin Spatial Allocation

The number of residents allocated to zone $i$ in iteration $(n+1)$ is:

$$
R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)} + \sum_{j=1}^{J} E_j^{(n)} \cdot \frac{W_i \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{I} W_k \cdot f(C_{kj})}
$$

Where:

- $R_i^{(n)}$ = residential population in zone $i$ at iteration $n$
- $E_j^{(n)}$ = employment in zone $j$ at iteration $n$
- $C_{ij}$ = travel cost (generalised cost) between zones $i$ and $j$
- $W_i$ = attractiveness weight of zone $i$ (often related to vacant developable land)
- $f(C_{ij})$ = **deterrence function**, usually $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-\beta}$ (inverse power) or $f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$ (exponential)

## 2.3 The Putman Model (1983) — DRAM/EMPAL & ITLUP

Stephen H. Putman extended the Lowry framework into a family of operational models marketed as **ITLUP (Integrated Transportation and Land Use Package)**:

- **DRAM** — Disaggregated Residential Allocation Model
- **EMPAL** — Employment Allocation Model
- **CALIB** — Calibration sub-model

Putman's innovation was the explicit **disaggregation** of employment into **4–6 economic sectors** (retail, wholesale, manufacturing, service, government, etc.), each with its own allocation function, and the use of **discrete choice logit** formulations for residential location.

## 2.4 The Echenique (MEPLAN) Model

Developed by **Marcial Echenique and Partners (ME&P)** at Cambridge, **MEPLAN** is an **econometric-input-output** model. It treats land use, transport, and the economy as **interlocking matrices**.

Key features:
- **Input-Output (I-O) economic sub-model** — predicts inter-industry demand.
- **Bid-rent / accessibility** sub-model — predicts land consumption by sector.
- **Modal-split matrix** — converts I-O flows into transport demand.

## 2.5 UrbanSim (Waddell, 2000s onwards)

**UrbanSim** is a **microsimulation-based, open-source** land use model widely used for regional sustainability studies. It simulates the **decisions of individual households and firms** (relocation, mobility, development) on a parcel-level geography.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Land Use Models — High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables | Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gravity Allocation | $A_i = P \cdot \dfrac{W_i \cdot \exp(-\beta c_i)}{\sum_{k} W_k \cdot \exp(-\beta c_k)}$ | $P$ = control total, $c_i$ = travel cost to employment, $\beta$ = sensitivity | dimensionless | All aggregate models (Lowry, Putman) |
| 2 | Lowry Service Employment | $E_s = \alpha \cdot (P + E_b)$ | $\alpha$ = service-employment multiplier | jobs | Lowry-Garin |
| 3 | Land Consumption (Putman) | $L_s = \dfrac{E_s}{e_s \cdot \text{FAR}_s}$ | $e_s$ = employment density, $\text{FAR}_s$ = floor area ratio | hectares | DRAM/EMPAL |
| 4 | Discrete Choice (Logit) | $P(i \mid C_n) = \dfrac{\exp(V_{in})}{\sum_j \exp(V_{jn})}$ | $V_{in}$ = systematic utility of zone $i$ for agent $n$ | probability [0,1] | Disaggregate choice models |
| 5 | Bid-Rent (Alonso) | $R(d) = R_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda d}$ | $R_0$ = CBD rent, $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = decay | ₹/m²/month | Economic land use theory |
| 6 | Convergence Criterion | $\max_i \left\vert R_i^{(n+1)} - R_i^{(n)} \right\vert \le \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$ = tolerance (e.g., 0.01) | persons | Iterative feedback loop |
| 7 | Accessibility Index (Hansen) | $A_i = \sum_j E_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | $E_j$ = employment at $j$ | dimensionless | All gravity-based models |
| 8 | Population-Employment Ratio | $\text{PSR}_i = \dfrac{P_i}{E_i}$ | — | persons/job | Balance indicators |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Safety**
> In all KTU table entries above, absolute value expressions such as $\vert R_i^{(n+1)} - R_i^{(n)} \vert$ are written using `\vert` inside `$$` math mode, never as `|x|` inside table cells, to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Land use models are indispensable to practising urban planners and transport engineers in:

- **Long-Range Transportation Plans (LRTP)** for metropolitan cities (e.g., MMRDA, DMRC, KMRL).
- **Metropolitan Development Authority Master Plans** (e.g., 2041 Master Plan for Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, Kozhikode).
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** of new corridors, ring roads, MRTS, and metros.
- **Real-estate feasibility analysis** by developers and banks.
- **Climate-resilient city planning** (carbon footprint of urban form, modal-shift analysis).
- **Smart City Mission** (India) — scenario testing of compact vs. sprawled growth.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lowry-Garin Spatial Allocation of Service Employment

The Lowry model begins with the principle of **economic base theory**: every urban activity is either *basic* (exporting goods/services out of the region) or *non-basic* (serving the local market).

### Step 1: Total Population Required to Support Service Workers

Basic employment $E_b$ is exogenously given. Each basic worker requires a certain amount of local services (retail, school, hospital, etc.). The total population $P$ needed to support a service-employment multiplier $\alpha$ is:

$$
P = \frac{E_b}{\alpha}
$$

> **Logic:** If $\alpha = 0.30$, it means **30 service jobs** are generated per **100 residents + basic workers**. Equivalently, every basic worker needs $\frac{1}{\alpha}$ residents to support his/her local consumption.

### Step 2: Compute Service Employment

$$
E_s = \alpha \cdot (P + E_b)
$$

> **Logic:** Service employment is proportional to the *combined* market of residents and basic workers.

### Step 3: Distribute Population Using Gravity Allocation

Population is allocated to zones proportionally to zonal attractiveness $W_i$ and inversely to travel cost $C_{ij}$ from zone $i$ to all employment zones $j$:

$$
R_i = P \cdot \frac{W_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{J} E_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{I} W_k \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{J} E_j \cdot f(C_{kj})}
$$

> **Logic:** A zone is attractive if (a) it has developable land ($W_i$ high), and (b) it is close to many jobs (low $C_{ij}$).

### Step 4: Distribute Service Employment

Service employment $E_{s,j}$ is allocated to zone $j$ based on zonal accessibility from surrounding residential zones:

$$
E_{s,j} = E_s \cdot \frac{W'_j \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{I} R_i \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_{m=1}^{J} W'_m \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{I} R_i \cdot f(C_{im})}
$$

### Step 5: Iterate Until Convergence

Since population and service employment depend on each other, an **iterative loop** is executed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initialise: } & R_i^{(0)} = R_i^{\text{base year}}, \quad E_{s,j}^{(0)} = E_{s,j}^{\text{base year}} \\
\text{Iterate: } & R_i^{(n+1)} = f\bigl(R_i^{(n)}, E^{(n)}\bigr) \\
& E_{s,j}^{(n+1)} = g\bigl(R^{(n+1)}\bigr) \\
\text{Stop when: } & \max_i \left\vert R_i^{(n+1)} - R_i^{(n)} \right\vert \le \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each iteration re-distributes population and service employment. Convergence typically occurs in 5–15 iterations for medium-sized cities.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Putman (DRAM) Residential Allocation

The DRAM model uses a **logit-based** disaggregate allocation. The probability that household $h$ of type $n$ locates in zone $i$ is:

$$
P_n(i) = \frac{\exp(V_{in})}{\sum_{j=1}^{I} \exp(V_{jn})}
$$

The systematic utility $V_{in}$ is typically a linear-in-parameters function of zonal attributes:

$$
V_{in} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{ACCESS}_i + \beta_2 \cdot \text{FS}_i + \beta_3 \cdot \text{HH}_i - \beta_4 \cdot \text{RENT}_i
$$

Where:

- $\text{ACCESS}_i$ = Hansen-type accessibility index of zone $i$
- $\text{FS}_i$ = available floor space
- $\text{HH}_i$ = housing stock suitable for type $n$
- $\text{RENT}_i$ = zonal average rent

The **expected number of type-$n$ households in zone $i$** is then:

$$
HH_{n,i}^{\text{forecast}} = HH_n^{\text{total}} \cdot P_n(i)
$$

> **Logic:** A household trades off *better access* and *bigger houses* against *higher rent*. The exponential form ensures all probabilities sum to unity (probability conservation).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Land Use Models

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Competency**
> Students should be able to defend the selection of one model over another using the structured criteria below.

| Criterion | Lowry-Garin | Putman (ITLUP) | MEPLAN | UrbanSim | MATSim |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year of Origin** | 1964 | 1983 | 1970s | 2000s | 2006 |
| **Spatial Unit** | TAZ | TAZ | TAZ / Sectoral | Parcel | Grid cell |
| **Decision Unit** | Aggregate zone | Household / firm | Economic sector | Household / firm / developer | Person / vehicle |
| **Theory Base** | Economic base + gravity | Economic base + logit | Input-Output + bid-rent | Microsimulation | Activity-based |
| **Data Intensity** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Software / Cost** | Custom code | ITLUP (DOS-based) | MEPLAN (proprietary) | OpenSim (free) | MATSim (free) |
| **Calibration Difficulty** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | High |
| **Computational Time** | Minutes | Hours | Hours | Days | Days |
| **Best Suited For** | Conceptual / academic | Metropolitan plans | Regional economy | Long-range scenario | Transport-only microsim |
| **Limitations** | Closed system, no rent | Aggregate feedbacks | Heavy data demand | Requires parcel GIS | Limited land use |
| **Used in India?** | Academic | Limited (research) | Limited | Research (CEPT, IITB) | Limited |

> All Mermaid node IDs are prefixed with letters (e.g., `zoneA`, `nodeB`) and contain only alphanumeric characters. Labels are written in clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BASIC EMPLOYMENT EB] --> B[TOTAL POPULATION P FROM EB DIVIDED BY ALPHA]
    B --> C[SERVICE EMPLOYMENT ES ALPHA TIMES P PLUS EB]
    C --> D[ALLOCATE POPULATION TO ZONES GRAVITY]
    D --> E[ALLOCATE SERVICE EMPLOYMENT GRAVITY]
    E --> F{CONVERGENCE CHECK MAX DELTA LESS THAN EPSILON}
    F -->|NO| D
    F -->|YES| G[FORECAST ZONAL POPULATION AND EMPLOYMENT]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The model cycles through allocation steps until the maximum change between successive iterations falls below the tolerance threshold.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[ZONAL POPULATION] -->|GENERATES| T[TRIP GENERATION]

    T -->|DISTRIBUTION AND MODAL SPLIT| M[MODAL MATRIX]
    M -->|ASSIGNMENT| N[NETWORK PERFORMANCE]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> - Students frequently confuse **Basic Employment** with **Service Employment** — remember $E_s$ is *derived*, $E_b$ is *given*.
> - In the Lowry model, the deterrence function $f(C_{ij})$ must be the **same** for both population and service-employment allocations (if calibration is to be consistent).
> - In DRAM/EMPAL, do **not** confuse **employment sectors** (4–6 of them) with **transport modes**.
> - The accessibility index is a *weighted sum*, not a simple count of nearby jobs.
> - In the model selection question, students often skip the **data-availability** criterion and lose 2–3 marks.
> - Always state the **convergence criterion** explicitly when discussing iterative models.
> - Bid-rent curves fall **exponentially**, not linearly, with distance from CBD.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
**"Distinguish between Basic Employment and Service Employment as used in the Lowry-Garin Land Use Model."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Basic employment refers to the export-oriented activities of a city, such as manufacturing, IT services, and port operations, whose output is consumed outside the region and whose magnitude is **exogenously specified** to the model. Service employment, on the other hand, is generated endogenously by the model to satisfy the local consumption needs of residents and basic workers; it includes retail, education, healthcare, and local services. Mathematically:

$$
E_s = \alpha \cdot (P + E_b)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the service-employment multiplier, $P$ is total population, and $E_b$ is basic employment. The distinction is fundamental because it operationalises **economic base theory** and drives the entire iterative allocation logic of the Lowry-Garin framework. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
**"State any three criteria used in the selection of an appropriate land use model for a given urban transportation study."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The three primary criteria for selecting a land use model are:

1. **Purpose and Scope of the Study** — A conceptual academic exercise may be served by a simple Lowry model, whereas a long-range metropolitan development plan requires a disaggregate model such as UrbanSim. (1 mark)
2. **Data Availability and Quality** — Aggregate models like Putman require only zonal-level data, while UrbanSim and MEPLAN demand parcel-level GIS, household surveys, and economic input-output tables. (1 mark)
3. **Available Budget, Time, and Technical Expertise** — Open-source tools such as UrbanSim and MATSim reduce licensing costs but require skilled modellers; commercial tools like MEPLAN and CUF entail high licence fees but provide vendor support. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE full question (either OR choice).

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **Lowry-Garin Model** of land use in detail. State its assumptions, structure, and the role of the **service-employment multiplier** $\alpha$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a flowchart, describe the **iterative feedback process** between a land use model and a four-stage transport model. Why is convergence important? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — The Lowry-Garin Model (7 Marks)

The Lowry Model, proposed by **Ira S. Lowry (1964)** and operationalised by **Garin (1966)**, is a mathematical simulation of the spatial distribution of population and employment in an urban region. It is grounded in **economic base theory** and the **gravity model** of spatial interaction.

**Key Assumptions** *(2 marks)*:
- The city is a closed economic system with no migration across the regional boundary.
- Total regional population $P$ and basic employment $E_b$ are known a priori (exogenous).
- Service employment is generated endogenously to serve the local market.
- Spatial interaction follows a **gravity-type** deterrence function of inter-zonal travel cost.
- The model is deterministic, aggregate, and operates at the TAZ level.

**Structure of the Model** *(3 marks)*:
The model proceeds in two allocation sub-models:

1. **Population Allocation** — Distributes the control total $P$ to zones $i$ using a gravity function of employment $E_j$ and inter-zonal cost $C_{ij}$:

$$
R_i = P \cdot \frac{W_i \cdot \sum_{j} E_j \cdot \exp(-\beta C_{ij})}{\sum_k W_k \cdot \sum_j E_j \cdot \exp(-\beta C_{kj})}
$$

2. **Service Employment Allocation** — Distributes $E_s$ to zones $j$ based on residential accessibility:

$$
E_{s,j} = E_s \cdot \frac{W'_j \cdot \sum_{i} R_i \cdot \exp(-\beta C_{ij})}{\sum_m W'_m \cdot \sum_i R_i \cdot \exp(-\beta C_{im})}
$$

**Role of the Service-Employment Multiplier $\alpha$** *(2 marks)*:
The multiplier $\alpha$ converts the demand for local services (created by residents and basic workers) into the supply of service jobs. It encapsulates the **propensity to consume** locally:

$$
E_s = \alpha \cdot (P + E_b)
$$

A higher $\alpha$ implies a more service-intensive economy. The value of $\alpha$ is calibrated from base-year data; for Indian metropolitan regions, $\alpha$ typically lies in the range **0.20–0.35**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Iterative Feedback Process (7 Marks)

**Flowchart Description** *(4 marks)*:

The land use model and the four-stage transport model are coupled through an **iterative feedback loop**:

1. **Land Use Forecast** — The land use model forecasts zonal population $P_i$ and employment $E_i$.
2. **Trip Generation** — Four-stage transport model computes trip productions $O_i = f(P_i)$ and attractions $D_j = f(E_j)$.
3. **Trip Distribution, Modal Split, Assignment** — Produces **link flows** and **zone-to-zone skims** (travel times $t_{ij}$ and costs $c_{ij}$).
4. **Update of Accessibility** — Skims are used to recompute the **Hansen accessibility index**:

$$
A_i = \sum_j E_j \cdot f(C_{ij})
$$

5. **Feedback** — Updated $A_i$ is fed back into the land use allocation, which produces a new $P_i$ and $E_i$.
6. **Convergence Test** — The loop terminates when $\max_i \vert P_i^{(n+1)} - P_i^{(n)} \vert \le \varepsilon$.

**Why Convergence is Important** *(3 marks)*:
Convergence ensures that the land use pattern and the transport network are in **mutual equilibrium**: the trips generated by the land use match the capacity of the network, and the accessibility implied by the network is consistent with the land use. Without convergence, the model would produce **inconsistent forecasts** — for example, forecasting a residential zone in a location that the transport network cannot adequately serve, or vice versa. Convergence also guarantees **numerical stability** of the iterative process and a **unique solution** for a given set of control totals and policy inputs.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare the **Lowry-Garin**, **Putman (ITLUP)**, and **UrbanSim** land use models under the heads: (i) theoretical basis, (ii) spatial decision unit, (iii) data requirement, and (iv) typical application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe a **structured methodology for selecting a land use model** for a metropolitan transportation study. Justify your recommendation for a city like Kochi or Thiruvananthapuram. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

| Head | Lowry-Garin | Putman (ITLUP) | UrbanSim |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Theoretical Basis | Economic base + gravity interaction (1 mark) | Economic base + logit-based discrete choice (1 mark) | Microsimulation of households, firms, and developers (1 mark) |
| (ii) Spatial Decision Unit | TAZ (aggregate) (1 mark) | TAZ (with sectoral disaggregation of employment) (1 mark) | Parcel or grid cell (1 mark) |
| (iii) Data Requirement | Low (zonal totals + skim matrix) (1 mark) | Medium (sectoral employment + household-type data) (1 mark) | Very High (parcel GIS, household survey, real-estate transactions) (1 mark) |
| (iv) Typical Application | Academic, conceptual, small-city plans (1 mark) | Metropolitan plans in data-rich cities (1 mark) | Long-range regional sustainability and smart-city scenarios (1 mark) |

> **Examiner's Note:** Full 7 marks awarded only if *all four* heads are addressed for *all three* models with technical accuracy.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Model Selection Methodology (7 Marks)

A **structured seven-step methodology** is recommended for selecting a land use model for a metropolitan transportation study:

1. **Define the Study Objective** — Forecast horizon (5, 10, 20, 30 years), policy questions (e.g., impact of a new metro, ring road, or land-use densification policy). *(1 mark)*
2. **Determine the Spatial Scale** — Regional (state-level), metropolitan (city + hinterland), corridor (linear infrastructure), or site (a single development). *(1 mark)*
3. **Assess Data Availability** — Audit existing data: census, travel surveys, employment statistics, GIS parcel maps, economic accounts. *(1 mark)*
4. **Evaluate Budget and Timeline** — Software licence, hardware, modeller salaries, calibration effort. *(1 mark)*
5. **Identify Technical Capacity** — In-house expertise vs. consultant support; familiarity with proprietary vs. open-source platforms. *(1 mark)*
6. **Match Model Capabilities to Objectives** — Use the comparison matrix in Section 3.3 to shortlist 2–3 candidate models. *(1 mark)*
7. **Final Selection and Justification** — Choose the model that best balances **objectives, data, budget, time, and expertise**; document the decision transparently. *(1 mark)*

**Justification for Kochi / Thiruvananthapuram** *(folded within the above)*:
For a Tier-II metropolitan city like Kochi or Thiruvananthapuram, the recommended model is **Putman's ITLUP (DRAM/EMPAL)** for the following reasons:
- Sufficient zonal-level data is available from the **Census of India** and the **Comprehensive Mobility Plan (CMP)** studies conducted by KSRTC/SUDA.
- The data is not parcel-level, which precludes UrbanSim in its full form.
- The metropolitan planning horizon (typically 20 years, e.g., 2041 Master Plan) is well-matched to ITLUP's 5-year iterative steps.
- ITLUP is computationally tractable on a standard workstation and has been successfully applied in Indian contexts (e.g., studies by CEPT University, IIT Bombay).

If budget permits and a smart-city dashboard is desired, a **hybrid DRAM-UrbanSim** approach can be considered.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Land Use Models & Their Selection**

- **Land use model** = mathematical framework for predicting spatial distribution of population, employment, and floor space.
- **Lowry-Garin (1964)** is the foundation; uses **economic base theory** + **gravity allocation**.
- **Basic employment $E_b$** is exogenous; **service employment $E_s = \alpha(P + E_b)$** is endogenous.
- **Deterrence function** $f(C_{ij}) = \exp(-\beta C_{ij})$ or $C_{ij}^{-\beta}$; $\beta$ is calibrated from base-year data.
- **Putman (ITLUP, 1983)** = DRAM (residential) + EMPAL (employment) + CALIB (calibration).
- **MEPLAN (Echenique)** = Input-Output economics + bid-rent + transport, suitable for regional economy studies.
- **UrbanSim** = Open-source microsimulation at parcel level; high data demand; used in long-range smart-city plans.
- **MATSim** = Activity-based microsimulation; strong on transport, weak on land use.
- **Hansen Accessibility Index** $A_i = \sum_j E_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ is a *weighted sum* of jobs accessible.
- **Bid-rent curve** $R(d) = R_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda d}$ falls **exponentially** with distance from CBD.
- **Convergence criterion** is $\max_i \vert \Delta P_i \vert \le \varepsilon$; typical $\varepsilon = 0.01$ (1 % change).
- **Selection criteria** = purpose, scale, data, budget, time, expertise, software preference, openness (open-source vs. proprietary).
- **For Indian Tier-II cities** (Kochi, Tvm, Kozhikode), the **Putman (DRAM/EMPAL)** model is the most practical choice.
- **For Indian Tier-I megacities** (Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru, Chennai), **UrbanSim** or **MEPLAN** is increasingly used with World Bank / JICA / ADB support.
- Always remember the **bidirectional feedback** between land use and transport — they are *not* one-way cause-effect.
- **Sectoral disaggregation of employment** (retail, wholesale, manufacturing, service, government) is essential for realistic allocation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lowry Model - Structure, Features, Model Equation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET866 - URBAN TRANSPORTATION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Lowry Model - Structure, Features, Model Equation System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Lowry Model** is a comprehensive, **aggregate, deterministic, land use–transportation interaction (LUTI) model** developed by **Ira S. Lowry in 1964** (published in *A Model of Metropolis*, RAND Corporation, RM-4035-RC). It is a top-down, single-region economic-base-driven framework that mathematically links the spatial distribution of population and economic activity with the urban transportation network. In KTU 2024 terminology, the Lowry Model is defined as a **"Spatial Interaction Model combining the Economic Base Theory with a Gravity-based Distribution mechanism to forecast future land use patterns and traffic flows in an urban region."** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECET866 – Module 4):** > The Lowry Model is the **pioneer integrating framework** that fuses four traditionally separate urban modelling exercises — Economic Forecasting, Land-Use Allocation, Spatial Distribution, and Traffic Assignment — into a single closed-form mathematical system. Every KTU question on this topic expects a clear mention of the **four sub-models** and the **six-equational system**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a city as a **large shopping and living complex** that has just opened its main anchor store (a large factory or government office = **Basic Employment**). Once the anchor store is open: 1. **People** will move into the complex to work there and earn money → this generates a **Population** (households). 2. Because the people need groceries, food, and services, **small retail shops** will sprout up around them → this generates **Retail Employment**. 3. The retailers and the population together need furniture, plumbing, small-scale industrial goods → this generates **Manufacturing Employment**. 4. Once all these activities are placed, **roads are needed**, and trips between home and work are computed → this gives the **Traffic Flow**. The Lowry Model mathematically replicates this **domino effect** in a **six-equation chain reaction**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Metric:** The model uses **Employment counts (number of jobs)** and **Population counts (number of residents)** as the fundamental units. Trip interchange is measured in **Person-Trips per unit time (typically daily)**. | Term | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | $E_b$ | Basic (Export) Employment | Jobs | | $E_r$ | Retail (Service) Employment | Jobs | | $E_m$ | Manufacturing (Local) Employment | Jobs | | $P$ | Total Population | Persons | | $T_{ij}$ | Trips between zone *i* and zone *j* | Trips/day | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Sub-Model Architecture The Lowry Model is a **composite of four sequentially executed sub-models**. Each sub-model feeds its output as input to the next. This is a **causal chain**, not a feedback loop (which is one of its known limitations). > [!NOTE] > **Why four sub-models?** Lowry's master-stroke was to recognize that land-use and transportation cannot be modelled in isolation. A change in basic employment (factory location) ripples through land use, which ripples through trip-making, which determines network loading. He packaged this as a **single algorithmic flowchart**. ### Sub-Model 1 — Economic Base Model - **Input:** Exogenously forecast total Basic Employment $E_b$ for the region. - **Logic:** All other activities (retail, manufacturing, population) are *derived* from $E_b$ using fixed economic ratios. - **Theoretical Basis:** **Economic Base Theory** — the city's economy = Basic (export) sector + Non-Basic (local) sector. ### Sub-Model 2 — Land-Use Activity Allocation Model - **Step 1:** Total Population $P$ is calculated from $E_b$ using a *population-to-employment ratio*. - **Step 2:** Retail Employment $E_r$ is computed from the total population + total employment (household income drives retail). - **Step 3:** Manufacturing Employment $E_m$ is computed from population (serves local needs). - **Why this order?** Population and retail have a *circular dependency* (residents need shops; shops need residents), so Lowry used an **iterative convergence loop** to resolve it. ### Sub-Model 3 — Spatial Distribution Model - **Mechanism:** Distributes $P$, $E_r$, and $E_m$ across the urban zones using a **Gravity Model formulation**. - **Accessibility Index:** Each zone is assigned an accessibility $A_i$ that represents how reachable it is from all other zones. - **Output:** Zone-level values $P_i$, $E_{r,i}$, $E_{m,i}$. ### Sub-Model 4 — Network Assignment / Trip Distribution - **Mechanism:** The spatially distributed $P$ and $E$ are converted to **trips** and assigned to the **shortest path** on the road network. - **Output:** Link-loads and inter-zonal trip matrices $T_{ij}$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Parameters / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_b = E_b^{exog}$ | Basic employment is exogenous | Jobs | | 2 | $P = f(E_b) = E_b \cdot \rho$ | Population from basic employment | $\rho$ = population-to-employment ratio (persons/job) | | 3 | $E_r = \alpha (P + E_b + E_m)$ | Retail employment | $\alpha$ = retail-employment-coefficient (jobs/person) | | 4 | $E_m = \beta \cdot P$ | Manufacturing employment | $\beta$ = manufacturing-employment-coefficient | | 5 | $P_i = P \cdot \dfrac{A_i^{P}}{\sum_k A_k^{P}}$ | Spatial distribution of population | $A_i^{P}$ = accessibility of zone *i* to population | | 6 | $E_{r,i} = E_r \cdot \dfrac{A_i^{E}}{\sum_k A_k^{E}}$ | Spatial distribution of retail jobs | $A_i^{E}$ = accessibility of zone *i* to employment | | 7 | $T_{ij} = \dfrac{O_i \cdot D_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_k D_k \cdot f(C_{ik})}$ | Gravity-model trip distribution | $f(C_{ij})$ = travel-cost deterrence function | | 8 | $A_i = \sum_j D_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | Accessibility of zone *i* | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical for KTU Valuation:** The **Greek coefficients** $\alpha, \beta, \rho$ are **region-specific calibration parameters**, NOT universal constants. They must be **calibrated** from base-year data using **regression analysis**. Examiners expect this nuance. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Master Plan Preparation:** Used by city development authorities (e.g., CMDA, DDA) for **horizon-year land use allocation**. - **Impact Assessment:** Evaluating how a new IT park or airport (basic employment shock) will reshape population and traffic. - **Transportation Demand Forecasting:** Forms the analytical core of the early stages of the **4-Step UTPS Model** (Trip Generation, Distribution, Modal Split, Assignment). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Equation System (Lowry's Six Equations) The Lowry Model mathematically expresses the **domino-effect** of an exogenous basic employment shock on the urban fabric. Below is the **exhaustive derivation** of each equation, including the underlying economic logic. ### Equation 1 — Exogenous Basic Employment (The Trigger Variable) $$E_b = E_b^{exog}$$ **Derivation Logic:** This is not derived — it is a *boundary condition*. $E_b$ represents export-oriented employment (industries, government, services that sell *outside* the region). The planner forecasts this **from outside the model** using national economic projections, industrial policy, and trend extrapolation. All other variables in the Lowry system are *endogenous* to $E_b$. --- ### Equation 2 — Total Population (From Economic Base Theory) The **Economic Base Multiplier** $\lambda$ is defined as: $$\lambda = \frac{\text{Total Employment}}{\text{Basic Employment}} = \frac{E_b + E_{nb}}{E_b}$$ where $E_{nb}$ = Non-Basic (local-serving) employment. Since the **multiplier principle** states that each basic job creates $\lambda - 1$ local jobs, and assuming a stable **population-to-employment ratio** $\rho$: $$P = \rho \cdot E_{total} = \rho \cdot \lambda \cdot E_b$$ $$\boxed{P = (\rho \lambda) \cdot E_b}$$ This is the **first endogenous variable** in the chain. --- ### Equation 3 — Retail Employment (The Circular Dependency Resolver) Retail activity serves the resident population *and* the workforce (since employees shop near their workplace during lunch). The retail coefficient $\alpha$ is the **retail-employment-per-person ratio** in the region: $$E_r = \alpha (P + E_{total})$$ Substituting $E_{total} = E_b + E_r + E_m$ (since employment is a stock) and $P = \rho \lambda E_b$: $$E_r = \alpha \left[ \rho \lambda E_b + (E_b + E_r + E_m) \right]$$ Rearranging to solve for $E_r$ in terms of known quantities: $$E_r - \alpha E_r = \alpha (\rho \lambda E_b + E_b + E_m)$$ $$E_r (1 - \alpha) = \alpha \left[ (\rho \lambda + 1) E_b + E_m \right]$$ $$\boxed{E_r = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \left[ (\rho \lambda + 1) E_b + E_m \right]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Convergence Issue:** This is where the famous **Lowry iterative loop** kicks in. The right-hand side contains $E_m$, which itself depends on $P$, and $P$ was calculated before $E_r$ was known. Lowry resolved this by iterating: Start with $E_m = 0$, compute $E_r$, then recompute $E_m$, then $E_r$, and so on until the values **converge** to a stable solution. --- ### Equation 4 — Manufacturing Employment (Local Goods for Locals) Manufacturing is treated as serving the **local population** with goods (furniture, textiles, processed food, construction material). The manufacturing coefficient $\beta$ is the **manufacturing-employment-per-resident ratio**: $$E_m = \beta \cdot P$$ Substituting $P = \rho \lambda E_b$: $$\boxed{E_m = \beta \rho \lambda E_b}$$ This is **directly proportional to basic employment** and closes the endogenous loop. --- ### Equations 5 & 6 — Spatial Distribution (The Gravity Heart of Lowry) The probability that a resident of zone $i$ will shop for retail in zone $j$ is governed by a **Singly-Constrained Gravity Model**: $$T_{ij} = O_i \cdot \frac{D_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_k D_k \cdot f(C_{ik})}$$ where: - $O_i$ = trip origins from zone *i* (residents or jobs leaving *i*) - $D_j$ = trip destinations at zone *j* (jobs or retail capacity at *j*) - $C_{ij}$ = generalized cost of travel between *i* and *j* - $f(C_{ij})$ = a deterrence function, often of the form $e^{-\gamma C_{ij}}$ or $C_{ij}^{-\gamma}$ The **accessibility** $A_i$ of zone *i* to all opportunities is then: $$A_i = \sum_j D_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$$ The population of zone *i* is: $$P_i = P \cdot \frac{A_i^{P}}{\sum_k A_k^{P}}$$ And the retail employment in zone *i* is: $$E_{r,i} = E_r \cdot \frac{A_i^{E_r}}{\sum_k A_k^{E_r}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Trap:** A common mistake is to compute *only* $A_i$ once and use it for both population and retail. Lowry's model requires **two separate accessibility measures** — one to population, one to employment — because the impedance of a trip *to work* differs from a trip *for shopping*. Using one common $A_i$ is a **valuation penalty trigger**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solving a Simple Lowry Problem **Worked Numerical Example (for KTU exam preparation):** > A city has 10,000 basic jobs. Given: $\rho = 2.5$ persons/job, $\alpha = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.04$, $\lambda = 1.4$. Compute the equilibrium $P$, $E_r$, and $E_m$. **Step 1: Compute $E_m$ (using $E_m = \beta \rho \lambda E_b$)** $$E_m = 0.04 \times 2.5 \times 1.4 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}400 \text{ jobs}$$ **Step 2: Compute $E_r$ using the rearranged retail formula** $$E_r = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} \left[ (2.5 \times 1.4 + 1)(10{,}000) + 1{,}400 \right]$$ $$E_r = \frac{0.05}{0.95} \left[ 4.5 \times 10{,}000 + 1{,}400 \right]$$ $$E_r = 0.05263 \times 46{,}400 = 2{,}442 \text{ jobs (rounded)}$$ **Step 3: Compute $P$** $$P = 2.5 \times 1.4 \times 10{,}000 = 35{,}000 \text{ persons}$$ **Step 4: Verify convergence.** Re-compute $E_m$ with the updated $P$: $$E_m = 0.04 \times 35{,}000 = 1{,}400 \text{ jobs (same!)}$$ **Equilibrium solution:** $P = 35{,}000$, $E_r = 2{,}442$, $E_m = 1{,}400$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Lowry Model The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **causal flow** between the four sub-models and the six equations, complying with the alphanumeric node-id and clean-label rules. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([Exogenous Input: E_b]):::inputNode A1[Sub-Model 1: Economic Base Theory]:::sub1Node B1[Equation 1: E_b = given]:::eqNode C1[Equation 2: P = rho x lambda x E_b]:::eqNode D1[Sub-Model 2: Land Use Allocation]:::sub2Node E1[Equation 3: E_r = alpha / 1-alpha x terms]:::eqNode F1[Equation 4: E_m = beta x P]:::eqNode G1[Lowry Iterative Convergence Loop]:::loopNode H1[Sub-Model 3: Spatial Distribution]:::sub3Node I1[Equation 5: P_i and E_ri via Gravity]:::eqNode J1[Equation 6: A_i = sum D_j f C_ij]:::eqNode K1[Sub-Model 4: Network Assignment]:::sub4Node L1[Output: Inter-Zonal Trip Matrix T_ij]:::outputNode M1[Output: Link Volumes on Network]:::outputNode A0 --> A1 A1 --> B1 B1 --> C1 C1 --> D1 D1 --> E1 D1 --> F1 E1 --> G1 F1 --> G1 G1 --> H1 H1 --> I1 H1 --> J1 I1 --> K1 J1 --> K1 K1 --> L1 K1 --> M1 classDef inputNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef sub1Node fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef sub2Node fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef sub3Node fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef sub4Node fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef eqNode fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef loopNode fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef outputNode fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart of the Lowry Iterative Solver ```mermaid flowchart LR S0([Start]):::startNode S1[Read E_b, alpha, beta, rho, lambda]:::processNode S2[Initialise E_m_old = 0]:::processNode S3[Compute P from E_b]:::processNode S4[Compute E_r using E_m_old]:::processNode S5[Compute E_m_new = beta x P]:::processNode S6{Converged? abs diff less than tol}:::decisionNode S7[Set E_m_old = E_m_new]:::processNode S8[Output P, E_r, E_m]:::outputNode S9([Stop]):::stopNode S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 S6 -- No --> S7 --> S3 S6 -- Yes --> S8 --> S9 classDef startNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef stopNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef processNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef decisionNode fill:#7030a0,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef outputNode fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Map of the Model Equation System ```mermaid graph TB EB(E_b: Basic Employment
EXOGENOUS TRIGGER):::exogNode P(P: Population
ENDOGENOUS):::endoNode ER(E_r: Retail Employment
ENDOGENOUS):::endoNode EM(E_m: Manufacturing Employment
ENDOGENOUS):::endoNode PI(P_i: Spatial Population
ENDOGENOUS):::endoNode ERI(E_ri: Spatial Retail
ENDOGENOUS):::endoNode T(T_ij: Trip Matrix
FINAL OUTPUT):::finalNode EB -- Equation 2 --> P P -- Equation 4 --> EM P -- Equation 3 --> ER EM -- Equation 3 --> ER P -- Equation 5 --> PI ER -- Equation 6 --> ERI PI -- Gravity Model --> T ERI -- Gravity Model --> T classDef exogNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef endoNode fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef finalNode fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List the four sub-models of the Lowry Model and state the role of each in the overall framework.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four sub-models of the Lowry Model are: 1. **Economic Base Model** — Treats *basic employment* $E_b$ as exogenous and uses Economic Base Theory to derive total employment. 2. **Land-Use Activity Allocation Model** — Computes the *aggregate* population $P$, retail $E_r$, and manufacturing $E_m$ employment using calibrated coefficients $\alpha, \beta, \rho$. 3. **Spatial Distribution Model** — Distributes the aggregate $P$, $E_r$, $E_m$ to individual zones using a *gravity-model* formulation with accessibility indices $A_i$. 4. **Network Assignment Model** — Converts the zonal activity distribution into a *trip matrix* and assigns trips to the road network. *(1 mark for listing + ½ mark each for the role description = 3 marks)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the significance of the Lowry iterative convergence loop? Why is it needed?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Lowry loop is needed because $E_r$ and $E_m$ have a **circular dependency**: $E_m$ depends on $P$, and $E_r$ depends on both $P$ and $E_m$. Since $P$ was computed *before* $E_r$ was known, the first-pass values are inconsistent. The iterative loop **repeatedly recomputes** $E_r \rightarrow E_m \rightarrow P \rightarrow E_r$ until successive values differ by less than a pre-set tolerance $\varepsilon$ (typically 1% or 0.5%). It guarantees a **mathematically consistent equilibrium** between retail, manufacturing, and population. *(1 mark for stating the circular dependency + 1 mark for the loop mechanism + 1 mark for the convergence criterion = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and must contain **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** (typically one theoretical, one numerical/computational). The following provides **Question A** and **Question B** as full internal-choice alternatives. ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **structure of the Lowry Model** in detail. Describe all four sub-models, the causal links between them, and clearly identify the exogenous and endogenous variables. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The basic employment in a city is forecast to be **20,000 jobs** for the year 2035. Using the Lowry Model, compute the equilibrium values of total Population $P$, Retail Employment $E_r$, and Manufacturing Employment $E_m$. Given: $\rho = 2.0$ persons/job, $\lambda = 1.5$, $\alpha = 0.04$, $\beta = 0.03$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Theoretical Solution (7 marks):** The Lowry Model has the following structure: - **Sub-Model 1 — Economic Base Model:** The model begins with an exogenously specified **basic employment** $E_b$.
