#### C. Regulatory Measures (Immediate, Politically Sensitive)

- **Staggered Hours:** Spreads peak demand over a longer window, reducing peak-hour VKT by **5–15 %**.
- **Low Emission Zone (LEZ):** Restricts polluting vehicles; reduces PM, NOx.
- **Odd-Even (Delhi):** Cuts vehicles on road by ~50 % on the day, but induces **trip chaining** and **secondary car purchase**; long-term efficacy is debated.
- **Vehicle Quota (Singapore COE):** Limits total vehicles; controls growth to < 3 %/year.

#### D. Technological / Substitution Measures (Post-2020 Acceleration)

- **Telecommuting:** COVID-19 demonstrated that **20–30 % of workforce** can work remotely permanently.
- **E-Commerce:** Reduces shopping trips; a single delivery van replaces **5–10 individual car trips**.
- **Tele-medicine / Tele-education:** Reduces non-mandatory peak-hour travel.

#### E. Behavioural / Soft Measures (Cultural Shift Required)

- **Carpooling:** Occupancy rises from 1.2 to 2.5+ persons/vehicle → VKT drops ~40 %.
- **Public Bike-Sharing (PBS):** Kochi Metro integrated "Yulu", Bengaluru's "Bounce/Vaah" — addresses **last-mile connectivity**.
- **Employer Trip Reduction Programs (ETRP):** US-EPA mandated under Clean Air Act; gives tax breaks to employers reducing SOV (Single-Occupant Vehicle) trips.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **VKT Elasticity w.r.t. Density** | $e_{VKT,\rho} = \frac{\%\Delta VKT}{\%\Delta \rho}$ | Reduction in VKT per % rise in density | **-0.3 to -0.5** |
| 2 | **Price Elasticity of Car Travel** | $e_{P,VKT} = \frac{\%\Delta VKT}{\%\Delta P_{fuel}}$ | Demand response to fuel price | **-0.1 (SR) to -0.4 (LR)** |
| 3 | **Modal Shift Elasticity** | $MS_{shift} = f(C_{car}, C_{transit}, T_{travel})$ | Shift to transit when $C_{car} \uparrow$ | **0.2 to 0.5** |
| 4 | **Peak-Hour VKT Reduction via Staggering** | $\Delta VKT_{peak} = \alpha \cdot VKT_{peak}$ | $\alpha$ = spread factor | **α ≈ 0.05–0.15** |
| 5 | **Trip Rate Equation** | $T = f( \text{HH size}, \text{Income}, \text{Auto Own}, \text{Density})$ | Cross-classification trip generation | **8–10 trips/HH** |
| 6 | **Accessibility Index (Gravity Model)** | $AI_i = \sum_j \frac{A_j}{t_{ij}^{\beta}}$ | Opportunities reachable from node $i$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Modal Share Logit Model** | $P_m = \frac{e^{U_m}}{\sum_k e^{U_k}}$ | Probability of choosing mode $m$ | $0 \le P_m \le 1$ |
| 8 | **Pigouvian Toll (Congestion)** | $\tau^* = MC - AC = MEC$ | Optimal toll to internalise externality | ₹/km or ₹/entry |
| 9 | **Carbon Reduction (Telecommuting)** | $CO_2 \downarrow = N_{WFH} \cdot VKT_{commute} \cdot EF$ | $EF$ = emission factor (kg CO₂/km) | **EF ≈ 0.12 kg CO₂/km** |
| 10 | **Carpooling Occupancy Gain** | $\Delta VKT = \frac{1}{2}(1 - \frac{1}{n})$ | For $n$ riders instead of 1 | **50 % drop for 2 riders** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Note:** In KTU 2024, students are expected to know the **mechanism** of these measures and at least **3–4 quantitative relationships**. Pure rote definitions score poorly; **cause-effect numerical reasoning** scores high.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Planning

- **Urban planners** use these solutions in **Comprehensive Mobility Plans (CMP)** for KTU-affiliated Smart City projects (Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram Smart City).
- **Traffic engineers** use congestion pricing data to design **Area Traffic Control (ATC)** systems.
- **Environmental engineers** quantify CO₂e reduction for **EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment)** of urban projects.
- **Policy makers** in MoHUA (Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs) cite these in **National Urban Transport Policy (NUTP), 2006/2014**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Density–VKT Relationship and Trip Length

**Problem Context:** Show how an increase in urban density reduces Vehicle Kilometres Travelled (VKT) per capita.

**Step 1 — Define Average Trip Length (ATL):**

$$ATL = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i}{N}$$

where $d_i$ = length of the $i$-th trip, $N$ = total number of trips per capita per day.

**Step 2 — Relate ATL to Urban Density:**

In a uniformly populated city of radius $R$, density $\rho = \dfrac{P}{\pi R^2}$.

For a randomly chosen origin–destination pair, the expected distance is:

$$E[d] = \frac{1}{R^2}\int_0^R \int_0^R \sqrt{r_1^2 + r_2^2 - 2r_1 r_2 \cos\theta} \cdot r_1 r_2 \, dr_1 dr_2 d\theta$$

This integral simplifies (Clark-Evans result) to:

$$E[d] = \frac{128 R}{45 \pi} = k \cdot R$$

where $k = \dfrac{128}{45\pi} \approx 0.906$.

**Step 3 — Express $R$ in terms of $\rho$:**

From $\rho = \dfrac{P}{\pi R^2}$, we get $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\pi \rho}}$.

Substitute back:

$$E[d] = k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P}{\pi \rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Elasticity:**

$$\frac{\partial E[d]}{\partial \rho} = -\frac{k}{2} \sqrt{\frac{P}{\pi}} \cdot \rho^{-3/2}$$

$$\frac{\rho}{E[d]} \cdot \frac{\partial E[d]}{\partial \rho} = -\frac{1}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** The elasticity of average trip length with respect to density is exactly **-0.5** under the uniform-density assumption. This means:

$$\%\Delta E[d] = -0.5 \cdot \%\Delta \rho$$

**Interpretation for a KTU student:**
- **Double the density → 29.3 % drop in average trip length** (since $2^{-0.5} = 0.707$).
- Since $VKT = N \cdot ATL$, VKT per capita also drops by ~29 % for doubling of density.
- This is the **quantitative foundation** for compact-city and TOD policies.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pigouvian Congestion Toll

**Problem Context:** Derive the optimal congestion toll that maximises social welfare on a road.

**Step 1 — Total Cost on a Link:**

Total travel time on the link is $T(x) = t_0 \left( 1 + \alpha \left( \dfrac{x}{C} \right)^{\beta} \right)$, where:
- $x$ = flow (vehicles/hr)
- $C$ = capacity (vehicles/hr)
- $t_0$ = free-flow travel time
- $\alpha, \beta$ = BPR (Bureau of Public Roads) parameters; typical $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4$

**Step 2 — Marginal Social Cost (MSC):**

$$MSC = T(x) + x \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$:**

$$\frac{dT}{dx} = t_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{x^{\beta-1}}{C^{\beta}}$$

**Step 4 — Set Optimal Flow via Marginal Cost Pricing:**

At the social optimum, **price = MSC**. The toll $\tau$ to be charged per vehicle is:

$$\tau = MSC - AC = x \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Substituting BPR parameters:

$$\tau = x \cdot t_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{x^{\beta-1}}{C^{\beta}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Given $t_0 = 5$ min, $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4$, $C = 2000$ veh/hr, current flow $x = 1800$ veh/hr.

$$\frac{dT}{dx} = 5 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{1800^3}{2000^4} \text{ min/veh}$$

$$= 3.0 \cdot \frac{5.832 \times 10^9}{1.6 \times 10^{13}} = 3.0 \cdot 3.645 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 1.093 \times 10^{-3} \text{ min/veh} = 0.066 \text{ sec/veh}$$

$$\tau = 1800 \cdot 0.066 \approx 118.4 \text{ sec/veh}$$

If we value time at **₹1/min (≈ ₹60/hr)** for a typical car:

$$\tau = \frac{118.4}{60} \approx 1.97 \text{ min equivalent} \approx ₹2/\text{veh}$$

This is the **optimal congestion toll** to internalise the externality.

**Interpretation:** Setting this toll forces each driver to consider the delay they impose on others, optimising total social welfare.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Modal Shift Logit Model (Closed-Form 2-Mode Case)

**Problem Context:** Estimate the share of commuters shifting from car to bus after a fuel price hike.

**Step 1 — Utility of Each Mode (Linear-in-Parameters):**

$$U_{car} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot t_{car} + \beta_2 \cdot c_{car} + \beta_3 \cdot \text{Comfort}_{car}$$

$$U_{bus} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot t_{bus} + \beta_2 \cdot c_{bus} + \beta_3 \cdot \text{Comfort}_{bus}$$

**Step 2 — Multinomial Logit Probability:**

$$P_{car} = \frac{e^{U_{car}}}{e^{U_{car}} + e^{U_{bus}}}$$

$$P_{bus} = 1 - P_{car} = \frac{e^{U_{bus}}}{e^{U_{car}} + e^{U_{bus}}}$$

**Step 3 — Probability Ratio (Log Odds):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_{car}}{P_{bus}}\right) = U_{car} - U_{bus} = \Delta U$$

**Step 4 — Effect of a Fuel Price Hike $\Delta c_{car}$:**

$$\Delta(\Delta U) = \beta_2 \cdot \Delta c_{car}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU):**

Let $U_{car} = 0.8$, $U_{bus} = 0.3$ (base case). Fuel price increases by ₹10/litre; assume $\beta_2 = -0.05$ per ₹.

$$\Delta(\Delta U) = -0.05 \cdot 10 = -0.5$$

$$\Delta U_{new} = 0.5 - 0.5 = 0$$

$$P_{car}^{new} = \frac{e^{0.5}}{e^{0.5} + e^{0}} = \frac{1.649}{1.649 + 1.000} = 0.622$$

$$P_{bus}^{new} = 0.378$$

**Original shares (without price hike):**

$$P_{car}^{old} = \frac{e^{0.8}}{e^{0.8} + e^{0.3}} = \frac{2.226}{2.226 + 1.350} = 0.622$$

$$P_{bus}^{old} = 0.378$$

Wait — this indicates the differential utility $\Delta U$ didn't change in a way that affects probability? Let me correct the calculation.

Let me redo with **clear numbers:**

Let $U_{car}^{old} = 0.8$, $U_{bus}^{old} = 0.3$, so $\Delta U = 0.5$.

$$P_{car}^{old} = \frac{e^{0.5}}{e^{0.5} + 1} = \frac{1.649}{2.649} = 0.622$$
$$P_{bus}^{old} = 0.378$$

After fuel price hike: $U_{car}^{new} = 0.8 - 0.5 = 0.3$, $U_{bus}^{new} = 0.3$.

$$P_{car}^{new} = \frac{e^{0.3}}{e^{0.3} + e^{0.3}} = 0.500$$
$$P_{bus}^{new} = 0.500$$

**Conclusion:** A ₹10/litre fuel hike (in this example) **reduces car share from 62.2 % → 50.0 %** — a **~12 percentage point shift to bus** for this commuter group. This quantifies the power of pricing measures.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Effectiveness

| Measure | Implementation Cost | Time to Impact | % VKT Reduction (Typical) | Political Difficulty | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Congestion Pricing** | High | 1–3 years | **10–20 %** | Very High | High |
| **TOD / Mixed-Use** | Low (regulatory) | 10–30 years | **20–40 %** (long-run) | Medium | Low |
| **Staggered Hours** | Very Low | Immediate | **5–15 %** (peak) | Low | Very High |
| **Carpooling Program** | Low | 1–2 years | **5–10 %** | Low | High |
| **Telecommuting** | Very Low | 3–6 months | **15–25 %** (work trips) | Low | High |
| **Cycle Infrastructure** | Medium | 3–5 years | **3–8 %** | Medium | Medium |
| **Vehicle Quota** | Low | 2–5 years | **5–10 %** | Very High | Low |
| **E-Commerce** | Very Low | 5–10 years | **3–7 %** | Low | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Always pair a measure with **(a) its mechanism**, **(b) its quantitative impact range**, and **(c) the time horizon**. This is the examiner's trinity.

---

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: The Decision Framework for Non-Transport Solutions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Urban Transport Problem Identified] --> B{Problem Type?}
    B -- Congestion --> C[Congestion Pricing Zone]
    B -- Pollution --> D[Low Emission Zone + Vehicle Quota]
    B -- Long Distance Trips --> E[TOD and Mixed-Use Zoning]
    B -- Peak Hour Crowding --> F[Staggered Work Hours + Flexi-Time]
    B -- Car Dominance --> G[Parking Pricing + Carpooling + NMT]
    B -- Work Trip Demand --> H[Telecommuting and E-Services]
    C --> I[Reduce VKT by 10 to 20 percent]
    D --> I
    E --> J[Reduce Trip Length by 20 to 40 percent]
    F --> K[Spread Peak Over Wider Window]
    G --> L[Mode Shift to Bus and Cycle]
    H --> M[Eliminate Physical Trip Entirely]
    I --> N[Improved Air Quality and Speed]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Sustainable Urban Mobility Achieved]
```

```mermaid
    P5 --> P5b[Bike Share]
    P5 --> P5c[ETRP]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: How Congestion Pricing Reduces VKT

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Driver
    participant TollSystem as Toll System
    participant Traffic as Traffic Network
    participant City as City Authority
    Driver ->> TollSystem: Enters Congestion Zone
    TollSystem ->> Driver: Charges Dynamic Toll
    alt Toll is High
        Driver ->> Driver: Cancels or Shifts Trip
        Driver ->> Traffic: Avoids Peak Travel
    else Toll is Low
        Driver ->> Traffic: Proceeds
    end
    Traffic ->> City: Reports Real Time Flow
    City ->> TollSystem: Adjusts Toll to Maintain Target Speed
    TollSystem -->> Driver: Communicates Updated Price
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Interaction Between Land Use and Transport

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LU[LAND USE SUBSYSTEM]
        LU1[Density]
        LU2[Diversity of Uses]
        LU3[Design Quality]
        LU4[Distance to Transit]
    end
    subgraph TR[TRANSPORT SUBSYSTEM]
        TR1[Trip Generation]
        TR2[Trip Distribution]
        TR3[Modal Choice]
        TR4[Trip Assignment]
    end
    LU1 --> TR1
    LU2 --> TR1
    LU3 --> TR3
    LU4 --> TR3
    TR1 --> TR2 --> TR3 --> TR4
    TR4 -->|Service Level| LU1
    TR4 -->|Service Level| LU2
    TR4 -->|Service Level| LU4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The double arrow between LU and TR is the **feedback loop** of urban transportation planning. Poor transport accessibility depresses land values; high density depresses car use. This is the foundation of the **LUTI (Land Use Transport Interaction)** models.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define Non-Transport Solutions to transport problems. List any four categories with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Non-Transport Solutions are **demand-side interventions** that aim to reduce, modify, or eliminate the need for physical movement of people and goods, without expanding the supply of transport infrastructure.

**Four Categories with Examples (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Land-Use Measures** — e.g., Mixed-Use Development, TOD (Transit-Oriented Development).
2. **Pricing Measures** — e.g., Congestion Pricing in London, Parking Pricing in CBD.
3. **Regulatory Measures** — e.g., Staggered Working Hours, Low Emission Zone.
4. **Technological Measures** — e.g., Telecommuting, E-Commerce.
5. **Behavioural Measures** — e.g., Carpooling, Public Bike-Sharing.

*(Any four acceptable; 5th gives bonus.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the concept of Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) as a non-transport solution. State any two benefits."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) is a **land-use strategy** that concentrates **high-density, mixed-use development** around **mass-transit stations** (within a typical 5–10 minute walking radius, i.e., 400–800 m). The objective is to **maximise transit ridership, minimise private vehicle dependence, and create walkable, vibrant urban nodes**. The 3Ds/5Ds/7Ds framework (Density, Diversity, Design, Destination, Distance, Demand Management, Demographics) underpins TOD design.

**Two Benefits (1 mark — 0.5 each):**

1. **Reduction in VKT per capita** by 20–40 % in the long run, due to shortened trip lengths and shift to NMT.
2. **Higher land values & fare-box recovery** for the transit operator due to densification near stations.
3. **Lower GHG emissions** — fewer car-km travelled.
4. **Improved accessibility** for low-income groups through proximity to transit.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various non-transport solutions to urban transport problems. Discuss in detail the **land-use based** non-transport solutions with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **congestion pricing** as a fiscal non-transport solution. Derive the expression for the **optimal Pigouvian toll** using the BPR (Bureau of Public Roads) cost function.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification and Land-Use Solutions [7 marks]**

**Classification [3 marks — 0.5 per category, 0.5 for overview]:**

| # | Category | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Land-Use Measures | Restructuring urban form |
| 2 | Pricing Measures | Internalising externalities |
| 3 | Regulatory Measures | Rules and ordinances |
| 4 | Technological Measures | Substituting physical travel |
| 5 | Behavioural Measures | Voluntary behavioural change |

**Land-Use Solutions in Detail [4 marks]:**

1. **Mixed-Use Development (1 mark):**
   - Integrates residential, commercial, retail, and recreational activities in a single zone.
   - Reduces the need for long-distance travel; promotes walkability.
   - **Example:** Bandra-Kurla Complex (Mumbai) mixing offices, residences, and malls.

2. **Density Intensification / Densification (1 mark):**
   - Increases Floor Space Index (FSI) and population per unit area.
   - **Mathematical relationship:** $e_{VKT,\rho} \approx -0.5$ (derived above).
   - **Example:** Kochi Metro Corridor densification plans.

3. **Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) (1 mark):**
   - High-density nodes around transit stations (400–800 m radius).
   - Combines all 5Ds of built environment.
   - **Example:** Delhi Metro TOD Policy 2021, Kochi Metro station-area plans.

4. **Compact City / 15-Minute City (1 mark):**
   - All daily needs accessible within a 15-minute walk/cycle ride.
   - Originally proposed by Carlos Moreno (Paris); adopted in Paris, Melbourne, Portland.
   - **Example:** Paris "Ville du Quart d'Heure" plan.

**Conclusion (no extra marks, but adds quality):** Land-use solutions are the **slowest-acting but most impactful** in the long run, with potential VKT reductions of 20–40 %.

---

**(b) Congestion Pricing & Pigouvian Toll [7 marks]**

**Concept of Congestion Pricing [2 marks]:**
Congestion pricing is a **demand-management strategy** that charges vehicles a fee for entering a **congested zone** during peak hours. It is based on the **principle of marginal social cost pricing** (Pigou, 1920). Successful global examples:
- **Singapore** (ERP since 1998)
- **London** (CCZ since 2003) — reduced traffic by ~15 %
- **Stockholm** (Congestion Tax since 2006) — 20–25 % reduction
- **Milan, Gothenburg, Beijing** (variants)

**Derivation of Pigouvian Toll [4 marks]:**

The BPR (Bureau of Public Roads) travel time function is:

$$T(x) = t_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left(\frac{x}{C}\right)^{\beta} \right]$$

where $x$ = flow, $C$ = capacity, $t_0$ = free-flow time, $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4$.

**Average Cost (AC):**
$$AC = T(x)$$

**Marginal Cost (MC):**
$$MC = T(x) + x \cdot T'(x)$$

Compute $T'(x)$:

$$T'(x) = t_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{x^{\beta - 1}}{C^{\beta}}$$

**Pigouvian Toll $\tau^*$:**
$$\tau^* = MC - AC = x \cdot T'(x) = x \cdot t_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{x^{\beta-1}}{C^{\beta}}$$

**Numerical Example [1 mark]:**
For $x = 1800$ veh/hr, $C = 2000$ veh/hr, $t_0 = 5$ min, $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4$:

$$\tau^* = 1800 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{1800^3}{2000^4} \approx 1.97 \text{ min equivalent per vehicle}$$

At value of time ₹1/min → optimal toll ≈ **₹2 per vehicle entry**.

**Conclusion [0 marks reserved, but quality addition]:** The toll internalises the externality of congestion, drives some peak-hour trips to off-peak or to other modes, and the revenue is often earmarked for public transport (e.g., London — ring-fenced for TfL buses and Tube).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Travel Demand Management (TDM)**? Explain with examples how **telecommuting and e-commerce** act as non-transport solutions to urban transport problems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **staggered work hours, carpooling, and parking pricing** as regulatory, behavioural, and pricing-based non-transport solutions. Compare their relative effectiveness in reducing peak-hour VKT.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TDM, Telecommuting, E-Commerce [7 marks]**

**TDM Definition [1 mark]:**
Travel Demand Management (TDM) refers to a set of **strategies aimed at influencing traveller behaviour** to reduce single-occupant vehicle (SOV) travel, shift demand to off-peak hours, or eliminate trips altogether. It is the **demand-side counterpart** of Transportation System Management (TSM).

**Telecommuting (Work from Home) [3 marks]:**
- **Concept:** Allowing employees to perform their regular work duties from home or a remote location using ICT (Information and Communication Technology).
- **Mechanism:** Eliminates the daily commute trip (typically 15–40 km round-trip in Indian cities).
- **Impact:** 
  - COVID-19 experience: **20–30 % of workforce** in IT/services can work permanently from home.
  - Peak-hour VKT reduction: **15–25 %** in the work-trip segment.
  - CO₂ savings: $N_{WFH} \cdot VKT_{commute} \cdot EF$ where $EF \approx 0.12$ kg CO₂/km.
- **Example:** TCS, Infosys, Wipro adopted hybrid work post-2020, reducing office-space and commuter demand.
- **Limitations:** Not feasible for manufacturing, retail, healthcare, education sectors.

**E-Commerce [3 marks]:**
- **Concept:** Online purchase and home delivery of goods/services, replacing physical shopping trips.
- **Mechanism:** One delivery vehicle (van/bike) replaces 5–10 individual car trips to retail outlets.
- **Impact:**
  - Reduction in shopping-trip VKT: **3–7 %** citywide.
  - Logistics load on city roads **increases** (a counter-effect that must be managed).
  - Amazon, Flipkart, BigBasket, Swiggy Instamart dominate Indian e-commerce.
- **Example:** Singapore's "ClickCollect" parcel lockers at MRT stations.
- **Limitations:** Last-mile freight congestion; warehouse land use.

---

**(b) Staggered Hours, Carpooling, Parking Pricing [7 marks]**

**Staggered Work Hours [2.5 marks]:**
- **Type:** Regulatory measure.
- **Mechanism:** Different offices/schools start and end at different times (e.g., Government 9 AM, Private 10 AM, Schools 8 AM).
- **Impact:** 
  - Spreads peak-hour demand over a longer window.
  - Peak-hour VKT reduction: **5–15 %**.
  - **Example:** Bengaluru IT companies experimented with staggered hours in 2018.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, immediate effect, voluntary adoption possible.
- **Disadvantages:** Coordination difficulty, family logistics.

**Carpooling [2.5 marks]:**
- **Type:** Behavioural measure.
- **Mechanism:** Two or more commuters share a single vehicle for the same route.
- **Impact:**
  - Average car occupancy rises from 1.2 → 2.5+ persons/vehicle.
  - VKT reduction: **5–10 %** citywide; up to **40 %** for participating corridors.
  - **Example:** Bengaluru "Quick Ride" app, Kochi "MiGS" (shared mobility).
- **Advantages:** Low cost, voluntary, app-enabled.
- **Disadvantages:** Privacy concerns, route inflexibility, COVID-19 reduced uptake.

**Parking Pricing [2 marks]:**
- **Type:** Pricing measure.
- **Mechanism:** Charge market-rate or premium prices for parking in CBD/commuter zones.
- **Impact:**
  - **Donald Shoup's "High Cost of Free Parking"** research shows **30 % of CBD traffic is "cruising for parking"**.
  - Eliminating free parking → **10–20 % reduction in car trips** to CBD.
  - **Example:** SF Park (San Francisco), Connaught Place (Delhi) paid parking.
- **Advantages:** Generates revenue, regulates demand, encourages transit use.
- **Disadvantages:** Strong political and merchant opposition.

**Comparative Effectiveness Table (0 marks — for examiner's reference):**

| Measure | % VKT Reduction | Time to Impact | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Staggered Hours | 5–15 % | Immediate | Very Low |
| Carpooling | 5–10 % | 1–2 years | Low |
| Parking Pricing | 10–20 % | 1–3 years | Low–Medium |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Transport Solutions (supply-side) with Non-Transport Solutions (demand-side).** Building a flyover is a *transport solution*; congestion pricing is a *non-transport solution*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this error.
>
> 2. **Writing only definitions without examples.** KTU 2024 scheme mandates **Application-based Learning** under NEP 2020. Always pair a measure with an **Indian context example** (Kochi Metro, Delhi Odd-Even, Bengaluru IT staggered hours).
>
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps in numerical problems.** When asked for a Pigouvian toll, you **MUST** show the BPR function and the differentiation. Skipping directly to the answer loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the limitations/disadvantages** of a measure. Every non-transport solution has trade-offs (political feasibility, equity, reversibility). Examiners reward balanced answers.
>
> 5. **Mixing up 4-step & 7-step planning processes** with non-transport solutions. They are related but distinct. The classical UTPS 4-step model is a **transport supply analysis**; NTS is a **demand management** framework.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Indian policy context.** Always mention at least one: **NUTP 2006/2014, Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT, Kochi Smart City CMP, Delhi LEZ**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Notes (MUST remember before KTU exam):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Non-Transport Solutions = Demand-side interventions to reduce/eliminate travel need, **not infrastructure supply**.
- ✅ **5 Categories (5 Ps):** **L**and-use, **P**ricing, **P**olicy, **P**romotion (behavioural), **P**edestrian infra.
- ✅ **Most impactful long-run measure:** Land-use integration (mixed-use + TOD) — 20–40 % VKT reduction possible.
- ✅ **Fastest-acting measure:** Congestion pricing — 10–20 % reduction within 1–3 years.
- ✅ **Density-VKT Elasticity:** $e_{VKT,\rho} \approx -0.3$ to $-0.5$ (rule of thumb: **doubling density cuts VKT by ~30 %**).
- ✅ **Pigouvian Toll Formula:** $\tau^* = x \cdot t_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot \dfrac{x^{\beta-1}}{C^{\beta}}$ with $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4$.
- ✅ **Successful Congestion Pricing Cities:** Singapore (1998), London (2003), Stockholm (2006), Milan (2008), Beijing.
- ✅ **TOD Definition:** High-density, mixed-use nodes within 400–800 m of mass-transit stations.
- ✅ **5Ds of Built Environment:** **D**ensity, **D**iversity, **D**esign, **D**estination accessibility, **D**istance to transit.
- ✅ **Staggered Hours Effect:** Spreads peak load; reduces peak VKT by **5–15 %**.
- ✅ **Telecommuting:** Can reduce work-trip VKT by **15–25 %** in IT/services sector.
- ✅ **E-Commerce:** Replaces 5–10 car shopping trips with 1 delivery van; net citywide VKT reduction **3–7 %**.
- ✅ **Carpooling:** Doubling occupancy from 1 to 2 cuts VKT by **~50 %** for that trip.
- ✅ **Parking Pricing (Shoup):** ~30 % of CBD traffic is "cruising for parking" — pricing eliminates it.
- ✅ **Vehicle Quota (Singapore COE):** Keeps vehicle growth at **< 3 %/year** vs. global 5–8 %/year.
- ✅ **Indian Examples to Quote:** Delhi Odd-Even (2016), Delhi LEZ, Bengaluru IT staggered hours, Kochi Metro TOD plan, Mumbai BEST electric bus push, Pune PCMC cycle track.
- ✅ **Policy Backdrop:** NUTP 2006 emphasises **"Move people, not vehicles"** — NTS is central to this.
- ✅ **Examiner's Favourite Equation:** $\Delta VKT = \alpha \cdot VKT_{peak}$ for staggered hours (α ≈ 0.05–0.15).
- ✅ **Difference between TDM and TSM:** **TDM = demand-side (NTS focus)**, **TSM = supply-side efficiency** (signals, one-way streets, lane management).
- ✅ **Always mention 3 things for any NTS:** **Mechanism + Quantitative Impact + Limitation**.

---

# Module 4: Transportation Demand Management (TDM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transportation Demand Management (TDM)** is the application of strategies, policies, and programs designed to influence travel behavior by **reducing vehicle trips**, **shifting travel to more efficient modes**, **optimizing trip timing**, and **increasing vehicle occupancy**. As per the **Transportation Research Board (TRB)** and FHWA standards adopted in KTU 2024 scheme, TDM is a demand-side counterpart to traditional supply-side transportation planning.

Mathematically, the core intent of TDM is to reduce the aggregate **Vehicle Miles Traveled (VMT)** generated by a region or facility, expressed as:

$$\text{VMT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i \times L_i)$$

Where $T_i$ = number of vehicle trips of type $i$ and $L_i$ = average length of trip type $i$ (miles or km).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Think of a city road like a water pipe. Traditional transport planning makes the pipe **wider** (more lanes, new roads) to handle more water (cars). TDM instead says: **"Let's use less water, or share the water better"** by carpooling, working from home, or shifting to buses/metro. We are not "widening the pipe" — we are **managing the tap**.

TDM operates on the **demand-side** of the transport equation, treating the traveller as a rational decision-maker who can be **nudged** (in choice-modelling terms) through pricing, information, infrastructure, and policy levers to alter their trip-making behavior.

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

| Term | Meaning (as per KTU syllabus) |
|------|-------------------------------|
| **VMT** | Vehicle Miles Traveled — total distance driven by all vehicles in an area |
| **Mode Shift** | Change in share of travellers using one mode (e.g., car) to another (e.g., bus) |
| **Trip Generation** | Total number of trips originating from a zone |
| **HOV** | High-Occupancy Vehicle (2+ or 3+ occupants) |
| **Peak-Hour Spreading** | Distributing commuter trips away from a single peak hour |
| **ITS** | Intelligent Transport Systems — technology supporting TDM |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** Students must understand **what TDM is**, the **classification of TDM strategies**, the **congestion mitigation outcomes**, and how TDM is integrated into the **4-stage urban transport planning process** covered in earlier modules.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Three Pillars" of TDM

KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 organises all TDM actions under three classical demand-management pillars. These map directly to the **push-pull-soft** framework referenced in FHWA's TDM Encyclopedia.

### Pillar 1 — **Reduce Trip Demand (Trip Reduction)**
*Logic:* If we can eliminate the need to travel, the network load drops automatically.

- **Telecommuting / Work-from-Home (WFH):** Eliminates commute trips on workdays.
- **Compressed Workweeks (4×10 schedules):** Reduces commuting days.
- **Land-Use Integration (Transit-Oriented Development – TOD):** Mixed-use zoning so daily needs are within walking distance.
- **E-commerce & Delivery Consolidation:** Reduces shopping-related trips.

### Pillar 2 — **Shift Mode (Modal Shift)**
*Logic:* Move travellers from low-occupancy, high-emission modes to high-occupancy or non-motorised modes.

- **Public Transport Improvement:** Higher frequency, reliability, and integration.
- **Bicycle and Pedestrian Infrastructure:** Cycle tracks, footpaths, end-of-trip facilities.
- **Carpooling & Vanpooling Programs:** Increase Average Vehicle Occupancy (AVO).
- **Bike-Share / E-Rickshaw / Micro-mobility Services.**

### Pillar 3 — **Shift Time (Peak Spreading & Route Diversion)**
*Logic:* Even without reducing or shifting mode, the time-of-day distribution of trips can flatten the peak.

- **Staggered Office Hours:** Different reporting times for different departments.
- **Flexible Working Hours (Flex-time):** Workers choose arrival window.
- **Congestion Pricing:** Toll during peak hours → financial disincentive to travel at peak.
- **Real-time Route Guidance (ITS):** Spreads traffic across alternate routes.

## 2.2 Why TDM? — The Engineering Justification

Traditional supply-side solutions (adding lanes) suffer from the well-documented **"Triple Convergence"** phenomenon (Duranton & Turner, 2011):

1. **Route Convergence:** Diverted traffic from parallel roads returns to the new road.
2. **Mode Convergence:** Public transport users shift to private cars.
3. **Time Convergence:** Off-peak travellers shift to the (now free-flowing) peak.

The result: **induced demand** negates capacity gains. TDM breaks this cycle by acting on the **demand generator**, not the **capacity provider**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the units, boundary values, and the algebraic form. Use `\vert` in place of `|` in exam scripts to avoid marking errors.

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Units |
|---|------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{VMT}_{\text{new}} = \text{VMT}_{\text{base}} \times (1 - r)$ | VMT after TDM reduction with rate $r$ (decimal) | vehicle-km/day |
| 2 | $\text{Trip Reduction (\%)} = \dfrac{T_{\text{base}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{base}}} \times 100$ | % reduction in trips | % |
| 3 | $\text{AVO} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Passengers}}{\sum \text{Vehicles}}$ | Average Vehicle Occupancy | persons/vehicle |
| 4 | $\Delta \text{VMT} = \text{AVO}_{\text{old}} \cdot T \cdot L - \text{AVO}_{\text{new}} \cdot T \cdot L$ | VMT saved by carpooling | vehicle-km |
| 5 | $\text{Mode Share (\%)} = \dfrac{T_{\text{mode}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Share of trips by a given mode | % |
| 6 | $\text{Congestion Cost} = \sum_{i} (t_i^{\text{actual}} - t_i^{\text{free-flow}}) \cdot v_i \cdot w$ | Total delay cost; $w$ = value of time (₹/hr) | ₹/day |
| 7 | $\text{Cost-Effectiveness} = \dfrac{\text{Total Program Cost}}{\Delta \text{VMT}}$ | ₹ saved per vehicle-km | ₹/veh-km |
| 8 | $\text{Elasticity of Demand } E_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$ | Sensitivity of travel to price/charge | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Peak-Hour Factor (PHF)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak-hour}}}{4 \times V_{\text{15-min peak}}}$ | Flattening of peak measured by PHF | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{CO}_2 \text{ Reduction (kg)} = \Delta \text{VMT} \times EF_{\text{co}_2}$ | $EF_{\text{co}_2}$ ≈ 0.21 kg/km (typical car) | kg |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

TDM is no longer "nice to have" — it is the **central pillar** of:

- **Smart City Mission (India):** Public bicycle sharing, multimodal integration.
- **Bengaluru & Delhi Congestion Pricing Pilots:** Dynamic tolling.
- **NMT (Non-Motorised Transport) Policy, MoHUA 2014:** Footpath and cycle-track networks.
- **Climate Action Plans:** TDM cited as the **lowest-cost, highest-leverage** decarbonisation tool for urban transport.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** TDM typically delivers a **5–15% reduction in VMT** at 1/10th the cost of equivalent road capacity expansion. This is the headline number examiners love.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Trip Reduction Percentage from a TDM Intervention

**Problem Context:** A residential zone produces **12,000 vehicle trips/day**. After implementing a TDM program (combination of carpooling and WFH), the daily vehicle trips drop to **9,600**. Compute the **Trip Reduction Percentage** and the **VMT saved**, given average trip length = 8 km.

### Step 1 — Identify Base and New Quantities

From the problem statement:

$$T_{\text{base}} = 12{,}000 \text{ trips/day}$$
$$T_{\text{new}} = 9{,}600 \text{ trips/day}$$
$$L = 8 \text{ km (average trip length)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Trip Reduction Formula

Substituting into the formula from Section 2.3:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Trip Reduction (\%)} &= \frac{T_{\text{base}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{base}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12{,}000 - 9{,}600}{12{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2{,}400}{12{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 0.20 \times 100 \\
&= 20\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The TDM program reduced vehicle trips by **20%** — a high-impact result.

### Step 3 — Compute VMT Saved per Day

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{VMT} &= (T_{\text{base}} - T_{\text{new}}) \times L \\
&= 2{,}400 \times 8 \\
&= 19{,}200 \text{ veh-km/day}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Annualise and Convert to Emissions

Assuming 300 working days/year and emission factor $EF = 0.21$ kg CO₂/km:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{VMT}_{\text{annual}} &= 19{,}200 \times 300 = 5{,}760{,}000 \text{ veh-km/year} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ saved} &= 5{,}760{,}000 \times 0.21 = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ kg/year} \approx 1{,}210 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{/year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently give a "compute the trip reduction and emissions avoided" numerical. Memorize Steps 1–4 as a template.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Mode Shift & AVO Improvement via Carpooling

**Problem:** A corporate park has **5,000 employees**. Currently, **4,000** drive alone, **600** carpool (2 per car), and **400** use bus. After TDM (rideshare incentive), **800** solo drivers shift to carpool, with carpool occupancy rising to 2.5. Compute:

(a) Old and new **AVO** (among all private vehicles).
(b) Reduction in vehicles entering the park.

### Step 1 — Calculate Baseline Vehicle Count

Solo drivers: 4,000 vehicles.
Carpool (occupancy 2): $600 / 2 = 300$ vehicles.

$$\text{Total vehicles}_{\text{old}} = 4{,}000 + 300 = 4{,}300 \text{ vehicles}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Old AVO

Considering only private vehicle occupants (solo + carpool) = $4{,}000 + 600 = 4{,}600$ persons.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AVO}_{\text{old}} &= \frac{4{,}600 \text{ persons}}{4{,}300 \text{ vehicles}} \\
&= 1.0698 \text{ persons/vehicle}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — New State After TDM

- Solo drivers remaining: $4{,}000 - 800 = 3{,}200$
- Carpoolers: $600 + 800 = 1{,}400$ (with 2.5 occupancy)

New vehicles from carpool: $1{,}400 / 2.5 = 560$ vehicles.

$$\text{Total vehicles}_{\text{new}} = 3{,}200 + 560 = 3{,}760 \text{ vehicles}$$

### Step 4 — New AVO

Private vehicle occupants: $3{,}200 + 1{,}400 = 4{,}600$ persons (unchanged — same employees).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AVO}_{\text{new}} &= \frac{4{,}600}{3{,}760} \\
&= 1.2234 \text{ persons/vehicle}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Reduction in Vehicles

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= 4{,}300 - 3{,}760 = 540 \text{ vehicles/day}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction \%} &= \frac{540}{4{,}300} \times 100 = 12.56\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takeaway:** A modest **20% carpool shift** yields a **12.56% drop in vehicle inflow**. The relationship is **non-linear** because of denominator dynamics. This is a common KTU viva question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Congestion Pricing — Price Elasticity of Demand

**Problem:** A city introduces a ₹50 toll during 7–10 AM. The peak-hour vehicle volume drops from 8,000 veh/hr to 7,200 veh/hr. Compute the **price elasticity of demand** and comment on the elasticity.

### Step 1 — Compute % Change in Quantity (Trips)

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta Q &= \frac{7{,}200 - 8{,}000}{8{,}000} \times 100 = -10\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Assume Base Toll = 0 → New Toll = ₹50 (infinite % change is undefined). Use a marginal change model.

If we consider a **doubling** scenario (₹50 → ₹100) to test elasticity, with volume dropping to 6,000 veh/hr:

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta Q &= \frac{6{,}000 - 7{,}200}{7{,}200} \times 100 = -16.67\% \\
\%\Delta P &= \frac{100 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_p &= \frac{-16.67}{100} = -0.1667
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

$\vert E_p \vert = 0.17 < 1$, indicating **inelastic demand** for peak-hour driving — commuters are largely captive. This is the canonical finding of metropolitan congestion-pricing studies (e.g., Singapore, London, Stockholm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** TDM pricing measures work better when paired with **viable alternatives** (good bus/metro service). Without alternatives, elasticity stays low.
