# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a Comprehensive Mobility Plan (CMP). How is it different from a traditional Comprehensive Traffic and Transportation Plan (CTTP)?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Comprehensive Mobility Plan (CMP)** is a long-range, multi-modal, sustainable transportation planning document that addresses the mobility needs of all city residents — pedestrians, cyclists, public transport users, IPT users, and private vehicle users — with the goal of providing safe, affordable, and environmentally sustainable mobility.

**Key differences from CTTP:**

| **Aspect** | **CTTP** | **CMP** |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Vehicle throughput | People & accessibility |
| Modal coverage | Road-centric, motorized only | All modes incl. NMT, IPT, freight |
| Approach | Demand-driven (4-stage UTPS) | Vision-led, scenario-based |
| Time horizon | 20–25 yrs | Short, medium, long-term phased |
| Sustainability | Often ignored | Core pillar |
| Stakeholder engagement | Limited | Extensive, citizen-driven |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation table.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any six major objectives of a Comprehensive Mobility Plan.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Provide **safe, affordable, and accessible mobility** for all sections of society.
2. Increase **public transport modal share** to at least 60%–80% by the horizon year.
3. Promote **walking and cycling (NMT)** for short trips to reduce car dependence.
4. **Reduce congestion**, air pollution, GHG emissions, and accident fatalities.
5. Achieve **integrated land-use and transport planning** (TOD, compact city).
6. Establish a **unified Metropolitan Transport Authority (MTA)** and sustainable funding mechanism.
7. Ensure **universal accessibility** for persons with disabilities, women, and the elderly.

*(Any 6 well-explained points = 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **objectives of a Comprehensive Mobility Plan**, structuring your answer into a 4-tier hierarchy.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **major activities involved in the preparation of a CMP**, with reference to data collection, scenario planning, and strategy formulation.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

A CMP is guided by objectives that cascade from **broad strategic vision** to **specific implementation targets**. These can be structured in a **four-tier hierarchy**:

**Tier 1 — Strategic / Vision Objectives:**
- Provide safe, secure, affordable, and reliable mobility for all city residents.
- Promote sustainable urban mobility aligned with **SDG 11.2** and **NUTP 2006**.
- Achieve a **modal shift from private vehicles to PT and NMT**.
- Reduce urban air pollution and **GHG emissions** (Paris Agreement alignment).
- Foster **transit-oriented, compact, mixed-use** urban form.

**Tier 2 — System-Level Objectives:**
- Increase **public transport modal share** to ≥ 60% by horizon year.
- Provide **≥ 80% population within 400 m of a PT stop** (PT coverage).
- Reduce **average commute time** by 20%–30%.
- Build a **continuous, barrier-free NMT network** (≥ 0.5 km/sq.km).
- Decongest CBD through **demand management** (parking, congestion pricing).

**Tier 3 — Operational / Service-Level Objectives:**
- Achieve **headway ≤ 4 minutes** on trunk PT corridors.
- Ensure **average waiting time ≤ 5 minutes** at stops during peak hour.
- Provide **real-time PIS and integrated ticketing** (single smart card).
- Reduce **road accident fatalities by 50%** (Vision Zero).
- Maintain **PT service reliability ≥ 95%** (on-time performance).

**Tier 4 — Implementation / Governance Objectives:**
- Establish a single **Metropolitan Transport Authority (MTA)**.
- Define a **dedicated, ring-fenced urban transport fund** (LVC, parking cess, bonds).
- Adopt **5-yearly M&E reviews** with public dashboards.
- Build **in-house capacity** in city bodies for transport planning.

*Closing line:* This hierarchical approach ensures that **every concrete project** is traceable to a **higher-order vision**, providing a logical, defensible plan.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

The preparation of a CMP follows **eight major activities** (this is a frequently-tested sub-question in KTU Module 4):

**1. Inception & Stakeholder Mapping:** Form a Project Steering Committee, identify stakeholders (citizens, RWAs, women, disabled, industry), define study area, base year (e.g., 2024), and horizon year (e.g., 2044). Deliverable: *Inception Report*.

**2. Existing Situation Analysis (Diagnostic Phase):** Conduct OD surveys (HISS, roadside), CTVC, speed-delay, parking, accident, NMT, and PT occupancy surveys. Compile secondary data (census, master plan, GIS). Identify problem inventory and SWOT. Deliverable: *Existing Situation Report + GIS Atlas*.

**3. Travel Demand Modelling:** Develop / update the 4-stage UTPS model. Build scenarios — S1 BAU, S2 PT-focused, S3 NMT + Compact, S4 Tech & Demand Mgmt, S5 Integrated Preferred. Output V/C, mode share, network loading for each scenario. For NMT, use direct demand / sketch models. Deliverable: *Travel Demand Model + Scenario Forecasts*.

**4. Vision, Goals & Objective Setting:** Conduct stakeholder visioning workshops. Formulate a vision statement. Set **SMART targets** aligned with SDG 11.2 and NUTP. Deliverable: *Vision & Goals Document*.

**5. Strategy Formulation (Multi-Modal):** Develop the long-list of interventions across all 9 sub-systems — Roads (Complete Streets), PT (Metro/BRTS/Bus), NMT (footpaths, cycle tracks, PBS), IPT, Freight, Parking & Demand Mgmt, Road Safety, ITS, and Institutions/Finance. Deliverable: *Strategy Catalogue + Concept Designs*.

**6. Multi-Criteria Evaluation (MCE) & Prioritization:** Score each intervention on Effectiveness, Equity, Cost, BCR, Land, Environment, Public Acceptance. Use AHP / Weighted Sum. Produce phased project list — Short (0–5 yrs), Medium (5–10 yrs), Long (10–20 yrs). Deliverable: *Prioritized Project Portfolio*.

**7. Financial Plan & Funding:** Estimate CapEx + OpEx. Identify sources — Central grants (UTS, Smart Cities, Gati Shakti), State budget, Multilateral loans (World Bank, ADB, JICA), PPP, and innovative finance (LVC, parking cess, green bonds). Affordability check. Deliverable: *Financial Annexure + Cash-flow Model*.

**8. Approval, Adoption & Monitoring:** Submit draft, conduct 30-day public consultation, address objections, get cabinet / council approval, notify in gazette, set up empowered committee, transport dashboard, 5-yearly mid-term and 10-yearly comprehensive review. Deliverable: *Final CMP + Executive Summary + KPIs*.

*Closing line:* These 8 activities are **iterative, stakeholder-driven, and evidence-based** — making the CMP a robust, defensible, and implementable plan rather than a wishlist.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **Comprehensive Mobility Plan (CMP)** with the traditional **Comprehensive Traffic and Transportation Plan (CTTP)**. Discuss why the shift from CTTP to CMP is necessary in modern Indian cities.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **data collection methods and surveys** required for preparing a Comprehensive Mobility Plan.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Comparison Table (must be drawn for full marks):**

| **Parameter** | **CTTP (Old Paradigm)** | **CMP (New Paradigm)** |
|---|---|---|
| Year of origin | 1960s–1990s (Western, adapted in India 1990s) | 2000s onward, formalized by MoHUA in 2010s |
| Primary focus | Vehicle flow, road capacity | People, accessibility, sustainability |
| Modal coverage | Road-based, motorized only | All modes — NMT, PT, IPT, freight, EVs |
| Approach | Reactive, demand-driven | Proactive, vision-led, supply-shaping |
| Modelling | 4-stage UTPS (TGD, TDM, TMS, TA) | UTPS + LUTI + ABM + scenario planning |
| Sustainability | Often absent | Core (climate, equity, safety) |
| Stakeholder | Top-down, technocratic | Participatory, citizen-driven |
| Time horizon | Single long-term plan | Short / medium / long-term phased |
| Land use link | Weak | Strong (TOD, mixed use) |
| Outcome metric | V/C, journey speed | Modal share, accessibility, fatalities, emissions |

**Why the shift is necessary in modern Indian cities:**

1. **Rapid urbanization** — India's urban population will reach **600 million by 2030**. Car-centric plans are unsustainable.
2. **Rising congestion & pollution** — Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru rank among the world's most congested / polluted cities. V/C-based CTTP has failed.
3. **Climate commitments** — India pledged **net-zero by 2070**. CMP aligns with low-carbon pathways.
4. **Equity & safety** — NMT and PT users (80%+ of urban population) were ignored by CTTPs. CMP addresses their needs.
5. **Smart Cities & Gati Shakti** — central schemes now require CMPs for funding.
6. **Technology disruption** — EVs, shared mobility, AVs require flexible, scenario-based planning, not rigid 4-stage models.

*Closing line:* The CMP is not a rejection of CTTP, but its **modern, multi-modal, sustainable, and people-centric evolution**.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

A robust CMP rests on **high-quality, multi-source data**. The data collection is organized into **primary surveys** and **secondary data** acquisition.

**1. Primary Surveys (Field Data):**

| **Survey** | **Method** | **Sample Size** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Home Interview (HIS)** | Face-to-face at sampled households | 1.5%–2% of HHs | Trip rates, OD, mode, purpose |
| **Roadside OD** | License-plate or intercept at cordon points | 10%–20% of traffic | External-internal trips |
| **Workplace / Establishment** | Questionnaire at offices, schools | 5%–10% of establishments | Non-home-based trips |
| **Stated Preference (SP)** | Hypothetical choice scenarios | 500–1500 respondents | Mode-choice model parameters |
| **Classified Traffic Volume Count (CTVC)** | Manual or video at screen lines | 15-min × 24-hr (or 7 days) | V/C, peak hour, modal split |
| **Speed-Delay** | Floating-car / GPS probe | 10–15 runs per corridor | LOS, journey speed |
| **Pedestrian / Cyclist Count** | Manual at midblock & junctions | Peak hours | NMT volumes |
| **PT Occupancy** | On-board survey of buses/metro | Peak + off-peak | Load factor, LOS |
| **Parking** | Inventory + accumulation + duration | All on-street + major off-street | Parking demand & turnover |
| **Accident Data** | Police records + spot visits | 5 years | Blackspot map |

**2. Secondary Data:**

- **Census of India** — population, workforce, gender, vehicle ownership.
- **Master Plan / Zonal Plans** — land use, FAR, road network.
- **GIS base maps** — NRSC Bhuvan, Survey of India, OpenStreetMap.
- **CPCB / SPCB** — air quality, noise data.
- **Smart City DPRs / Mobility Plans** — previous plans for the city.
- **Transport Undertaking data** — bus routes, ridership, depot locations.

**3. Modern Data Tools:**

- **GPS-based probe data** (Google, TomTom) for speed & travel time.
- **Mobile-phone OD** (anonymized CDR-based, with privacy safeguards).
- **Automatic Passenger Counters (APC)** in buses.
- **IoT sensors** at signals and parking lots.
- **Drones & video analytics** for pedestrian counts.

*Closing line:* A CMP's **credibility is only as strong as its data**. Hence, primary surveys, secondary data, and modern digital tools must be combined in a **triangulated approach**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating CMP with CTTP** — many students write "CMP = CTTP + sustainability." Examiners deduct marks. CMP is a *paradigm shift*, not a cosmetic upgrade.
> 2. **Listing objectives without hierarchy** — a flat bullet list of 10 points is worth only 50% marks. Always use the **4-tier hierarchy** (Strategic → System → Operational → Governance).
> 3. **Skipping the 4-stage UTPS link** — even though CMP is broader, the **demand modelling** activity is *still anchored in UTPS* (4-stage model). Don't ignore it.
> 4. **Ignoring stakeholders & finance** — a CMP without institutional and financial plan is incomplete. Always include **MTA, LVC, PPP** in the answer.
> 5. **Forgetting NMT and ITS** — modern CMPs are *multi-modal*. Skipping NMT (footpath, cycle, PBS) or ITS is a guaranteed 2-mark cut.
> 6. **No phasing** — interventions must be **short / medium / long-term**, not a single flat list.
> 7. **Vague diagrams** — examiners reward **labelled, hierarchical flowcharts** (Vision → System → Operational → Implementation) drawn neatly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision points — read this twice before the exam:**

- **CMP** = long-range, multi-modal, sustainable, people-centric transportation plan for an urban area.
- **CMP ≠ CTTP.** CMP is *vision-led, scenario-based, multi-modal*; CTTP is *vehicle-centric, demand-forecast, road-only*.
- **Why CMP needed:** rapid urbanization, climate change, equity, road safety, smart-city linkage.
- **4-Tier Objectives:**
  1. **Strategic / Vision** — safe, affordable, sustainable mobility for all; PT/NMT shift; climate.
  2. **System** — PT share ≥ 60%, PT coverage ≥ 80%, commute ↓ 20–30%, NMT density ≥ 0.5 km/sq.km.
  3. **Operational** — headway ≤ 4 min, real-time PIS, integrated ticketing, accident ↓ 50%, reliability ≥ 95%.
  4. **Governance** — single MTA, dedicated fund (LVC, parking cess, bonds), 5-yrly M&E, KPIs.
- **8 Activities of CMP Preparation:**
  1. Inception + Stakeholder Mapping → Inception Report.
  2. Existing Situation Analysis → OD, CTVC, speed-delay, parking, accident, GIS.
  3. Travel Demand Modelling → 4-stage UTPS + LUTI + ABM + Scenarios (BAU, PT, NMT, Tech, Integrated).
  4. Vision & Goals → SMART targets aligned with SDG 11.2, NUTP 2006, Paris Agreement.
  5. Strategy Formulation → 9 sub-systems (RPNI-FPSI: Roads, PT, NMT, IPT, Freight, Parking, Safety, ITS, Institutions).
  6. MCE & Prioritization → AHP, Weighted Sum, BCR, phased (Short 0–5, Medium 5–10, Long 10–20 yrs).
  7. Financial Plan → CapEx + OpEx + funding mix (Centre / State / Multilateral / PPP / LVC).
  8. Approval & Monitoring → Public consultation 30 days, cabinet approval, gazette notification, Transport Dashboard, 5-yrly M&E.
- **Key KPIs to remember:** Modal Share %, PT Coverage %, NMT Density, Accessibility Index, GHG/capita, BCR, V/C, Reliability.
- **Institutional linkage:** MoHUA, NUTP 2006, UTS, Smart Cities Mission, Gati Shakti, NMT Guidelines, MTA.
- **Mnemonic — 9 Sub-systems:** **"RPNI-FPSI"** — Roads, PT, NMT, IPT, Freight, Parking, Safety, ITS, Institutions.
- **Mnemonic — 4 Tiers:** **"SSOG"** — Strategic, System, Operational, Governance.
- **Mnemonic — 8 Activities:** **"I-S-M-V-S-M-F-A"** — Inception, Situation, Modelling, Vision, Strategy, MCE, Finance, Approval.
- **In the exam:** always **draw a hierarchical diagram** of the 4-tier objectives and the **8-step activity flow**. Neat diagrams = 1–2 extra marks.
- **Avoid:** vague phrases like "improve transport" — use **SMART, measurable** targets (e.g., "PT share 28% → 65% by 2044").
- **Final tip:** the **CMP is a living document** — never say it is "one-time." Always mention **periodic review (5-yearly)** and **adaptive management** in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of GIS in Transport Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET866 - URBAN TRANSPORTATION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of GIS in Transport Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of GIS

**Geographic Information System (GIS)** is a computer-based system designed to capture, store, manipulate, analyze, manage, and present all types of spatial or geographic data. In the context of transportation engineering, GIS serves as a powerful digital framework that integrates locational data (where things are) with descriptive information (what things are like there) to support informed decision-making across the entire urban transport planning lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A GIS is "a system of hardware, software, data, procedures, and people used for the acquisition, storage, analysis, and management of spatial and non-spatial data related to transportation networks and land use."

The acronym **GIS** universally stands for:
- **G**eographic — Refers to location-based referencing using coordinates (latitude/longitude, UTM, or local grid).
- **I**nformation — Refers to the descriptive attributes linked to locations (population, road width, traffic volume).
- **S**ystem — Refers to the integrated hardware + software + people + procedures working in unison.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Map"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are a city planner holding a giant paper map of your city. On it, you have marked hospitals, schools, roads, and bus stops using colored pins. Now, instead of pins, imagine every location has a small digital card attached — the card for a bus stop tells you the routes passing through, the passenger count, the timing, and the shelter type. The card for a road tells you its width, surface type, accident history, and traffic volume. If you "zoom in" on a particular area, all the cards automatically refresh and tell you fresh data. If you "drag" a new road, the system automatically tells you how many houses will be affected and how traffic will redistribute. **That is exactly what GIS does for a transport planner!**

Think of GIS as a **layered cake**:
- The bottom layer is the **base map** (streets, rivers, boundaries).
- Above it, separate transparent sheets contain **thematic layers** — bus routes, traffic counts, land use, population density.
- The planner can add, remove, or "see through" each layer at will.
- A simple "click" on any feature retrieves a complete database record.

## 1.3 Why GIS is Indispensable in Transport Planning

Transport planning involves enormous volumes of geographically referenced data: O-D surveys (origin-destination), traffic counts, household income, land use, network geometry, and socio-economic indicators. Before GIS, planners relied on manual map overlays — a tedious, error-prone, and non-quantitative process. GIS revolutionized this by:

- **Storing** data in a spatially indexed database.
- **Overlaying** multiple thematic layers simultaneously.
- **Querying** "what lies within X meters of route Y?"
- **Modeling** accessibility, connectivity, and network flows.
- **Visualizing** results via digital maps for stakeholder communication.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love questions like *"Why is GIS preferred over manual methods in transport planning?"* Always answer in 4 key points: (1) Data integration, (2) Spatial analysis capability, (3) Faster updates, (4) Visualization and decision support.

## 1.4 Spatial Data vs. Non-Spatial (Attribute) Data

A fundamental concept is that GIS deals with **two coupled types of data**:

| Data Type | Definition | Transport Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial Data** | Describes the geographic location and shape of features (geometry) | Coordinates of a bus stop, line representing a road |
| **Non-Spatial Data** | Descriptive attributes linked to spatial features | Passenger boarding count, road name, surface type |
| **Temporal Data** | Time-stamped information showing change over time | Traffic volume on Monday 8 AM vs. Saturday 8 AM |

The combination "where + what + when" makes GIS a true **4D decision-support system** (3 spatial dimensions + 1 time dimension) for modern transport planning.

## 1.5 Coordinate Systems and Geo-Referencing

Every GIS dataset must be tied to a **coordinate reference system (CRS)**. Common ones include:
- **WGS 84** — Global standard (used by Google Maps, GPS).
- **UTM (Universal Transverse Mercator)** — Used for medium-scale engineering projects.
- **State Plane / Local Grid** — Used for city-level planning in Kerala (e.g., KSDB local grid).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Mixing coordinate systems (e.g., overlaying WGS 84 data on a UTM base map) causes features to appear shifted by hundreds of meters. Always **reproject** all layers to a common CRS before spatial analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Components of a GIS

A complete GIS consists of **five interlinked components** (Korte's framework, widely cited):

### 2.1.1 Hardware
The physical computing equipment — computers (workstations, servers), GPS receivers, total stations, digitizers, plotters, and high-resolution monitors. Modern transport-GIS setups also use **drones (UAVs)** for aerial survey of road networks.

### 2.1.2 Software
GIS software provides tools for data input, storage, query, analysis, and output. Categories include:
- **Desktop GIS** — ArcGIS (Esri), QGIS (open-source), MapInfo.
- **Web GIS** — ArcGIS Online, Leaflet, Mapbox.
- **Database engines** — PostgreSQL/PostGIS, Oracle Spatial, SQL Server.
- **Transport-specific extensions** — ArcGIS Network Analyst, TransCAD, EMME, CUBE.

### 2.1.3 Data
The most expensive and time-consuming component (often 60–80% of project cost). Transport-GIS data include:
- **Base layers** — Road network, administrative boundaries, rivers.
- **Land use layers** — Residential, commercial, industrial zones.
- **Census/Taz data** — Population, employment, income by Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ).
- **Traffic data** — Volume counts, speeds, accident locations.

### 2.1.4 People
GIS professionals — analysts, database administrators, transport engineers, and decision-makers who interpret outputs.

### 2.1.5 Procedures
Standard operating protocols — data collection standards, metadata documentation, QA/QC routines, and update policies.

## 2.2 GIS Functional Capabilities in Transport Planning

GIS provides **six core functional capabilities** that directly support the 4-step transport planning process (Trip Generation → Distribution → Mode Split → Assignment):

| Function | Description | Transport Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Capture & Input** | Digitizing maps, GPS surveys, remote sensing | Converting paper road maps into digital layers |
| **Data Storage & Management** | Spatial databases (PostGIS, file geodatabase) | Storing multi-year traffic count history |
| **Data Query & Retrieval** | SQL-based spatial and attribute queries | "List all schools within 500 m of NH-66" |
| **Spatial Analysis** | Overlay, buffer, network, proximity | Catchment analysis of metro stations |
| **Modeling & Simulation** | Integration with EMME/TransCAD/CUBE | Running 4-step model with GIS inputs/outputs |
| **Visualization & Output** | Thematic maps, 3D scenes, web maps | Public consultation maps for new BRT corridor |

## 2.3 Key Spatial Analysis Operations (Transport-Relevant)

### 2.3.1 Buffering
Creates a zone of specified width around a feature. **Transport use:** identifying properties within 100 m of a proposed flyover (for land acquisition), or finding households within 400 m of a bus stop (for accessibility analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Buffer Distances in Indian Urban Transport (as per MoHUA guidelines):**
> - Bus stop accessibility: **400 m (5-min walk)**
> - Metro station accessibility: **800 m (10-min walk)**
> - Noise impact zone of highway: **75 m**

### 2.3.2 Overlay Analysis
Stacks multiple thematic layers to find spatial intersections. **Transport use:** combining (Slum layer + Road Network + Flood Zone) to identify vulnerable settlements near flood-prone roads for evacuation planning.

Types of overlay:
- **Union** — Combines all features from both layers.
- **Intersect** — Keeps only overlapping areas.
- **Erase (Difference)** — Removes features of one layer from another.

### 2.3.3 Network Analysis
Specialized functions operating on topologically connected linear features (roads, rail, transit lines). Sub-functions:
- **Shortest path** — Dijkstra's algorithm for route optimization.
- **Service area** — All locations reachable within X minutes.
- **OD matrix computation** — Travel times between all zone pairs.
- **Vehicle routing problem (VRP)** — Fleet assignment for buses/garbage trucks.

### 2.3.4 Thiessen Polygons (Voronoi Diagrams)
Divides a region such that every point inside a polygon is closer to its assigned facility than to any other. **Transport use:** assigning each bus stop to its nearest metro station, or delineating school catchment areas.

### 2.3.5 Spatial Interpolation
Estimates values at unsampled locations based on known samples. **Transport use:** creating a continuous **noise contour map** from point measurements, or generating a traffic density surface from sparse count stations (using IDW or Kriging).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for GIS-Based Transport Analysis**

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean Distance** | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | $x_i, y_i$ = coordinates of two points | meters (m) | Straight-line distance between O-D |
| **Network Distance** | $D_{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ | $L_i$ = length of each road segment on the chosen path | km | Actual travel distance along network |
| **Route Directness (Circuitous Factor)** | $CF = \dfrac{D_{net}}{d_{Euclidean}}$ | $D_{net}$ = network distance, $d$ = straight-line distance | dimensionless (ideal = 1.0) | Measures network efficiency |
| **Buffer Area (Circular)** | $A = \pi r^2$ | $r$ = buffer radius | m² | Catchment of a transit stop |
| **Network Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{L_{road}}{A_{city}}$ | $L_{road}$ = total road length, $A_{city}$ = city area | km/km² | Quantifies road provision |
| **Service Area Population** | $P_{SA} = \sum_{i \in \text{buffer}} P_i$ | $P_i$ = population in zone $i$ within buffer | persons | Population within 400 m of bus stop |
| **Spatial Autocorrelation (Moran's I)** | $I = \dfrac{n}{W} \cdot \dfrac{\sum_i \sum_j w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x})}{\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | $w_{ij}$ = spatial weight, $W$ = sum of weights | dimensionless (-1 to +1) | Detects clustering of accidents |
| **Gravity Model (GIS-coupled)** | $T_{ij} = \dfrac{P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_{j} A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}$ | $P_i$ = prod, $A_j$ = attr, $C_{ij}$ = cost, $f$ = impedance | trips | Trip distribution in 4-step model |
| **Accessibility Index (Hansen)** | $A_i = \sum_{j} O_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$ | $O_j$ = opportunities at $j$, $C_{ij}$ = travel cost | dimensionless | GIS-evaluated accessibility |
| **IDW Interpolation** | $z(s_0) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} z(s_i) \cdot d_{i0}^{-p}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i0}^{-p}}$ | $z(s_0)$ = estimated value, $d$ = distance, $p$ = power (usually 2) | varies | Estimating traffic at unsampled points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** All absolute-value-like expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (never the bare pipe `|`), to preserve the markdown table structure. You will see $\vert CF - 1 \vert$ used in the KTU answer scripts.

## 2.5 Integration of GIS with 4-Step Transport Model

The 4-step urban transport model (UTM) — **Trip Generation, Trip Distribution, Mode Split, Traffic Assignment** — has been revolutionized by GIS integration:

- **Trip Generation:** GIS links socio-economic data (population, employment) to **Traffic Analysis Zones (TAZs)** as polygon features. Regression equations use zonal attributes.
- **Trip Distribution:** GIS computes **inter-zonal travel times** ($C_{ij}$) using the road network layer via shortest-path algorithms, replacing manual skim trees.
- **Mode Split:** GIS overlays transit network on road network; service-area tools measure accessibility by each mode.
- **Traffic Assignment:** GIS feeds networks to assignment engines (TransCAD, EMME) and visualizes congested links via color-coded volume-to-capacity (V/C) maps.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** "Explain how GIS enhances the 4-step transport planning process." This is a guaranteed 14-mark question. Structure your answer around each of the 4 steps, showing what GIS adds at every stage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Route Directness (Circuitous Factor) from First Principles

The **Route Directness (RD)** or **Circuitous Factor (CF)** is a fundamental GIS-calculated performance metric. It compares the actual network path with the ideal straight-line (Euclidean) path.

### Step 1: Define the two distances

Let point A (origin) have coordinates $(x_A, y_A)$ and point B (destination) have coordinates $(x_B, y_B)$.

The **Euclidean (straight-line) distance** is derived from the Pythagorean theorem in 2D Cartesian space:

$$
d_{E} = \sqrt{(x_B - x_A)^2 + (y_B - y_A)^2}
$$

The **Network distance** is the sum of lengths of road segments forming the shortest path between A and B along the actual road network:

$$
D_{net} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} L_k
$$

where $L_k$ is the length of the $k$-th segment in the chosen path, and $n$ is the total number of segments.

### Step 2: Form the ratio

By definition, the circuitous factor compares the actual travel distance with the ideal straight-line distance:

$$
CF = \frac{D_{net}}{d_{E}}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret the result

- If $CF = 1.0$, the network offers a perfectly direct path (rare in real cities).
- If $CF > 1.0$, the path is circuitous; higher values indicate worse connectivity.
- **KTU typical acceptable CF for urban areas:** between **1.2 and 1.5**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Kerala's coastal cities (Kochi, Kozhikode) often have CF values exceeding 1.6 in older neighborhoods due to dense, organic street patterns inherited from pre-automobile eras. GIS can compute CF for every O-D pair, producing a "connectivity quality" map for the entire city.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Compute the Circuitous Factor for a trip from Home (A) to Office (B).

- Coordinates of A: $(100, 200)$ in local grid (meters)
- Coordinates of B: $(500, 700)$ in local grid (meters)
- Network path: A → P1 → P2 → B, with segment lengths $L_1 = 350$ m, $L_2 = 320$ m, $L_3 = 180$ m.

**Step 1 — Euclidean Distance:**

$$
d_{E} = \sqrt{(500 - 100)^2 + (700 - 200)^2}
$$

$$
d_{E} = \sqrt{(400)^2 + (500)^2}
$$

$$
d_{E} = \sqrt{160000 + 250000} = \sqrt{410000} \approx 640.31 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Network Distance:**

$$
D_{net} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 350 + 320 + 180 = 850 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Circuitous Factor:**

$$
CF = \frac{D_{net}}{d_{E}} = \frac{850}{640.31} \approx 1.327
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $CF \approx 1.33$, the network route is about 33% longer than the ideal straight-line path. This is acceptable for a typical urban area (within the 1.2–1.5 range).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hansen-Type Accessibility Index (GIS-Computed)

Hansen's accessibility index (1959) is the most-cited measure of transport opportunity access. It is naturally computed using GIS overlay of network and opportunity layers.

### Step 1: Define opportunities at destination zones

Let $O_j$ be the number of opportunities (jobs, schools, hospitals) at destination zone $j$. For a city with $N$ TAZs, we have opportunity set $\{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_N\}$.

### Step 2: Define impedance function

The impedance $f(C_{ij})$ is a decreasing function of generalized travel cost $C_{ij}$ (time or distance). Common forms include:

- **Exponential:** $f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta C_{ij}}$
- **Power:** $f(C_{ij}) = C_{ij}^{-\beta}$
- **Tanner-style:** $f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta C_{ij}^{\gamma}}$
- **BPR-based:** $f(C_{ij}) = 1 / t_{ij}$, where $t_{ij}$ is congested travel time.

> [!NOTE]
> **BPR Function (Bureau of Public Roads):** $t = t_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{\beta} \right]$, with standard coefficients $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4$. GIS uses this to convert free-flow time $t_0$ into peak-hour congested time $t$.

### Step 3: Hansen's accessibility from zone $i$

$$
A_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} O_j \cdot f(C_{ij})
$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

Consider a city with 3 TAZs, with $O_1 = 1000$ jobs, $O_2 = 500$ jobs, $O_3 = 200$ jobs. Travel times from zone 1 to others: $C_{11} = 5$ min, $C_{12} = 15$ min, $C_{13} = 25$ min. Use exponential impedance with $\beta = 0.1$.

**Computation:**

$$
f(C_{11}) = e^{-0.1 \times 5} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

$$
f(C_{12}) = e^{-0.1 \times 15} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231
$$

$$
f(C_{13}) = e^{-0.1 \times 25} = e^{-2.5} \approx 0.0821
$$

Now compute the weighted sum:

$$
A_1 = (1000 \times 0.6065) + (500 \times 0.2231) + (200 \times 0.0821)
$$

$$
A_1 = 606.5 + 111.55 + 16.42 \approx 734.47
$$

**Interpretation:** From zone 1, the resident has access to an "effective opportunity count" of about 734 jobs, weighted by travel-time friction. Zone 1 has good accessibility because most opportunities are nearby.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to "compute accessibility for all zones," repeat the calculation for each origin $i$ and present a small table. This demonstrates GIS-style batch processing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: GIS Operations vs. Transport Planning Tasks

The following matrix is a **high-yield study aid** for KTU board preparation. It maps every major GIS operation to its specific transport planning application:

| GIS Operation | Trip Generation | Trip Distribution | Mode Split | Network Assignment | Land Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Buffering** | Population within 500 m of major road | Catchment of CBD | Walking distance to transit | Impact zone of new link | Notification zone for zoning change |
| **Overlay** | Combine pop + emp + land use | Combine O + D + cost | Combine transit + road | Combine network + demand | Identify conflicting land uses |
| **Network Analysis** | Accessibility to jobs | OD matrix build | Service area by mode | Shortest path, all-or-nothing | Connectivity index |
| **Thiessen Polygons** | Nearest TAZ assignment | Nearest service zone | Nearest transit stop | Nearest interchange | Nearest commercial hub |
| **Spatial Interpolation** | Traffic density map | Surfaces of trip ends | Mode share surface | Volume surface | Land value surface |
| **Hotspot Analysis (Getis-Ord)** | Accident-prone zones | High trip-end zones | High transit-use zones | Congestion hotspots | High-density development zones |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **Network Distance** with **Euclidean Distance** in their answers. Network distance follows the road; Euclidean is straight-line. For transport planning, **only network distance is realistic** for vehicular trips.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Network Density per TAZ

A GIS-enabled transport planner can compute road density for every TAZ. Let TAZ $i$ have area $A_i$ (in km²) and total length of roads within it $L_i$ (in km).

$$
\rho_i = \frac{L_i}{A_i}
$$

The city's overall network density is the area-weighted mean:

$$
\bar{\rho} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Benchmark Values (Indian Roads Congress, IRC):**
> - Arterial roads: **1.2 to 1.5 km/km²** in central business districts.
> - Sub-arterial: **2.0 to 3.0 km/km²**.
> - Local streets: **5.0 to 10.0 km/km²**.
> - **Overall desirable density for a major Indian city: 11–15 km/km²** (Kochi, Chennai ≈ 9–10; Delhi ≈ 21).

## 4.3 Conceptual Layer-Cake Model of GIS

This shows how different data layers stack to produce a transport-planning visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer Cake Concept (KSU/KTU standard):** Each thematic layer is a "transparent sheet" stacked on the base. Turning layers on/off produces different views (e.g., accidents-on-roads, or transit-on-population). This is the **single most-asked concept** in KTU module 4 exams.

## 4.4 Network Analysis Sub-Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NA[Network Analysis Engine] --> SP[Shortest Path Dijkstra]
    NA --> SA[Service Area Isochrone]
    NA --> ODM[O-D Matrix Cost Matrix]
    NA --> VRP[Vehicle Routing Problem]
    NA --> TURN[Turn Restrictions Handling]
    NA --> TMC[Live Traffic Integration]
    SP --> U1[Route Guidance for Buses]
    SA --> U2[Catchment of Metro Station]
    ODM --> U3[Travel Time Skim Matrix]
    VRP --> U4[Garbage Truck Fleet Routing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **MapInfo / ArcGIS Network Analyst** uses **Dijkstra's algorithm** for shortest path on weighted networks, where edge weight is travel time or distance. The algorithm finds the path of minimum total weight between two nodes in a graph with non-negative weights.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define GIS. List any four components of a GIS.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Geographic Information System (GIS)** is a computer-based information system that captures, stores, manipulates, analyzes, manages, and presents spatial or geographically referenced data.

The four primary components of a GIS are:
1. **Hardware** — Computers, GPS, digitizers, plotters, and storage servers.
2. **Software** — Desktop GIS (ArcGIS, QGIS), web GIS, and database engines (PostGIS).
3. **Data** — Spatial layers (roads, zones) and attribute tables (population, traffic counts).
4. **People** — GIS analysts, transport engineers, and decision-makers.

*(The fifth component, **Procedures**, includes standards, metadata, and QA/QC protocols.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**What is a buffer in GIS? Mention its application in transport planning with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **buffer** is a polygon feature of specified width created around a point, line, or polygon feature at a user-defined distance. It represents a zone of influence around a geographic feature.

**Transport Planning Application:**
- Identifying households within **400 meters** of a proposed bus stop to estimate ridership potential.
- Delineating the **75-meter noise-impact zone** along a national highway.
- Marking properties to be acquired within **100 m** of a new flyover alignment.
- Defining the **800-meter walking catchment** of a metro station for last-mile planning.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question 1(A)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the major spatial analysis operations performed by GIS, with specific reference to their use in transport planning. Give at least four operations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail how GIS is integrated with the classical 4-step urban transport planning process. Illustrate your answer with a suitable diagram.

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

The major spatial analysis operations in GIS, with their transport-planning applications, are:

**1. Buffering:**
A buffer creates a zone of specified width around a feature. In transport planning, buffering is used to:
- Compute the **catchment area** of a transit stop (e.g., 400 m around a bus stop).
- Identify land parcels affected by a proposed road (e.g., 50 m on either side).
- Define impact zones for noise/air pollution around highways.
- Determine the **service area** of a railway station for first/last-mile connectivity.

**2. Overlay Analysis:**
Overlay stacks multiple thematic layers to find spatial relationships. In transport:
- Overlaying **accident locations** on the **road network** identifies black spots.
- Combining **land use** with **population density** supports trip-end estimation.
- Intersecting **flood zones** with **road network** prioritizes roads for climate-resilient upgrades.
- Types include **Union, Intersect, Erase**, and **Symmetrical Difference**.

**3. Network Analysis:**
Network analysis operates on topologically connected linear features. It includes:
- **Shortest path** algorithms (Dijkstra's, A*) for route finding.
- **Service area / isochrone** analysis — all locations reachable within X minutes.
- **OD matrix (cost matrix)** computation for travel-time skims.
- **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** for bus and freight scheduling.

**4. Spatial Interpolation:**
Interpolation estimates unknown values from known sample points. In transport:
- Generating **noise contour maps** from sparse monitoring stations.
- Creating **traffic volume surfaces** from limited automatic traffic counters.
- Methods include **IDW (Inverse Distance Weighting)**, **Kriging**, and **Spline**.

**5. Thiessen Polygons (Voronoi Diagrams):**
Thiessen polygons partition a region so every point is closest to its assigned facility. In transport:
- Assigning each bus stop to its nearest metro interchange.
- Determining school catchment zones.
- Allocating demand to the nearest fuel station or parking lot.

**6. Hotspot / Cluster Analysis:**
Statistical techniques like **Getis-Ord Gi*** and **Moran's I** detect spatial clusters. In transport:
- Identifying **accident-prone zones** for black-spot treatment.
- Recognizing **high-congestion corridors** for capacity augmentation.
- Locating clusters of poor transit accessibility for equity-focused interventions.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

The classical 4-step urban transport planning process — **Trip Generation, Trip Distribution, Mode Split, and Traffic Assignment** — has been completely transformed by GIS integration. In the pre-GIS era, all spatial data were handled manually, using paper maps, hand-drawn isochrones, and spreadsheet-based calculations. GIS introduces automation, accuracy, and visualization at every stage.

**Step 1 — Trip Generation:**
Trip generation estimates the number of trips produced by and attracted to each Traffic Analysis Zone (TAZ). GIS stores TAZs as polygon features in a spatial database, with attributes like population, employment, household income, and car ownership. Regression equations (e.g., $T_i = a + b P_i + c E_i$, where $P_i$ = population and $E_i$ = employment in zone $i$) are applied to zonal attributes retrieved from the GIS. The output is a map of trip ends per zone, color-coded by production or attraction intensity.

**Step 2 — Trip Distribution:**
Trip distribution distributes the generated trips between O-D pairs using the **Gravity Model**:

$$
T_{ij} = \frac{P_i \cdot A_j \cdot f(C_{ij})}{\sum_{k} A_k \cdot f(C_{ik})}
$$

GIS plays a critical role here by computing the **inter-zonal travel cost $C_{ij}$** (time/distance) using shortest-path algorithms on the road network layer. The result is a "sk impedance matrix" that feeds the gravity model. In modern software (TransCAD, EMME, CUBE Voyager), this step is fully GIS-integrated.

**Step 3 — Mode Split:**
Mode split divides O-D trips among available modes (walk, bicycle, bus, metro, car). GIS overlays multiple network layers (road, rail, bus routes) and computes **accessibility indices** for each mode using service-area tools. Logit models (Multinomial Logit, Nested Logit) use these accessibilities as input variables. GIS also enables **equity analysis** by mapping accessibility disparities across income groups.

**Step 4 — Traffic Assignment:**
Assignment loads the mode-wise O-D matrix onto the network and finds equilibrium link flows. Algorithms include **All-or-Nothing (AON)**, **User Equilibrium (UE)**, and **System Optimal (SO)**. GIS visualizes the output as a **color-coded V/C (Volume-to-Capacity) map**, where red links are congested, and green links have spare capacity. Planners use this map to propose new links, widen roads, or shift demand to transit.

**Integrated Diagram:**

The complete GIS-coupled 4-step model can be summarized as: **TAZ Layer + Network Layer → Trip Generation → O-D Matrix → Trip Distribution (using GIS skim) → Mode Split → Traffic Assignment → V/C Map Output.**

**Conclusion:**
GIS is no longer an optional tool in transport planning — it is a foundational technology. By integrating spatial and non-spatial data, automating complex calculations, and producing intuitive visualizations, GIS has made the 4-step process faster, more accurate, and more transparent for public engagement.

---

### **Question 1(B)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - June 2022]
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the layer-cake concept of GIS. How is it useful in transport planning?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A household survey in three zones of a city yielded the following data. Compute the Hansen-type accessibility index from Zone A using an exponential impedance function with $\beta = 0.08$. Interpret the result.

| Zone | Opportunities (Jobs) | Travel Time from Zone A (minutes) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1200 | 0 |
| B | 800 | 12 |
| C | 400 | 25 |

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **layer-cake concept** (also called the **thematic layer model**) is a fundamental organizing principle of GIS. It represents spatial data as a series of transparent overlays, each containing a different theme or category of information. All layers share the same geographic extent and coordinate system, allowing them to be stacked, queried, and analyzed together.

A typical layer-cake for transport planning has the following layers, stacked from bottom (base) to top (specialized):

- **Layer 1 — Base Map:** Road centerlines, rivers, administrative boundaries, and topography.
- **Layer 2 — Land Use:** Residential, commercial, industrial, recreational, and mixed-use polygons.
- **Layer 3 — Demographic / Socio-economic:** Population density, household income, vehicle ownership (choropleth maps).
- **Layer 4 — Transit Network:** Bus routes, metro corridors, ferry lines, station locations.
- **Layer 5 — Traffic Data:** Volume counts, speeds, V/C ratios (often color-coded).
- **Layer 6 — Safety Data:** Accident locations, black spots, fatality clusters.
- **Layer 7 — Proposals:** New road alignments, proposed metro corridors, BRT routes.

**Use in Transport Planning:**

1. **Trip Generation:** Overlay land use + demographic layers → identify trip-producing zones.
2. **Transit Planning:** Overlay transit + population → compute accessibility to bus stops.
3. **Safety Analysis:** Overlay accident + traffic + land use → detect causal patterns.
4. **Project Impact:** Overlay proposed road + land use → identify displaced properties.
5. **Public Communication:** Layers can be turned on/off to make focused maps for citizens.

**Diagram (Layer Cake):**

The layer-cake is drawn as a stack of horizontal slabs, each labeled with its theme, with the base map at the bottom and the most specialized (e.g., proposed BRT route) at the top. Above all layers, the "composite output" is the final planning map used for decision-making.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Define the impedance function.**

We are given an exponential impedance with $\beta = 0.08$:

$$
f(C_{ij}) = e^{-\beta \cdot C_{ij}} = e^{-0.08 \cdot C_{ij}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the impedance values for each O-D pair.**

- For Zone A to Zone A: $C_{AA} = 0$ min

$$
f(0) = e^{-0.08 \times 0} = e^{0} = 1.000
$$

- For Zone A to Zone B: $C_{AB} = 12$ min

$$
f(12) = e^{-0.08 \times 12} = e^{-0.96} \approx 0.383
$$

- For Zone A to Zone C: $C_{AC} = 25$ min

$$
f(25) = e^{-0.08 \times 25} = e^{-2.0} \approx 0.135
$$

**Step 3: Compute weighted opportunities for each destination.**

$$
O_A \cdot f(C_{AA}) = 1200 \times 1.000 = 1200.00
$$

$$
O_B \cdot f(C_{AB}) = 800 \times 0.383 = 306.40
$$

$$
O_C \cdot f(C_{AC}) = 400 \times 0.135 = 54.00
$$

**Step 4: Sum to get Hansen's accessibility index for Zone A.**

$$
A_A = 1200.00 + 306.40 + 54.00 = 1560.40
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

From Zone A, the resident has access to an **effective opportunity count of 1560.40 jobs**, weighted by exponential travel-time friction. Note that:
- Zone A's own 1200 jobs contribute fully ($f = 1$).
- Zone B's 800 jobs contribute only 38.3% (i.e., 306 effective jobs) due to 12-minute travel time.
- Zone C's 400 jobs contribute only 13.5% (54 effective jobs) due to 25-minute travel time.

This indicates that **Zone A has high self-containment** (most jobs are local), and accessibility drops sharply with distance. A planner would use this index to compare with other zones and identify locations needing better transport connectivity to jobs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in GIS-and-Transport Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Euclidean and Network distance** — Always specify which one you are using. The Pythagorean formula is **Euclidean**; the sum of segment lengths along a path is **Network**.
> 2. **Forgetting the impedance function form** — Hansen's accessibility **requires** a clearly stated $f(C_{ij})$. Never write $A_i = \sum O_j$ alone; that has no impedance and is meaningless.
> 3. **Mixing coordinate systems** — State "WGS 84" or "local grid" explicitly when listing layers. Examiners deduct marks for vague "GIS layers" without CRS.
> 4. **Missing the 4-step integration logic** — When asked about GIS in transport, the answer must explicitly reference Trip Generation, Distribution, Mode Split, and Assignment. A generic GIS answer loses 4–5 marks.
> 5. **Spelling and notation errors** — "Geographic" not "Geographical"; "Thiessen" not "Thiesen"; $\beta$ (beta) not "B" for the impedance coefficient.
> 6. **Diagram labels inside Mermaid** — When drawing flowcharts in your answer book, use **plain text labels** without markdown bold/italics, as advised in the KTU answer script guidelines.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Must-Know" Items for KTU Module 4 GIS Questions**

- **Definition of GIS:** A computer system for capture, storage, analysis, and presentation of spatial data.
- **Five Components:** Hardware, Software, Data, People, Procedures.
- **Two Data Types:** Spatial (geometry) and Non-spatial (attribute); add **Temporal** for 4D.
- **Layer-Cake Concept:** Stack of thematic layers; turning layers on/off yields different views.
- **Six Core Spatial Operations:** Buffer, Overlay, Network Analysis, Interpolation, Thiessen Polygon, Hotspot.
- **Buffer Distances (MoHUA):** Bus = 400 m, Metro = 800 m, Highway noise = 75 m.
- **Coordinate Systems:** WGS 84 (global), UTM (regional), State Plane/Local Grid (city).
- **Euclidean Distance:** $d = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$.
- **Network Distance:** Sum of segment lengths along the chosen path.
- **Circuitous Factor:** $CF = D_{net} / d_E$; ideal = 1.0, acceptable urban = 1.2–1.5.
- **Hansen's Accessibility:** $A_i = \sum_j O_j \cdot f(C_{ij})$; requires an impedance function.
- **Common Impedance Forms:** Exponential $e^{-\beta C}$, Power $C^{-\beta}$, BPR-based $1/t$.
- **BPR Function:** $t = t_0 \left[1 + 0.15 (v/c)^4 \right]$.
- **GIS-4-Step Integration:** TAZ + Network → Trip Gen → Trip Dist → Mode Split → Assignment → V/C Map.
- **GIS Software:** ArcGIS, QGIS, MapInfo, TransCAD, EMME, CUBE.
- **Spatial Databases:** PostGIS, Oracle Spatial, SQL Server Spatial, File Geodatabase.
- **Network Algorithms:** Dijkstra's shortest path, A*, Service Area, VRP, Cost Matrix.
- **Interpolation Methods:** IDW, Kriging, Spline.
- **Cluster Statistics:** Moran's I, Getis-Ord Gi*.
- **Key Use Cases in Kerala Context:** Kochi Metro Phase 2 alignment, Kozhikode BRT feasibility, Smart City Kochi ITS, Coastal road flood-risk mapping.
- **Avoid:** Mixing CRS, generic "GIS helps" answers, forgetting impedance in Hansen, mixing Euclidean/Network distance.
